Source: Web Fetch
---
Prevent your app from getting drowned in WebSocket messages or flooding a WebSocket server with messages by applying backpressure.

## Background

The [WebSocket API](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSockets_API)
provides a JavaScript interface to the [WebSocket protocol](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455),
which makes it possible to open a two-way interactive communication session
between the user's browser and a server.
With this API, you can send messages to a server and receive event-driven responses
without polling the server for a reply.

### The Streams API

The [Streams API](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Streams_API)
allows JavaScript to programmatically access streams of data chunks received over the network
and process them as desired.
An important concept in the context of streams is
[backpressure](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Streams_API/Concepts#Backpressure).
This is the process by which a single stream or a pipe chain
regulates the speed of reading or writing.
When the stream itself or a stream later in the pipe chain is still busy
and isn't yet ready to accept more chunks,
it sends a signal backwards through the chain to slow delivery as appropriate.

### The Problem with the current WebSocket API

#### Applying backpressure to received messages is impossible

With the current WebSocket API, reacting to a message happens in
[WebSocket.onmessage](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/onmessage),
an EventHandler called when a message is received from the server.

Let's assume you had an application that needs to perform heavy data crunching operations
whenever a new message is received.
You would probably set up the flow similar to the code below,
and since you await the result of the process() call, you should be good, right?

// A heavy data crunching operation.
const process = async (data) => {
 return new Promise((resolve) => {
 window.setTimeout(() => {
 console.log('WebSocket message processed:', data);
 return resolve('done');
 }, 1000);
 });
};

webSocket.onmessage = async (event) => {
 const data = event.data;
 // Await the result of the processing step in the message handler.
 await process(data);
};

Wrong! The problem with the current WebSocket API is that there is no way to apply backpressure.
When messages arrive faster than the process() method can handle them,
the render process will either fill up memory by buffering those messages,
become unresponsive due to 100% CPU usage, or both.

#### Applying backpressure to sent messages is non-ergonomic

Applying backpressure to sent messages is possible, but involves polling the
[WebSocket.bufferedAmount](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/bufferedAmount)
property, which is inefficient and non-ergonomic.
This read-only property returns the number of bytes of data that have been queued
using calls to
[WebSocket.send()](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/send),
but not yet transmitted to the network.
This value resets to zero once all queued data has been sent,
but if you keep calling WebSocket.send(),
it will continue to climb.

## What is the WebSocketStream API?

The WebSocketStream API deals with the problem of non-existent or non-ergonomic backpressure
by integrating streams with the WebSocket API.
This means backpressure can be applied "for free", without any extra cost.

### Suggested use cases for the WebSocketStream API

Examples of sites that can use this API include:

- High-bandwidth WebSocket applications that need to retain interactivity,
in particular video and screen-sharing.

- Similarly, video capture and other applications that generate a lot of data in the browser
that needs to be uploaded to the server.
With backpressure, the client can stop producing data rather than accumulating data in memory.

## Current status

Step
Status

1. Create explainer
[Complete](https://github.com/ricea/websocketstream-explainer/blob/master/README.md)

2. Create initial draft of specification
[In progress](https://github.com/ricea/websocketstream-explainer/blob/master/README.md)

3. Gather feedback & iterate on design
[In progress](#feedback)

4. Origin trial
[Complete](https://developers.chrome.com/origintrials/#/view_trial/1977080236415647745)

5. Launch
Not started

## How to use the WebSocketStream API

The WebSocketStream API is promise-based, which makes dealing with it feel natural
in a modern JavaScript world.
You start by constructing a new WebSocketStream and passing it the URL of the WebSocket server.
Next, you wait for the connection to be opened,
which results in a
[ReadableStream](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStream/ReadableStream)
and/or a
[WritableStream](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WritableStream/WritableStream).

By calling the
[ReadableStream.getReader()](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStream/getReader)
method, you finally obtain a
[ReadableStreamDefaultReader](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStreamDefaultReader),
which you can then [read()](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/ReadableStreamDefaultReader/read)
data from until the stream is done, that is, until it returns an object of the form
{value: undefined, done: true}.

Accordingly, by calling the
[WritableStream.getWriter()](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WritableStream/getWriter)
method, you finally obtain a
[WritableStreamDefaultWriter](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WritableStreamDefaultWriter),
which you can then [write()](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WritableStreamDefaultWriter/write)
data to.

 const wss = new WebSocketStream(WSS_URL);
 const {readable, writable} = await wss.opened;
 const reader = readable.getReader();
 const writer = writable.getWriter();

 while (true) {
 const {value, done} = await reader.read();
 if (done) {
 break;
 }
 const result = await process(value);
 await writer.write(result);
 }

### Backpressure

What about the promised backpressure feature?
You get it "for free", no extra steps needed.
If process() takes extra time, the next message is only consumed once the pipeline is ready.
Likewise the WritableStreamDefaultWriter.write() step
only proceeds if it is safe to do so.

### Advanced examples

The second argument to WebSocketStream is an option bag to allow for future extension.
The only option is protocols, which behaves the same as the
[second argument to the WebSocket constructor](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/WebSocket#Parameters:%7E:text=respond.-,protocols):

const chatWSS = new WebSocketStream(CHAT_URL, {protocols: ['chat', 'chatv2']});
const {protocol} = await chatWSS.opened;

The selected protocol as well as potential extensions are part of the dictionary
available via the WebSocketStream.opened promise.
All the information about the live connection is provided by this promise,
since it is not relevant if the connection fails.

const {readable, writable, protocol, extensions} = await chatWSS.opened;

### Information about closed WebSocketStream connection

The information that was available from the
[WebSocket.onclose](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/onclose) and
[WebSocket.onerror](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/onerror) events
in the WebSocket API is now available via the WebSocketStream.closed promise.
The promise rejects in the event of an unclean close,
otherwise it resolves to the code and reason sent by the server.

All possible status codes and their meaning is explained in the
[list of CloseEvent status codes](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/CloseEvent#Status_codes).

const {code, reason} = await chatWSS.closed;

### Closing a WebSocketStream connection

A WebSocketStream can be closed with an
[AbortController](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/AbortController).
Therefore, pass an [AbortSignal](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/AbortSignal)
to the WebSocketStream constructor. AbortController.abort() only works before the handshake, not after.

const controller = new AbortController();
const wss = new WebSocketStream(URL, {signal: controller.signal});
setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 1000);

As an alternative, you can also use the WebSocketStream.close() method,
but its main purpose is to permit specifying the
[code](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/CloseEvent#Status_codes)
and reason which is sent to the server.

wss.close({closeCode: 4000, reason: 'Game over'});

### Progressive enhancement and interoperability

Chrome is currently the only browser to implement the WebSocketStream API.
For interoperability with the classic WebSocket API,
applying backpressure to received messages is not possible.
Applying backpressure to sent messages is possible, but involves polling the
[WebSocket.bufferedAmount](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/WebSocket/bufferedAmount)
property, which is inefficient and non-ergonomic.

#### Feature detection

To check if the WebSocketStream API is supported, use:

if ('WebSocketStream' in window) {
 // `WebSocketStream` is supported!
}

## Demo

On supporting browsers, you can see the [WebSocketStream API in action](https://us-central1-web-devrel-apps.cloudfunctions.net/websocketstream/), or see the embedded iframe.

## Feedback

The Chrome team wants to hear about your experiences with the WebSocketStream API.

### Tell us about the API design

Is there something about the API that doesn't work like you expected?
Or are there missing methods or properties that you need to implement your idea?
Have a question or comment on the security model?
File a spec issue on the corresponding [GitHub repo](https://github.com/ricea/websocketstream-explainer/issues),
or add your thoughts to an existing issue.

### Report a problem with the implementation

Did you find a bug with Chrome's implementation?
Or is the implementation different from the spec?
File a bug at [new.crbug.com](https://new.crbug.com).
Be sure to include as much detail as you can, simple instructions for reproducing,
and enter Blink>Network>WebSockets in the Components box.

### Show support for the API

Are you planning to use the WebSocketStream API?
Your public support helps the Chrome team to prioritize features
and shows other browser vendors how critical it is to support them.

Send a tweet to [@ChromiumDev](https://twitter.com/ChromiumDev) using the hashtag
[#WebSocketStream](https://twitter.com/search?q=%23WebSocketStream&src=typed_query&f=live)
and let us know where and how you're using it.

## Helpful links

- [Public explainer](https://github.com/ricea/websocketstream-explainer/blob/master/README.md)

- [WebSocketStream API Demo](https://us-central1-web-devrel-apps.cloudfunctions.net/websocketstream/) | [WebSocketStream API Demo source](https://github.com/GoogleChrome/samples/tree/gh-pages/websocketstream-demo)

## Acknowledgements

The WebSocketStream API was implemented by [Adam Rice](https://github.com/ricea) and
[Yutaka Hirano](https://github.com/yutakahirano).

/wiki/Wikipedia:Protection_policy#semi

 From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa.jpgThe 828-metre-tall (2,717 ft) [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) has been the tallest building since 2009.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Burj Khalifa has been classified as [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers).[[2]](#cite_note-CTBUH-2)
/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_2022.pngA diagram showing the tallest buildings as of 2024
This is a list of the tallest [buildings](/wiki/Building). Tall buildings, such as [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper), are intended here as enclosed structures with continuously occupiable floors and a height of at least 350 metres (1,150 ft). Such definition excludes [non-building structures](/wiki/Non-building_structure), such as [towers](/wiki/Tower).

## History

Historically, the world's tallest man-made structure was the [Great Pyramid of Giza](/wiki/Great_Pyramid_of_Giza) in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt), which held the position for over 3,800 years[[3]](#cite_note-3) until the construction of [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral) in 1311. The [Strasbourg Cathedral](/wiki/Strasbourg_Cathedral) in [France](/wiki/France), completed in 1439, was the world's tallest building until 1874.

The first building considered to be a skyscraper was the 138 ft (42.1 m) [Home Insurance Building](/wiki/Home_Insurance_Building), built in Chicago in 1885. The [United States](/wiki/United_States) would remain the location of the world's tallest building throughout the 20th century until 1998, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) were completed. Since then, two other buildings have gained the title: [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) in 2004 and [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in 2009.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Since the beginning of the 21st century, the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East), [China](/wiki/China), and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia) have experienced booms in skyscraper construction.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Ranking criteria and alternatives

The international non-profit organization [Council on Vertical Urbanism](/wiki/Council_on_Vertical_Urbanism) (CVU) was formed in 1969 and announces the title of "The World's Tallest Building" and sets the standards by which buildings are measured. It maintains a list of the 100 tallest completed buildings in the world.[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) The organization currently ranks [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in Dubai as the tallest at 828 m (2,717 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) However, the CVU only recognizes buildings that are complete, and some buildings included within the lists in this article are not considered finished by the CVU.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

In 1996, as a response to the dispute as to whether the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) or the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) was taller,[[7]](#cite_note-PetronasSears-7) the council listed and ranked buildings in four categories:[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

- height to structural or architectural top;

- height to highest occupied floor;

- height to top of roof (removed as category in November 2009);[[9]](#cite_note-9) and

- height to top of any part of the building.

All categories measure the building from the level of the lowest significant open-air pedestrian entrance.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

[Spires](/wiki/Spire) are considered integral parts of the architectural design of buildings, changes to which would substantially change the appearance and design of the building, whereas antennas may be added or removed without such consequences. The Petronas Towers, with their spires, are thus ranked higher than the Willis Tower (formerly the Sears Tower) with its antennas, despite the Petronas Towers' lower roofs and lower highest point.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Until 1996, the world's tallest building was defined by the height to the top of the tallest architectural element, including spires but not antennae.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In 1930, this definitional argument led to a rivalry between the [Bank of Manhattan Building](/wiki/40_Wall_Street) and the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building). The Bank of Manhattan Building (i.e. 40 Wall Street) employed only a short spire, was 282.5 m (927 ft) tall, and had a much higher top occupied floor (the second category in the 1996 criteria for tallest building). In contrast, the Chrysler Building employed a very large 38.1 m (125 ft) spire secretly assembled inside the building to claim the title of world's tallest building with a total height of 318.9 m (1,046 ft), although it had a lower top occupied floor and a shorter height when both buildings' spires were excluded.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Upset by Chrysler's victory, [Shreve & Lamb](/wiki/Shreve,_Lamb_%26_Harmon), the consulting architects of the Bank of Manhattan Building, wrote a newspaper article claiming that their building was actually the tallest, since it contained the world's highest usable floor, at 255 m (837 ft). They pointed out that the observation deck in the Bank of Manhattan Building was nearly 30 m (98 ft) above the top floor in the Chrysler Building, whose surpassing spire was strictly ornamental and inaccessible.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Burj Khalifa currently tops the list regardless of which criterion is applied, though at a much lower margin when measured to highest occupied floor.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Tallest buildings in the world

As of 2026, this list includes all 97 buildings (completed and architecturally [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out)) that reach a height of 350 m (1,150 ft) or more, as assessed by their highest architectural feature. The building is considered as architecturally topped out when it is under construction, structurally topped out, fully clad, and the highest finished architectural elements are in place.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

Of these buildings, almost half are in China. Six of the last seven buildings to have held the record as "[tallest building](/wiki/Skyscraper#History_of_the_tallest_skyscrapers)" are still found in the list, with the exception being [the North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the original [New York](/wiki/New_York_City) [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) at 417 m (1,368 ft) after its destruction in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) of 2001. Had the World Trade Center Twin Towers never been destroyed and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) never built, they would have ranked 39 and 40 on the list today.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Clear

Denotes building that is or was once the tallest in the world

## Alternative measurements

Parts of this article (those related to the alternative measurement lists) need to be updated. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (January 2023)

### Height to pinnacle (highest point)

/wiki/File:Tallest_Buildings_in_the_World_by_pinnacle_height.pngTallest buildings by pinnacle height, including all masts, poles, antennae, etc. in 2014
This measurement disregards distinctions between architectural and non-architectural extensions, and simply measures to the highest point, irrespective of material or function of the highest element.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

This measurement is useful for [air traffic obstacle](/wiki/Air_traffic_obstacle) determinations, and is also a wholly objective measure. However, this measurement includes extensions that are easily added, removed, and modified from a building and are independent of the overall structure.

This measurement only recently came into use, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) passed the Sears Tower (now named [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower)) in height. The former was considered taller because its spires were considered architectural, while the latter's antennae were not. This led to the split of definitions, with the Sears Tower claiming the lead in this and the height-to-roof (now highest occupied floor) categories, and with the Petronas claiming the lead in the architectural height category.

If the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers were still standing, the [North](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) (1,728 feet (526.7 m), including the antenna) and [South](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) Towers (1,377 feet (419.7 m)) would fall between numbers 11 and 37 on the current list (as it can be assumed the rebuilt One World Trade Center would have never been built).

### Height to roof

This list of tallest buildings by height to roof ranks completed [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper) by height to roof which reach a height of 300 metres (984 ft) or more. Only buildings with continuously occupiable floors are included, thus [non-building structures](/wiki/Nonbuilding_structure), including towers, are not included. Some assessments of the tallest building use 'height to roof' to determine tallest building, as 'architectural feature' is regarded as a subjective and an imprecise comparative measure. However, in November 2009, the [CTBUH](/wiki/CTBUH) stopped using the roof height as the metric for tall buildings because modern tall buildings rarely have a part of the building that can categorically be deemed the roof.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

/wiki/Wikipedia:Protection_policy#semi

 From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa.jpgThe 828-metre-tall (2,717 ft) [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) has been the tallest building since 2009.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Burj Khalifa has been classified as [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers).[[2]](#cite_note-CTBUH-2)
/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_2022.pngA diagram showing the tallest buildings as of 2024
This is a list of the tallest [buildings](/wiki/Building). Tall buildings, such as [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper), are intended here as enclosed structures with continuously occupiable floors and a height of at least 350 metres (1,150 ft). Such definition excludes [non-building structures](/wiki/Non-building_structure), such as [towers](/wiki/Tower).

## History

Historically, the world's tallest man-made structure was the [Great Pyramid of Giza](/wiki/Great_Pyramid_of_Giza) in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt), which held the position for over 3,800 years[[3]](#cite_note-3) until the construction of [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral) in 1311. The [Strasbourg Cathedral](/wiki/Strasbourg_Cathedral) in [France](/wiki/France), completed in 1439, was the world's tallest building until 1874.

The first building considered to be a skyscraper was the 138 ft (42.1 m) [Home Insurance Building](/wiki/Home_Insurance_Building), built in Chicago in 1885. The [United States](/wiki/United_States) would remain the location of the world's tallest building throughout the 20th century until 1998, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) were completed. Since then, two other buildings have gained the title: [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) in 2004 and [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in 2009.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Since the beginning of the 21st century, the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East), [China](/wiki/China), and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia) have experienced booms in skyscraper construction.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Ranking criteria and alternatives

The international non-profit organization [Council on Vertical Urbanism](/wiki/Council_on_Vertical_Urbanism) (CVU) was formed in 1969 and announces the title of "The World's Tallest Building" and sets the standards by which buildings are measured. It maintains a list of the 100 tallest completed buildings in the world.[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) The organization currently ranks [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in Dubai as the tallest at 828 m (2,717 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) However, the CVU only recognizes buildings that are complete, and some buildings included within the lists in this article are not considered finished by the CVU.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

In 1996, as a response to the dispute as to whether the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) or the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) was taller,[[7]](#cite_note-PetronasSears-7) the council listed and ranked buildings in four categories:[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

- height to structural or architectural top;

- height to highest occupied floor;

- height to top of roof (removed as category in November 2009);[[9]](#cite_note-9) and

- height to top of any part of the building.

All categories measure the building from the level of the lowest significant open-air pedestrian entrance.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

[Spires](/wiki/Spire) are considered integral parts of the architectural design of buildings, changes to which would substantially change the appearance and design of the building, whereas antennas may be added or removed without such consequences. The Petronas Towers, with their spires, are thus ranked higher than the Willis Tower (formerly the Sears Tower) with its antennas, despite the Petronas Towers' lower roofs and lower highest point.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Until 1996, the world's tallest building was defined by the height to the top of the tallest architectural element, including spires but not antennae.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In 1930, this definitional argument led to a rivalry between the [Bank of Manhattan Building](/wiki/40_Wall_Street) and the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building). The Bank of Manhattan Building (i.e. 40 Wall Street) employed only a short spire, was 282.5 m (927 ft) tall, and had a much higher top occupied floor (the second category in the 1996 criteria for tallest building). In contrast, the Chrysler Building employed a very large 38.1 m (125 ft) spire secretly assembled inside the building to claim the title of world's tallest building with a total height of 318.9 m (1,046 ft), although it had a lower top occupied floor and a shorter height when both buildings' spires were excluded.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Upset by Chrysler's victory, [Shreve & Lamb](/wiki/Shreve,_Lamb_%26_Harmon), the consulting architects of the Bank of Manhattan Building, wrote a newspaper article claiming that their building was actually the tallest, since it contained the world's highest usable floor, at 255 m (837 ft). They pointed out that the observation deck in the Bank of Manhattan Building was nearly 30 m (98 ft) above the top floor in the Chrysler Building, whose surpassing spire was strictly ornamental and inaccessible.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Burj Khalifa currently tops the list regardless of which criterion is applied, though at a much lower margin when measured to highest occupied floor.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Tallest buildings in the world

As of 2026, this list includes all 97 buildings (completed and architecturally [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out)) that reach a height of 350 m (1,150 ft) or more, as assessed by their highest architectural feature. The building is considered as architecturally topped out when it is under construction, structurally topped out, fully clad, and the highest finished architectural elements are in place.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

Of these buildings, almost half are in China. Six of the last seven buildings to have held the record as "[tallest building](/wiki/Skyscraper#History_of_the_tallest_skyscrapers)" are still found in the list, with the exception being [the North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the original [New York](/wiki/New_York_City) [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) at 417 m (1,368 ft) after its destruction in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) of 2001. Had the World Trade Center Twin Towers never been destroyed and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) never built, they would have ranked 39 and 40 on the list today.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Clear

Denotes building that is or was once the tallest in the world

## Alternative measurements

Parts of this article (those related to the alternative measurement lists) need to be updated. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (January 2023)

### Height to pinnacle (highest point)

/wiki/File:Tallest_Buildings_in_the_World_by_pinnacle_height.pngTallest buildings by pinnacle height, including all masts, poles, antennae, etc. in 2014
This measurement disregards distinctions between architectural and non-architectural extensions, and simply measures to the highest point, irrespective of material or function of the highest element.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

This measurement is useful for [air traffic obstacle](/wiki/Air_traffic_obstacle) determinations, and is also a wholly objective measure. However, this measurement includes extensions that are easily added, removed, and modified from a building and are independent of the overall structure.

This measurement only recently came into use, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) passed the Sears Tower (now named [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower)) in height. The former was considered taller because its spires were considered architectural, while the latter's antennae were not. This led to the split of definitions, with the Sears Tower claiming the lead in this and the height-to-roof (now highest occupied floor) categories, and with the Petronas claiming the lead in the architectural height category.

If the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers were still standing, the [North](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) (1,728 feet (526.7 m), including the antenna) and [South](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) Towers (1,377 feet (419.7 m)) would fall between numbers 11 and 37 on the current list (as it can be assumed the rebuilt One World Trade Center would have never been built).

### Height to roof

This list of tallest buildings by height to roof ranks completed [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper) by height to roof which reach a height of 300 metres (984 ft) or more. Only buildings with continuously occupiable floors are included, thus [non-building structures](/wiki/Nonbuilding_structure), including towers, are not included. Some assessments of the tallest building use 'height to roof' to determine tallest building, as 'architectural feature' is regarded as a subjective and an imprecise comparative measure. However, in November 2009, the [CTBUH](/wiki/CTBUH) stopped using the roof height as the metric for tall buildings because modern tall buildings rarely have a part of the building that can categorically be deemed the roof.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

Burj Khalifa

References
[[1]](#cite_note-1)

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa_(worlds_tallest_building)_and_the_Dubai_skyline_(25781049892).jpgPhotographic [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette) of the [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) [skyline](/wiki/Skyline); the Burj Khalifa is visible at the center
The Burj Khalifa[[a]](#cite_note-2) (previously known as Burj Dubai prior to inauguration) is a [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai), United Arab Emirates. Designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), it is the [world's tallest structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_the_world), with a total height of 829.8 m (2,722 ft, or just over half a mile) and a roof height (excluding the antenna, but including a 242.6 m spire)[[2]](#cite_note-Vanity-3) of 828 m (2,717 ft). It has also been the [tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_and_structures) in the world since its [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) in 2009, surpassing [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), which had held the record for a half-decade.[[3]](#cite_note-CTBUHdb-4)[[4]](#cite_note-DubaiOneInauguration-5)

Construction of the Burj Khalifa began in 2004; the exterior was completed five years later. The primary structure is [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete). Some of the [structural steel](/wiki/Structural_steel) for the building was salvaged from the demolished [Palace of the Republic](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Republic,_Berlin) in East Berlin.[[5]](#cite_note-dw-6) The building was opened in 2010 as part of a new development called [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai). It was designed to be the centerpiece of large-scale, mixed-use development.

The building is named after the former president of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Sheikh [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan).[[6]](#cite_note-USAtoday-7) The [United Arab Emirates government](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_government) provided Dubai with financial support as the developer, [Emaar Properties](/wiki/Emaar_Properties), experienced financial problems during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession). Then-president of the United Arab Emirates, Khalifa bin Zayed, organized federal financial support. For his support, [Mohammad bin Rashid](/wiki/Mohammad_bin_Rashid), [Ruler of Dubai](/wiki/Ruler_of_Dubai), changed the name from "Burj Dubai" to "Burj Khalifa" during inauguration.

The design is derived from the [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture) of the region, such as in the [Great Mosque of Samarra](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Samarra). The Y-shaped tripartite floor geometry is designed to optimise residential and hotel space. A [buttressed central core and wings](/wiki/Buttressed_core) are used to support the height of the building. The Burj Khalifa's central core houses all vertical transportation except egress stairs within each of the wings.[[7]](#cite_note-academic.csuohio.edu-8) The structure also features a [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction)) system which is designed to withstand Dubai's hot summer temperatures.[[8]](#cite_note-9) It contains a total of 57 elevators and 8 escalators.

## Development

Construction began on 12 January 2004, with the exterior of the structure completed on 1 October 2009. The building officially opened on 4 January 2010[[9]](#cite_note-opening-10)[[10]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-11) and is part of the 2 km2 (490 acres) [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai) development at the 'First Interchange' along [Sheikh Zayed Road](/wiki/Sheikh_Zayed_Road), near Dubai's main business district.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The tower's architecture and engineering were performed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) of Chicago, with [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)) as chief architect, and [Bill Baker](/wiki/William_F._Baker_(engineer)) as a chief structural engineer.[[11]](#cite_note-Record_High-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The firm had designed the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) in Chicago, a previous record holder for the world's tallest building.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

[Hyder Consulting](/wiki/Hyder_Consulting) was supervising engineer and NORR Group Consultants supervised the architecture. The primary contractor was [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T) of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea), together with the Belgian group [BESIX](/wiki/Besix) and the local company [Arabtec](/wiki/Arabtec_Holding_PJSC).

Numerous complaints concerned migrant workers from [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia), the primary building labour force, who were paid low wages and sometimes had their passports confiscated.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Conception

Burj Khalifa was designed to be the centerpiece of a large-scale, mixed-use development to include 30,000 homes,[[16]](#cite_note-17) nine hotels (including [The Address Downtown Dubai](/wiki/The_Address_Downtown_Dubai)), 3 hectares (7.4 acres) of parkland, at least 19 residential skyscrapers, the [Dubai Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Mall), and the 12-hectare (30-acre) artificial Burj Khalifa Lake. The decision to build Burj Khalifa was reportedly based on the government's decision to diversify from an oil-based economy to one that is service and tourism based. According to officials, projects like Burj Khalifa needed to be built to garner more international recognition and hence investment. "He (Sheikh [Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum)) wanted to put Dubai on the map with something really sensational," said Jacqui Josephson, a tourism and VIP delegations executive at [Nakheel Properties](/wiki/Nakheel_Properties).[[17]](#cite_note-18)

The tower was known as Burj Dubai ("Dubai Tower") until its official opening in January 2010.[[18]](#cite_note-name_change-19) It was renamed in honour of the ruler of [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Emirate_of_Abu_Dhabi), [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan); [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi) and the federal government of UAE lent Dubai tens of billions of US dollars so that Dubai could pay its debts – Dubai borrowed at least $80 billion for construction projects.[[18]](#cite_note-name_change-19) In the 2000s, Dubai started diversifying its economy but it suffered from the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis) and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession), leaving large-scale projects already in construction abandoned.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Records

The Burj Khalifa set several world records, including:

- Tallest existing structure: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [KVLY-TV mast](/wiki/KVLY-TV_mast) – 628.8 m or 2,063 ft)

- Tallest structure ever built: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [Warsaw radio mast](/wiki/Warsaw_radio_mast) – 646.38 m or 2,121 ft)

- Tallest freestanding structure: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) – 553.3 m or 1,815 ft)

- Tallest skyscraper (to top of [spire](/wiki/Spire)): 828 m (2,717 ft) (previously [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) – 509.2 m or 1,671 ft)[[20]](#cite_note-dupre-21)

- Tallest skyscraper to top of antenna: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously the [Willis (formerly Sears) Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) – 527 m or 1,729 ft)

- Building with most floors: 163 (previously [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) – 110)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

- World's highest [elevator](/wiki/Elevator) installation (situated inside a rod at the very top of the building)[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23)

- World's longest travel distance elevators: 504 m (1,654 ft)[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23)[[23]](#cite_note-Otis-24)

- Highest vertical [concrete pumping](/wiki/Concrete_pump) (for a building): 606 m (1,988 ft)[[24]](#cite_note-Putzmeister-25)

- [World's tallest structure](/wiki/World%27s_tallest_structures) that includes residential space[[25]](#cite_note-Emporis-26)

- World's highest installation of an aluminium and glass façade: 512 m (1,680 ft)[[26]](#cite_note-timeline-27)

- World's highest restaurant ([At.mosphere](/wiki/At.mosphere)): 122nd floor at 442 m (1,450 ft) (previously 360, at a height of 350 m (1,148 ft) in CN Tower)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

- World's highest New Year display of [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks).[[28]](#cite_note-gulfnews.com-29)

- World's largest light and sound show staged on a single building.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Architecture and design

/wiki/File:Comparisonfinal001fx7.pngA cross-section of comparisons of various towers, from ground level from top to bottom: Burj Khalifa, [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower), and [the original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001))
The tower was designed by Skidmore, Owings, and Merrill (SOM), which also designed the [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) (formerly the Sears Tower) in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago) and the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in New York City. Burj Khalifa uses the bundled [tube](/wiki/Tube_(structure)) design of the Willis Tower, invented by [Fazlur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Fazlur_Rahman_Khan).[[32]](#cite_note-constructionweekonline.com-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) Due to its tubular system, proportionally only half the amount of steel was used in the construction, compared to the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building).[[32]](#cite_note-constructionweekonline.com-33)[[34]](#cite_note-35) Khan's contributions to the design of tall buildings have had a profound impact on architecture and engineering. It would be difficult to find any worldwide practices in the design of tall buildings that have not been directly or indirectly influenced by his work.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The design is reminiscent of [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright)'s vision for [The Illinois](/wiki/The_Illinois), a mile-high skyscraper designed for Chicago, as well as Chicago's [Lake Point Tower](/wiki/Lake_Point_Tower). When [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)) was conceiving the project at SOM, he looked out his office window toward [Lake Point Tower](/wiki/Lake_Point_Tower)'s curved three-wing layout and thought, "There's the prototype".[[36]](#cite_note-37) According to Strabala, Burj Khalifa was designed based on the 73 floor [Tower Palace Three](/wiki/Tower_Palace_Three_Tower), an all-residential building in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul). In its early planning, Burj Khalifa was intended to be entirely residential.[[37]](#cite_note-Height_Revealed-38)

After the original design by Skidmore, Owings, and Merrill, Emaar Properties chose Hyder Consulting to be the supervising engineer and NORR Group Consultants International Ltd to supervise the architecture of the project.[[38]](#cite_note-Hyder1-39) Hyder was selected for their expertise in structural and [MEP](/wiki/Architectural_engineering#Mechanical,_electrical,_and_plumbing_(MEP)) (mechanical, electrical and plumbing) engineering.[[39]](#cite_note-Hyder2-40) Hyder Consulting's role was to supervise construction, certify the architect's design, and be the engineer and architect of record to the UAE authorities.[[38]](#cite_note-Hyder1-39) NORR's role was the supervision of all architectural components including on-site supervision during the construction and design of a 6-storey addition to the office annex building for architectural documentation. NORR was also responsible for the architectural integration drawings for the Armani Hotel included in the Tower. Emaar Properties also engaged [GHD](/wiki/GHD_Group),[[40]](#cite_note-GHD_Projects-41) an international multidisciplinary consulting firm, to act as an independent verification and testing authority for concrete and steelwork.

The design is derived from Islamic architecture.[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23) As the tower rises from the flat desert base, there are 27 [setbacks](/wiki/Setback_(architecture)) in a spiral pattern, decreasing the cross-section of the tower as it rises and creating convenient outdoor terraces. These setbacks are arranged and aligned in a way that minimizes vibration wind loading from eddy currents and vortices.[[7]](#cite_note-academic.csuohio.edu-8) At the top, the central core emerges and is sculpted to form a finishing spire. At its tallest

Burj Khalifa

References
[[1]](#cite_note-1)

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa_(worlds_tallest_building)_and_the_Dubai_skyline_(25781049892).jpgPhotographic [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette) of the [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) [skyline](/wiki/Skyline); the Burj Khalifa is visible at the center
The Burj Khalifa[[a]](#cite_note-2) (previously known as Burj Dubai prior to inauguration) is a [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai), United Arab Emirates. Designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), it is the [world's tallest structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_the_world), with a total height of 829.8 m (2,722 ft, or just over half a mile) and a roof height (excluding the antenna, but including a 242.6 m spire)[[2]](#cite_note-Vanity-3) of 828 m (2,717 ft). It has also been the [tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_and_structures) in the world since its [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) in 2009, surpassing [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), which had held the record for a half-decade.[[3]](#cite_note-CTBUHdb-4)[[4]](#cite_note-DubaiOneInauguration-5)

Construction of the Burj Khalifa began in 2004; the exterior was completed five years later. The primary structure is [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete). Some of the [structural steel](/wiki/Structural_steel) for the building was salvaged from the demolished [Palace of the Republic](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Republic,_Berlin) in East Berlin.[[5]](#cite_note-dw-6) The building was opened in 2010 as part of a new development called [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai). It was designed to be the centerpiece of large-scale, mixed-use development.

The building is named after the former president of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Sheikh [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan).[[6]](#cite_note-USAtoday-7) The [United Arab Emirates government](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_government) provided Dubai with financial support as the developer, [Emaar Properties](/wiki/Emaar_Properties), experienced financial problems during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession). Then-president of the United Arab Emirates, Khalifa bin Zayed, organized federal financial support. For his support, [Mohammad bin Rashid](/wiki/Mohammad_bin_Rashid), [Ruler of Dubai](/wiki/Ruler_of_Dubai), changed the name from "Burj Dubai" to "Burj Khalifa" during inauguration.

The design is derived from the [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture) of the region, such as in the [Great Mosque of Samarra](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Samarra). The Y-shaped tripartite floor geometry is designed to optimise residential and hotel space. A [buttressed central core and wings](/wiki/Buttressed_core) are used to support the height of the building. The Burj Khalifa's central core houses all vertical transportation except egress stairs within each of the wings.[[7]](#cite_note-academic.csuohio.edu-8) The structure also features a [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction)) system which is designed to withstand Dubai's hot summer temperatures.[[8]](#cite_note-9) It contains a total of 57 elevators and 8 escalators.

## Development

Construction began on 12 January 2004, with the exterior of the structure completed on 1 October 2009. The building officially opened on 4 January 2010[[9]](#cite_note-opening-10)[[10]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-11) and is part of the 2 km2 (490 acres) [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai) development at the 'First Interchange' along [Sheikh Zayed Road](/wiki/Sheikh_Zayed_Road), near Dubai's main business district.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The tower's architecture and engineering were performed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) of Chicago, with [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)) as chief architect, and [Bill Baker](/wiki/William_F._Baker_(engineer)) as a chief structural engineer.[[11]](#cite_note-Record_High-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The firm had designed the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) in Chicago, a previous record holder for the world's tallest building.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

[Hyder Consulting](/wiki/Hyder_Consulting) was supervising engineer and NORR Group Consultants supervised the architecture. The primary contractor was [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T) of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea), together with the Belgian group [BESIX](/wiki/Besix) and the local company [Arabtec](/wiki/Arabtec_Holding_PJSC).

Numerous complaints concerned migrant workers from [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia), the primary building labour force, who were paid low wages and sometimes had their passports confiscated.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Conception

Burj Khalifa was designed to be the centerpiece of a large-scale, mixed-use development to include 30,000 homes,[[16]](#cite_note-17) nine hotels (including [The Address Downtown Dubai](/wiki/The_Address_Downtown_Dubai)), 3 hectares (7.4 acres) of parkland, at least 19 residential skyscrapers, the [Dubai Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Mall), and the 12-hectare (30-acre) artificial Burj Khalifa Lake. The decision to build Burj Khalifa was reportedly based on the government's decision to diversify from an oil-based economy to one that is service and tourism based. According to officials, projects like Burj Khalifa needed to be built to garner more international recognition and hence investment. "He (Sheikh [Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum)) wanted to put Dubai on the map with something really sensational," said Jacqui Josephson, a tourism and VIP delegations executive at [Nakheel Properties](/wiki/Nakheel_Properties).[[17]](#cite_note-18)

The tower was known as Burj Dubai ("Dubai Tower") until its official opening in January 2010.[[18]](#cite_note-name_change-19) It was renamed in honour of the ruler of [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Emirate_of_Abu_Dhabi), [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan); [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi) and the federal government of UAE lent Dubai tens of billions of US dollars so that Dubai could pay its debts – Dubai borrowed at least $80 billion for construction projects.[[18]](#cite_note-name_change-19) In the 2000s, Dubai started diversifying its economy but it suffered from the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis) and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession), leaving large-scale projects already in construction abandoned.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Records

The Burj Khalifa set several world records, including:

- Tallest existing structure: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [KVLY-TV mast](/wiki/KVLY-TV_mast) – 628.8 m or 2,063 ft)

- Tallest structure ever built: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [Warsaw radio mast](/wiki/Warsaw_radio_mast) – 646.38 m or 2,121 ft)

- Tallest freestanding structure: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) – 553.3 m or 1,815 ft)

- Tallest skyscraper (to top of [spire](/wiki/Spire)): 828 m (2,717 ft) (previously [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) – 509.2 m or 1,671 ft)[[20]](#cite_note-dupre-21)

- Tallest skyscraper to top of antenna: 829.8 m (2,722 ft) (previously the [Willis (formerly Sears) Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) – 527 m or 1,729 ft)

- Building with most floors: 163 (previously [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) – 110)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

- World's highest [elevator](/wiki/Elevator) installation (situated inside a rod at the very top of the building)[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23)

- World's longest travel distance elevators: 504 m (1,654 ft)[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23)[[23]](#cite_note-Otis-24)

- Highest vertical [concrete pumping](/wiki/Concrete_pump) (for a building): 606 m (1,988 ft)[[24]](#cite_note-Putzmeister-25)

- [World's tallest structure](/wiki/World%27s_tallest_structures) that includes residential space[[25]](#cite_note-Emporis-26)

- World's highest installation of an aluminium and glass façade: 512 m (1,680 ft)[[26]](#cite_note-timeline-27)

- World's highest restaurant ([At.mosphere](/wiki/At.mosphere)): 122nd floor at 442 m (1,450 ft) (previously 360, at a height of 350 m (1,148 ft) in CN Tower)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

- World's highest New Year display of [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks).[[28]](#cite_note-gulfnews.com-29)

- World's largest light and sound show staged on a single building.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Architecture and design

/wiki/File:Comparisonfinal001fx7.pngA cross-section of comparisons of various towers, from ground level from top to bottom: Burj Khalifa, [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower), and [the original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001))
The tower was designed by Skidmore, Owings, and Merrill (SOM), which also designed the [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) (formerly the Sears Tower) in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago) and the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in New York City. Burj Khalifa uses the bundled [tube](/wiki/Tube_(structure)) design of the Willis Tower, invented by [Fazlur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Fazlur_Rahman_Khan).[[32]](#cite_note-constructionweekonline.com-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) Due to its tubular system, proportionally only half the amount of steel was used in the construction, compared to the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building).[[32]](#cite_note-constructionweekonline.com-33)[[34]](#cite_note-35) Khan's contributions to the design of tall buildings have had a profound impact on architecture and engineering. It would be difficult to find any worldwide practices in the design of tall buildings that have not been directly or indirectly influenced by his work.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The design is reminiscent of [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright)'s vision for [The Illinois](/wiki/The_Illinois), a mile-high skyscraper designed for Chicago, as well as Chicago's [Lake Point Tower](/wiki/Lake_Point_Tower). When [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)) was conceiving the project at SOM, he looked out his office window toward [Lake Point Tower](/wiki/Lake_Point_Tower)'s curved three-wing layout and thought, "There's the prototype".[[36]](#cite_note-37) According to Strabala, Burj Khalifa was designed based on the 73 floor [Tower Palace Three](/wiki/Tower_Palace_Three_Tower), an all-residential building in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul). In its early planning, Burj Khalifa was intended to be entirely residential.[[37]](#cite_note-Height_Revealed-38)

After the original design by Skidmore, Owings, and Merrill, Emaar Properties chose Hyder Consulting to be the supervising engineer and NORR Group Consultants International Ltd to supervise the architecture of the project.[[38]](#cite_note-Hyder1-39) Hyder was selected for their expertise in structural and [MEP](/wiki/Architectural_engineering#Mechanical,_electrical,_and_plumbing_(MEP)) (mechanical, electrical and plumbing) engineering.[[39]](#cite_note-Hyder2-40) Hyder Consulting's role was to supervise construction, certify the architect's design, and be the engineer and architect of record to the UAE authorities.[[38]](#cite_note-Hyder1-39) NORR's role was the supervision of all architectural components including on-site supervision during the construction and design of a 6-storey addition to the office annex building for architectural documentation. NORR was also responsible for the architectural integration drawings for the Armani Hotel included in the Tower. Emaar Properties also engaged [GHD](/wiki/GHD_Group),[[40]](#cite_note-GHD_Projects-41) an international multidisciplinary consulting firm, to act as an independent verification and testing authority for concrete and steelwork.

The design is derived from Islamic architecture.[[22]](#cite_note-gn-23) As the tower rises from the flat desert base, there are 27 [setbacks](/wiki/Setback_(architecture)) in a spiral pattern, decreasing the cross-section of the tower as it rises and creating convenient outdoor terraces. Merdeka 118, formerly known as Menara Warisan Merdeka,[[a]](#cite_note-13) KL 118, and PNB 118, is a 118-story [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia. At 678.9 metres (2,227 feet) tall,[[1]](#cite_note-Merdeka_118-1) it is the [second-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and [structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures) in the world, only behind the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) (Dubai, UAE), at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). Construction was controversially entirely funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad), an investment management company [owned by the Malaysian government](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise).[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) The structure was completed in November of 2023, celebrated its grand opening on 10 January 2024, but remains closed and under further construction as of 1 July 2025.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)] The Park Hyatt Kuala Lumpur Hotel, located on level 75, began operation on 7 August 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-parkhyatt-15)

The building's name, Merdeka, which means "independence" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language), is inspired by its proximity to [Stadium Merdeka](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka).[[15]](#cite_note-future-16) The spire of the building was completed in December 2022, which marked its final height of 678.9 m (2,227 ft) above ground and 700.9 m (2,300 ft) above sea level.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-17)

It is the [tallest building in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia)—surpassing the 451.9-metre (1,483 ft) [Petronas Twin Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers)—and in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), surpassing the 462-metre (1,516 ft) [Landmark 81](/wiki/Landmark_81).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18) The building will also be the first in Malaysia to receive a triple platinum rating from worldwide sustainability certifications such as Green Building Index (GBI), including [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18)

When it was completed in 2023,[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) the tower became the tallest building in Malaysia.[[20]](#cite_note-21) It was constructed in three phases and consists of 400,000 square metres (4,300,000 sq ft) of residential, hotel and commercial space.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The building is a mix of office spaces, hotels, and retail outlets and an observatory floor which will be the highest observation deck in Southeast Asia. It has two observation decks, the first inside the building and the second inside the spire, and a retail business center ([118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall)). It will be surrounded by four acres of urban and linear parks.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The non-rentable space consists of elevators, recreational and maintenance facilities, as well as parking spaces for up to 8,500 cars. Sixty out of the 80 storeys of office space will be reserved for [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB), the developer of the project, and its subsidiaries.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Merdeka 118 precinct is a 7.7-hectare (19-acre) land development funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise) of the Malaysian government.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14)

The budget of RM5 billion[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27) has faced criticism from many Malaysians, saying that the money was in essence public money and could have been better used elsewhere, such as for basic infrastructure and a raise in the minimum wage.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) In 2016, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia) [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak), who would eventually be directly involved with the [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad) scandal, claimed that the project was not a waste and it would "bring more benefits" by generating "economic opportunities".[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The building is situated on [Jalan Hang Jebat](/wiki/Jalan_Hang_Jebat), on the location of the former Merdeka Park (subsequently repurposed into an open-air car park). The site lies within the vicinity of landmarks such as [Petaling Street](/wiki/Petaling_Street), sporting venues including [Merdeka Stadium](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka), [Stadium Negara](/wiki/Stadium_Negara) and the Chin Woo Stadium, three schools (the [Victoria Institution](/wiki/Victoria_Institution), the [Methodist Boys' School](/wiki/Methodist_Boys%27_School,_Kuala_Lumpur) and the [Chinese-type Jalan Davidson Primary School](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Kuala_Lumpur)), and the stalled [Plaza Rakyat](/wiki/Plaza_Rakyat) project (across the [Ampang Line](/wiki/Ampang_Line)).[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27)

The Merdeka 118 development also has access to the newly built [Merdeka MRT station](/wiki/Merdeka_MRT_station) on the [Kajang Line](/wiki/Kajang_line) (SBK)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) and is directly linked from three major roads via the Belfield Tunnel, which is a 2-storey underground tunnel passing underneath Kampung Attap and [Jalan Maharajalela](/wiki/Jalan_Maharajalela) to the basement of the precinct.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

/wiki/File:Merdeka_1957_tunku_abdul_rahman.jpgMerdeka 118 was designed to resemble [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s stature as he proclaimed Malaysian independence on 31 August 1957, chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!" seven times.
The building is designed with a mixture of diamond-shaped glass facades to signify the diversity of Malaysians.[[4]](#cite_note-Abdelrazaq2020-4) The design was made to resemble and inspired by [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s outstretched hand gesture while chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!",[[31]](#cite_note-32) when he proclaimed the [independence of Malaya](/wiki/Independence_of_Malaya) on 31 August 1957. The building's cladding was to comprise 18,144 panels, 114,000 square-meter of glass, and 1,600 tonnes of window frame extrusions. It was to contain the [118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall), Grade-A offices, hotels, and residential areas. The structural engineers were [Leslie E. Robertson](/wiki/Leslie_E._Robertson) Associates and [Robert Bird Group](/wiki/Robert_Bird_Group) while the civil and structural engineer of record for this tower is [Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group).[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The building has been equipped and illuminated at night with 8.4 kilometres of LED light strips which would gradually move from one corner to another.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The Neapoli Group, an environmental design and engineering firm, was employed to provide consultancy services towards achieving platinum rating with three Green Building certification bodies: [Green Building Index](/w/index.php?title=Green_Building_Index&action=edit&redlink=1) (GBI), [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design](/wiki/LEED) (LEED), and GreenRE.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

/wiki/File:PNB118_Jalan_Hang_Jebat_entrance.pngConstruction of the tower's base as viewed from Jalan Hang Jebat in September 2021
The piling and foundation work for the project was awarded to Pintaras Geotechnics Sdn Bhd.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The Permodalan Nasional Berhad shortlisted six groups for various construction jobs: [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) and [UEM Group](/wiki/UEM_Group) Bhd; [IJM Corp Bhd](/wiki/IJM_Corporation), Norwest Holdings Sdn Bhd, and Shimizu Corp; [Malaysian Resources Corp Bhd](/wiki/MRCB) and State Construction Engineering Corp; WCT Bhd and Arabtec Construction LLC; TSR Capital Bhd and Daewoo Group; Seacera Group Bhd with Spaz Sdn Bhd, [Sinohydro Corp](/wiki/SinoHydro_Corp), and Shanghai Construction Group. These companies submitted their bids by 28 January 2015.[[39]](#cite_note-40) [KONE](/wiki/KONE), a Finnish group, is supplying around 87 elevators and escalators for the project.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

On 23 November 2015, PNB announced a contract worth RM3.4 billion has been awarded to the joint venture of South Korea's Samsung C&T and UEM Group Berhad.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Furthermore, on 9 November 2017, PNB planned to raise up to RM5 billion fund for its project via a green [sukuk](/wiki/Sukuk), the Merdeka Asean Green SRI Sukuk, with a 15-year tenure. The sukuk covered the development of its 83-storey office space, which forms part of the tower. It is the first adopter of the Asean Green Bond Standards launched by the [Securities Commission Malaysia](/wiki/Securities_Commission_Malaysia) th

Merdeka Residential Tower 1

242.3 m (795 ft)

Merdeka Residential Tower 2

220.4 m (723 ft)

/wiki/File:PNB118_Jalan_Hang_Jebat_entrance.pngConstruction of the tower's base as viewed from Jalan Hang Jebat in September 2021
The piling and foundation work for the project was awarded to Pintaras Geotechnics Sdn Bhd.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The Permodalan Nasional Berhad shortlisted six groups for various construction jobs: [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) and [UEM Group](/wiki/UEM_Group) Bhd; [IJM Corp Bhd](/wiki/IJM_Corporation), Norwest Holdings Sdn Bhd, and Shimizu Corp; [Malaysian Resources Corp Bhd](/wiki/MRCB) and State Construction Engineering Corp; WCT Bhd and Arabtec Construction LLC; TSR Capital Bhd and Daewoo Group; Seacera Group Bhd with Spaz Sdn Bhd, [Sinohydro Corp](/wiki/SinoHydro_Corp), and Shanghai Construction Group. These companies submitted their bids by 28 January 2015.[[39]](#cite_note-40) [KONE](/wiki/KONE), a Finnish group, is supplying around 87 elevators and escalators for the project.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

On 23 November 2015, PNB announced a contract worth RM3.4 billion has been awarded to the joint venture of South Korea's Samsung C&T and UEM Group Berhad.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Furthermore, on 9 November 2017, PNB planned to raise up to RM5 billion fund for its project via a green [sukuk](/wiki/Sukuk), the Merdeka Asean Green SRI Sukuk, with a 15-year tenure. The sukuk covered the development of its 83-storey office space, which forms part of the tower. It is the first adopter of the Asean Green Bond Standards launched by the [Securities Commission Malaysia](/wiki/Securities_Commission_Malaysia) th

Shanghai Tower
上海中心大厦
Shànghǎi Zhōngxīn Dàshà

/wiki/File:Shanghai_Tower_logo.png
/wiki/File:Shanghai_-_Shanghai_Tower_-_0003(cropped).jpgShanghai Tower in 2016

Interactive map of the Shanghai Tower area

Former namesShanghai Center, Shanghai Center Tower
General information
StatusCompleted
TypeMixed-use
Location501 Yincheng Middle Rd, [Lujiazui, Pudong, Shanghai](/wiki/Lujiazui,_Pudong,_Shanghai), China
Construction started1 November 2008
Completed31 August 2014; 12 years ago
Opened1 February 2015
Cost[CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi)15.9 billion
Owner[Shanghai Tower Construction and Development](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Investment_Group)
Height
Architectural632 m (2,073 ft)
Tip632 m (2,073 ft)
Roof587.4 m (1,927 ft)
Top floor583.5 m (1,914 ft) (Level 127)
Observatory562 m (1,844 ft) (Level 121)[[8]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-8)
Technical details
Floor count128 above ground
5 below ground 133 total floors
Floor area380,000 m2 (4,090,300 sq ft) above grade
170 m2 (1,800 sq ft) below grade
Lifts/elevators97 (mall included)
Design and construction
Architects[Marshall Strabala](/wiki/Marshall_Strabala) & [Jun Xia](/wiki/Jun_Xia_(architect)) ([Gensler](/wiki/Gensler))
TJAD
Engineer[Thornton Tomasetti](/wiki/Thornton_Tomasetti)
[Cosentini Associates](/wiki/Cosentini_Associates)
I.DEA Ecological Solutions
Main contractorShanghai Construction Group
Other information
Public transit access[Lujiazui station](/wiki/Lujiazui_station)
Website
[https://en.shanghaitower.com/](https://en.shanghaitower.com/)
References
[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1)[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCentre-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Forbes2013-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)[[7]](#cite_note-LATimesVideo-7)

The Shanghai Tower[[a]](#cite_note-9) is a 128-story, 632-meter-tall (2,073-foot) [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) located in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui), [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai).[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10) It is currently the [tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China), and the world's [third-tallest building by height to architectural top](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). It was the tallest and largest [LEED](/wiki/LEED)-CS Platinum certified building in the world from 2015 to 2024. It was also the second tallest-building in the world, from 2015 to 2021, until the completion of [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in Malaysia. The Shanghai Tower also had the world's fastest [elevators](/wiki/Elevator) at a top speed of 20.5 meters per second (74 kilometers per hour; 46 miles per hour) until 2017,[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) when it was surpassed by the [Guangzhou CTF Finance Center](/wiki/Guangzhou_CTF_Finance_Center), with its top speed of 21 meters per second (76 km/h; 47 mph).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Designed by the international design firm [Gensler](/wiki/Gensler) and owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government,[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1) it is the tallest of the world's first triple-adjacent supertall buildings in [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), the other two being the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) and the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center). Its tiered construction, designed for high energy efficiency, provides nine separate zones divided between office, retail and leisure use.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) The US-based [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) cites it as "one of the most sustainably advanced tall buildings in the world."[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Groundbreaking and construction work on the tower began on 29 November 2008 and [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on 4 August 2013.[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The exterior was completed in summer 2015,[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) and work was considered complete in September 2014. Although the building was originally scheduled to open to the public in November 2014, the actual public-use date was shifted to February 2015. The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) was opened to visitors in July 2016; the period from July through September 2018 was termed a "test run" or "commissioning" period.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18)[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

Since 26 April 2017, the sightseeing decks on the 118th and 119th floors (546 m and 552 m high respectively) have been fully open to the public.[[19]](#cite_note-20) By 2020, the opening of a further deck, dubbed the "Top of Shanghai" on the 121st floor at 562 m (1844 ft), made it the highest observation deck in the world, beating out the [Burj Khalifa's](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) observation deck at 555 m (1823 ft).[[20]](#cite_note-21) The J Hotel Shanghai Tower, opened on the 120th floor in 2021, became the world's highest luxury hotel by height [above ground level](/wiki/Height_above_ground_level).[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Planning and funding
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Tower&action=edit&section=2)]

Planning models for the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui) financial district dating back to 1993 show plans for a close group of three supertall skyscrapers.[[23]](#cite_note-24) The first of these, the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower), was completed in 1999; the adjacent [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) (SWFC) opened in 2008.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Shanghai Tower is owned by Yeti Construction and Development, a consortium of state-owned development companies which includes Shanghai Chengtou Corp., Shanghai Lujiazui Finance & Trade Zone Development Co., and [Shanghai Construction Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Construction_Group).[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1)[[25]](#cite_note-:2-26) Funding for the tower's construction was obtained from [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder), bank loans, and Shanghai's municipal government.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The tower had an estimated construction cost of US$2.4 billion.[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)

In 2008, the site – previously a [driving range](/wiki/Driving_range)[[27]](#cite_note-28) – was prepared for construction.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) A groundbreaking ceremony was held on 29 November 2008, after the tower had passed an [environmental impact study](/wiki/Environmental_impact_study).[[30]](#cite_note-31) The main construction contractor for the project was [Shanghai Construction Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Construction_Group), a member of the consortium that owns the tower.[[25]](#cite_note-:2-26)

A repetitive slip-forming process was used to construct the tower's core floor by floor.[[31]](#cite_note-GenOn-32) By late April 2011, the tower's steel reinforcement had risen to the 18th floor, while its concrete core had reached the 15th floor, and floor framing had been completed up to the fourth floor.[[31]](#cite_note-GenOn-32) By late December 2011, the tower's foundations had been completed, and its steel construction had risen above the 30th floor.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In the first months of 2012, cracks began appearing in the roads near the tower's construction site. These were blamed on ground [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence), which was likely caused by excessive [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater) extraction in the Shanghai area, rather than by the weight of the Shanghai Tower.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

By May 2012, the tower's core stood 250 meters (820 feet) high, while floors had been framed to a height of 200 meters (660 ft).[[34]](#cite_note-:5-35) By early September 2012, the core had reached a height of 338 meters (1,109 ft).[[35]](#cite_note-36) By the end of 2012, the tower had reached the 90th floor, standing approximately 425 meters (1,394 ft) tall.[[36]](#cite_note-37) By 11 April 2013, the tower had reached 108 stories, standing over 500 meters (1,600 ft) tall and thusly exceeding the heights of its two adjacent supertall skyscrapers, the Jin Mao Tower and the Shanghai World Financial Center.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Construction crews laid the final structural beam of the tower on 3 August 2013, thus [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) the tower as China's tallest, and the world's second-tallest, building.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-39)[[39]](#cite_note-TopOut-40) A topping-out ceremony was held at the site of the last beam.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-39)[[40]](#cite_note-41) During the ceremony, Gensler co-founder [Art Gensler](/wiki/Art_Gensler) stated:

The Shanghai Tower represents a new way of defining and creating cities. By incorporating best practices in sustainability and high-performance design, by weaving the building into the urban fabric of Shanghai and drawing community life into the building, Shanghai Tower redefines the role of tall buildings in contemporary cities and raises the bar for the next generation of super-highrises.[[41]](#cite_note-AzoAug2013-42)
/wiki/File:View_on_the_observatory_level_of_Shanghai_Tower.jpgView from Shanghai Tower Observation DeckThe principal architect of the project, Jun Xia, said: "With the topping out of Shanghai Tower, the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui) trio will serve as a stunning representation of our past, our present, and China's boundless future."[[41]](#cite_note-AzoAug2013-42) Gu Jianping, general manager of the Shanghai Tower Construction Company, expressed the firm's wish "to provide higher quality office and shopping space, as well as contribute to the completeness of the city skyline's and the entire region's functionality".[[39]](#cite_note-TopOut-40)

In January 2014, the tower's crown structure passed the 600-meter (2,000 ft) mark when its construction entered its final phase.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The tower's crown structure was completed in August 2014, and its façade was completed shortly after.[[43]](#cite_note-Aug2014-44) The tower's interior construction and electrical fitting-out were completed in late 2014.[[44]](#cite_note-Econ2015-45)[[45]](#cite_note-Lady2015-46)[[43]](#cite_note-Aug2014-44) The opening was gradually introduced during the summer of 2015.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18)

Until June 2017, the tower faced problems attracting tenants due to the absence of all the necessary permits from the local fire department, and consequent impossibility of obtaining the official occupancy permit.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Following a report in June 2017, approximately 60% of its office space has been leased, but only 33% of those tenants have moved in, leaving entire floors of the tower empty; the luxury J hotel has also yet to open. The tower's floor plate has an "efficiency rate of only 50 per cent on some floors, compared with 70 per cent for a typical [skyscraper]", as the tower's "much-talked-about outer skin, which is ideal for allowing in natural light and cuts down on air-conditioning costs... means much of the floor space can't be used."[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In July 2020, major water leaks broke out from the 9th to 60th floors of the tower, which damaged a number of office equipment and electronics. The tower management said that following emergency repairs, a comprehensive inspection would be taken on the floor where the leak originated, later reporting that the leaks had been caused by equipment failure.[[48]](#cite_note-49) According to Shanghai Observer, misinformation videos had circulated online alleging that the tower's ceiling was collapsed using footage from a shopping center in [Nanning](/wiki/Nanning) in a 2016 incident.[[49]](#cite_note-50) On 19 June 2021, the J Hotel Shanghai Tower, a luxury hotel owned and operated by Jin Jiang International, a major Chinese hotel and tourism company, opened for business. Occupying the topmost 120th floor of the tower with 165 rooms, it became the "highest hotel in the world" according to CNN.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Being the second-tallest building in the world from since its completion in 2015, the Shanghai lost its title in 2021, when the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) surpassed it. The tower surpassed the Shanghai Tower by slightly over 49 meters (160 feet).

In December 2022, it was reported that, following its initial pre-pandemic tenancy issues, the tower had reached office space occupancy rates of 80% and commercial space occupancy rates of 98%. The tower management disclosed that 41% of its leased space were to foreign company tenants, which included JPMorgan, CNG Paribas and Allianz, and 30% were to Fortune 500 companies.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Current tenants of the tower also include [Alibaba](/wiki/Alibaba_Group), [Intesa Sanpaolo](/wiki/Intesa_Sanpaolo) and [AllBright Law Offices](/wiki/AllBright_Law_Offices).[[52]](#cite_note-53)

/wiki/File:Shanghai_tower_2021_summer.jpgShanghai Tower in 2021
The Shanghai Tower was designed by the American architectural firm [Gensler](/wiki/Gensler), with Shanghainese architect [Jun Xia](/wiki/Jun_Xia_(architect)) leading the design team.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The tower takes the form of nine cylindrical buildings stacked atop each other that total 128 floors, all enclosed by the inner layer of the glass [facade](/wiki/Facade).[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4) Between that and the outer layer, which twists as it rises, nine indoor zones provide public space for visitors.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[55]](#cite_note-56) Each of these nine areas has its own [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)), featuring gardens, cafés, restaurants and retail space, and providing panoramic views of the city.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Both layers of the façade are transparent, and retail and event spaces are provided at the tower's base.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4) The transparent façade is a unique design feature, because most buildings have only a single façade using highly reflective glass to reduce heat absorption, but the Shanghai Tower's [double layer of glass](/wiki/Double-skin_facade) eliminates the need for either layer to be opaqued.[[57]](#cite_note-matternetwork-58) The tower can accommodate as many as 16,000 people daily.[[58]](#cite_note-ThermosF-59)

The Shanghai Tower joins the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) and [SWFC](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Centre) to form the world's first adjacent grouping of three supertall buildings. Its 258-room hot
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="fdde3bab3652268a">>>"
}
