These were blamed on ground [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence), which was likely caused by excessive [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater) extraction in the Shanghai area, rather than by the weight of the Shanghai Tower.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

By May 2012, the tower's core stood 250 meters (820 feet) high, while floors had been framed to a height of 200 meters (660 ft).[[34]](#cite_note-:5-35) By early September 2012, the core had reached a height of 338 meters (1,109 ft).[[35]](#cite_note-36) By the end of 2012, the tower had reached the 90th floor, standing approximately 425 meters (1,394 ft) tall.[[36]](#cite_note-37) By 11 April 2013, the tower had reached 108 stories, standing over 500 meters (1,600 ft) tall and thusly exceeding the heights of its two adjacent supertall skyscrapers, the Jin Mao Tower and the Shanghai World Financial Center.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Construction crews laid the final structural beam of the tower on 3 August 2013, thus [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) the tower as China's tallest, and the world's second-tallest, building.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-39)[[39]](#cite_note-TopOut-40) A topping-out ceremony was held at the site of the last beam.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-39)[[40]](#cite_note-41) During the ceremony, Gensler co-founder [Art Gensler](/wiki/Art_Gensler) stated:

The Shanghai Tower represents a new way of defining and creating cities. By incorporating best practices in sustainability and high-performance design, by weaving the building into the urban fabric of Shanghai and drawing community life into the building, Shanghai Tower redefines the role of tall buildings in contemporary cities and raises the bar for the next generation of super-highrises.[[41]](#cite_note-AzoAug2013-42)
/wiki/File:View_on_the_observatory_level_of_Shanghai_Tower.jpgView from Shanghai Tower Observation DeckThe principal architect of the project, Jun Xia, said: "With the topping out of Shanghai Tower, the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui) trio will serve as a stunning representation of our past, our present, and China's boundless future."[[41]](#cite_note-AzoAug2013-42) Gu Jianping, general manager of the Shanghai Tower Construction Company, expressed the firm's wish "to provide higher quality office and shopping space, as well as contribute to the completeness of the city skyline's and the entire region's functionality".[[39]](#cite_note-TopOut-40)

In January 2014, the tower's crown structure passed the 600-meter (2,000 ft) mark when its construction entered its final phase.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The tower's crown structure was completed in August 2014, and its façade was completed shortly after.[[43]](#cite_note-Aug2014-44) The tower's interior construction and electrical fitting-out were completed in late 2014.[[44]](#cite_note-Econ2015-45)[[45]](#cite_note-Lady2015-46)[[43]](#cite_note-Aug2014-44) The opening was gradually introduced during the summer of 2015.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18)

Until June 2017, the tower faced problems attracting tenants due to the absence of all the necessary permits from the local fire department, and consequent impossibility of obtaining the official occupancy permit.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Following a report in June 2017, approximately 60% of its office space has been leased, but only 33% of those tenants have moved in, leaving entire floors of the tower empty; the luxury J hotel has also yet to open. The tower's floor plate has an "efficiency rate of only 50 per cent on some floors, compared with 70 per cent for a typical [skyscraper]", as the tower's "much-talked-about outer skin, which is ideal for allowing in natural light and cuts down on air-conditioning costs... means much of the floor space can't be used."[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In July 2020, major water leaks broke out from the 9th to 60th floors of the tower, which damaged a number of office equipment and electronics. The tower management said that following emergency repairs, a comprehensive inspection would be taken on the floor where the leak originated, later reporting that the leaks had been caused by equipment failure.[[48]](#cite_note-49) According to Shanghai Observer, misinformation videos had circulated online alleging that the tower's ceiling was collapsed using footage from a shopping center in [Nanning](/wiki/Nanning) in a 2016 incident.[[49]](#cite_note-50) On 19 June 2021, the J Hotel Shanghai Tower, a luxury hotel owned and operated by Jin Jiang International, a major Chinese hotel and tourism company, opened for business. Occupying the topmost 120th floor of the tower with 165 rooms, it became the "highest hotel in the world" according to CNN.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Being the second-tallest building in the world from since its completion in 2015, the Shanghai lost its title in 2021, when the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) surpassed it. The tower surpassed the Shanghai Tower by slightly over 49 meters (160 feet).

In December 2022, it was reported that, following its initial pre-pandemic tenancy issues, the tower had reached office space occupancy rates of 80% and commercial space occupancy rates of 98%. The tower management disclosed that 41% of its leased space were to foreign company tenants, which included JPMorgan, CNG Paribas and Allianz, and 30% were to Fortune 500 companies.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Current tenants of the tower also include [Alibaba](/wiki/Alibaba_Group), [Intesa Sanpaolo](/wiki/Intesa_Sanpaolo) and [AllBright Law Offices](/wiki/AllBright_Law_Offices).[[52]](#cite_note-53)

/wiki/File:Shanghai_tower_2021_summer.jpgShanghai Tower in 2021
The Shanghai Tower was designed by the American architectural firm [Gensler](/wiki/Gensler), with Shanghainese architect [Jun Xia](/wiki/Jun_Xia_(architect)) leading the design team.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The tower takes the form of nine cylindrical buildings stacked atop each other that total 128 floors, all enclosed by the inner layer of the glass [facade](/wiki/Facade).[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4) Between that and the outer layer, which twists as it rises, nine indoor zones provide public space for visitors.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[55]](#cite_note-56) Each of these nine areas has its own [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)), featuring gardens, cafés, restaurants and retail space, and providing panoramic views of the city.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Both layers of the façade are transparent, and retail and event spaces are provided at the tower's base.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4) The transparent façade is a unique design feature, because most buildings have only a single façade using highly reflective glass to reduce heat absorption, but the Shanghai Tower's [double layer of glass](/wiki/Double-skin_facade) eliminates the need for either layer to be opaqued.[[57]](#cite_note-matternetwork-58) The tower can accommodate as many as 16,000 people daily.[[58]](#cite_note-ThermosF-59)

The Shanghai Tower joins the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) and [SWFC](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Centre) to form the world's first adjacent grouping of three supertall buildings. Its 258-room hot
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="fdde3bab3652268a">>>

Lotte World Tower ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language): 롯데월드타워; [RR](/wiki/Revised_Romanization_of_Korean): lotdewoldeutawo) is a 123-story, 555 metres (1,821 ft; 21,900 in) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers), located in [Sincheon-dong](/wiki/Sincheon-dong,_Seoul), [Songpa District](/wiki/Songpa_District), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea). It is [the sixth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), the tallest in [OECD](/wiki/OECD) countries and [in South Korea](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_South_Korea), and also the first in South Korea to be over 100 stories tall. It was the fifth-tallest in the world when completed.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The tower opened to the public on April 3, 2017.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A "Sky Bridge Tour" is located on the roof of Lotte World Tower at 541 m (1,775 ft),[[6]](#cite_note-6) which is about the same height of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City) (1,776 feet or 541 meters), the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/Tallest_building_in_the_United_States).

After 13 years of planning and site preparation,[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) the tower gained final approval to start construction by the government in November 2010[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) and the first groundbreaking activities of piling and frame assembly were observed at the construction site in March 2012.

On December 31, 2015, the [LED](/wiki/LED)-[pixels](/wiki/Pixel) of the façade displayed the number "2016".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On April 3, 2017, Lotte shot off fireworks to celebrate the tower's official opening.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Fireworks display of the Lotte World Tower's grand opening
On January 1, 2018, Lotte shot off fireworks with a LED laser show for seven minutes to celebrate New Year's Day and the [2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On March 17, 2016, before the final phase of external construction, the [diagrid](/wiki/Diagrid) lantern-shaped roof structure was completed. The roof structure was constructed with steel counterparts that are each 12 m (39 ft) and weigh 20 tonnes (20 long tons; 22 short tons). The counterparts were made up of bent metal panels that are 6 centimetres (2.4 in) thick. The roof structure itself is 120 m (390 ft) high and covers floors 107–123. Approximately 3,000 tonnes (3,000 long tons; 3,300 short tons) of steel parts, a high-precision 64-tonne (63-long-ton; 71-short-ton) tower crane, and [GPS](/wiki/GPS) alignment systems, as well as welding technicians, were used in the construction of the roof.

The roof structure is engineered to withstand its weight without reinforcing pillars, endure earthquakes up to a magnitude of 9 under the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale), and winds up to 80 m/s (260 ft/s).

The exterior of pale-coloured glass draws inspiration from Korean [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic_art) and features accents of metal [filigree](/wiki/Filigree).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The tower incorporates several environmental sustainability features, including an advanced heat pump system for heating and cooling, which utilizes 720 geothermal probes installed about 200 meters underground as well as water from the Han River. Additional technologies include heat-insulating glass with passive shading systems, photovoltaic panels, wind turbines, and rainwater harvesting.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

External videos
/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg [https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ru1XEFllhtE](https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ru1XEFllhtE) The elevators in Lotte World Tower

Seoul Sky occupies the top seven levels. Floors 117–118 are the entrance and view floor, including a glass floor and sky show on 118. Sky Friends Cafe and the sky terrace are located on floors 119–120. Seoul Sky Cafe and a souvenir shop are on floors 121–122, and the premium lounge bar, 123 lounge, is on the top floor at 499 m (1,637 ft).

The observatory charges an admission for entry.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The skylight of Seoul Sky was the highest glass floor observatory as of the tower's completion. The tower and One World Trade Center have concluded an operation and technical service agreement.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
The operating hours of the observatory are divided into two periods. From Sunday to Thursday, it is open from 10:30 AM to 10:00 PM. On Fridays, Saturdays, and public holidays, it is open from 10:30 AM to 11:00 PM.

## Incidents and accidents
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=4)]

In 2013 and 2014, three construction workers died during the construction of the tower, and an executive director of Lotte's construction arm was given a suspended sentence of eight months in prison in 2016 for his role in neglecting safety measures at the site.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In December 2014, the government of Seoul ordered the mall and cinema complex at the base of the tower, which opened before completion of the tower, to be closed for five months after water was found leaking from an internal aquarium. Independent engineers who assessed the building found that the issues bore little relation to the overall structural integrity of the building and the mall and cinema complex reopened in 2015.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Illegal entries and climbing
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=6)]

In 2016, two men from Ukraine illegally climbed the under-construction Lotte World Tower via its stairs, and one of them free solo climbed up the crane to the top.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Lotte World Tower had advance knowledge of their interest in climbing the tower and their entering the country, and responded with the posting of approximately 400 security agents around the construction site.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Posters with the photos of the two stating they were banned from the building were plastered around the construction site.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On June 12, 2023, a 24-year-old British man illegally climbed the tower without ropes from the ground. The building security discovered him on the 42nd floor at 192 m (630 ft) above ground at 7:50 am. While he was taking a break on the 72nd floor, the police stopped him on the 73rd floor at 313 m (1,027 ft) above ground by using a [building maintenance unit](/wiki/Building_maintenance_unit) at 8:47 am. After his arrest, he said that he had planned the climb for six months.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Cherry Blossom Festival
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=8)]

Even when Lotte World Tower was under construction, there were many people who celebrated the cherry blossom festival from April 10 to 12, 2015, and it is still possible to celebrate after its completion. In 2017, the Seokchon Lake cherry blossom festival was held from April 1 to 9. It was held at Seokchon Lake waterfront stages (East and West Lake), Seoul Playground, and Gallery Sue, and was free of charge. Recently, it was held from April 5 to 9 in 2023, and March 27 to 31 in 2024.[[24]](#cite_note-Lee_2024-03-21_StripesKorea-24)

The World Tower was completed in December 2016, and the fireworks festival was held for the first time on April 2, 2017. The venue is in the Lotte World Tower and Seokchon Lake area and is free of charge. There was a K-POP concert from 7:30 pm to 9 pm, a Lotte World Tower fireworks show from 9 pm to 9:10 pm, and recreation and giveaways from 9:10 to 9:30 pm. The Lotte World Tower Fireworks Show was held again on May 4, 2019, and was accompanied by an eight-hour public broadcast event in collaboration with CBS Music FM.[[25]](#cite_note-Choi_2019-04-26_mk-25) From 2020 to 2022, it is not progressing due to COVID-19.

The [Lotte Championship](/wiki/Lotte_Championship), an official event on the [LPGA Tour](/wiki/LPGA_Tour) held in Hawaii, has its trophy modeled after the World Tower.

### Citizen's evaluation
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=12)]

In 2014, Lotte held a 10-day pre- opening in the second week of September, commissioning experts to diagnose the structural safety of the building and offering preview tours to the public, which was generally well received, although it was pointed out that Lotte's actions were merely a publicity stunt for the building. The building under construction at the time received negative reviews for being dange

Lotte World Tower ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language): 롯데월드타워; [RR](/wiki/Revised_Romanization_of_Korean): lotdewoldeutawo) is a 123-story, 555 metres (1,821 ft; 21,900 in) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers), located in [Sincheon-dong](/wiki/Sincheon-dong,_Seoul), [Songpa District](/wiki/Songpa_District), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea). It is [the sixth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), the tallest in [OECD](/wiki/OECD) countries and [in South Korea](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_South_Korea), and also the first in South Korea to be over 100 stories tall. It was the fifth-tallest in the world when completed.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The tower opened to the public on April 3, 2017.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A "Sky Bridge Tour" is located on the roof of Lotte World Tower at 541 m (1,775 ft),[[6]](#cite_note-6) which is about the same height of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City) (1,776 feet or 541 meters), the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/Tallest_building_in_the_United_States).

After 13 years of planning and site preparation,[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) the tower gained final approval to start construction by the government in November 2010[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) and the first groundbreaking activities of piling and frame assembly were observed at the construction site in March 2012.

On December 31, 2015, the [LED](/wiki/LED)-[pixels](/wiki/Pixel) of the façade displayed the number "2016".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On April 3, 2017, Lotte shot off fireworks to celebrate the tower's official opening.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Fireworks display of the Lotte World Tower's grand opening
On January 1, 2018, Lotte shot off fireworks with a LED laser show for seven minutes to celebrate New Year's Day and the [2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On March 17, 2016, before the final phase of external construction, the [diagrid](/wiki/Diagrid) lantern-shaped roof structure was completed. The roof structure was constructed with steel counterparts that are each 12 m (39 ft) and weigh 20 tonnes (20 long tons; 22 short tons). The counterparts were made up of bent metal panels that are 6 centimetres (2.4 in) thick. The roof structure itself is 120 m (390 ft) high and covers floors 107–123. Approximately 3,000 tonnes (3,000 long tons; 3,300 short tons) of steel parts, a high-precision 64-tonne (63-long-ton; 71-short-ton) tower crane, and [GPS](/wiki/GPS) alignment systems, as well as welding technicians, were used in the construction of the roof.

The roof structure is engineered to withstand its weight without reinforcing pillars, endure earthquakes up to a magnitude of 9 under the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale), and winds up to 80 m/s (260 ft/s).

The exterior of pale-coloured glass draws inspiration from Korean [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic_art) and features accents of metal [filigree](/wiki/Filigree).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The tower incorporates several environmental sustainability features, including an advanced heat pump system for heating and cooling, which utilizes 720 geothermal probes installed about 200 meters underground as well as water from the Han River. Additional technologies include heat-insulating glass with passive shading systems, photovoltaic panels, wind turbines, and rainwater harvesting.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

External videos
/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg [https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ru1XEFllhtE](https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ru1XEFllhtE) The elevators in Lotte World Tower

Seoul Sky occupies the top seven levels. Floors 117–118 are the entrance and view floor, including a glass floor and sky show on 118. Sky Friends Cafe and the sky terrace are located on floors 119–120. Seoul Sky Cafe and a souvenir shop are on floors 121–122, and the premium lounge bar, 123 lounge, is on the top floor at 499 m (1,637 ft).

The observatory charges an admission for entry.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The skylight of Seoul Sky was the highest glass floor observatory as of the tower's completion. The tower and One World Trade Center have concluded an operation and technical service agreement.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
The operating hours of the observatory are divided into two periods. From Sunday to Thursday, it is open from 10:30 AM to 10:00 PM. On Fridays, Saturdays, and public holidays, it is open from 10:30 AM to 11:00 PM.

## Incidents and accidents
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=4)]

In 2013 and 2014, three construction workers died during the construction of the tower, and an executive director of Lotte's construction arm was given a suspended sentence of eight months in prison in 2016 for his role in neglecting safety measures at the site.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In December 2014, the government of Seoul ordered the mall and cinema complex at the base of the tower, which opened before completion of the tower, to be closed for five months after water was found leaking from an internal aquarium. Independent engineers who assessed the building found that the issues bore little relation to the overall structural integrity of the building and the mall and cinema complex reopened in 2015.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Illegal entries and climbing
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=6)]

In 2016, two men from Ukraine illegally climbed the under-construction Lotte World Tower via its stairs, and one of them free solo climbed up the crane to the top.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Lotte World Tower had advance knowledge of their interest in climbing the tower and their entering the country, and responded with the posting of approximately 400 security agents around the construction site.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Posters with the photos of the two stating they were banned from the building were plastered around the construction site.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On June 12, 2023, a 24-year-old British man illegally climbed the tower without ropes from the ground. The building security discovered him on the 42nd floor at 192 m (630 ft) above ground at 7:50 am. While he was taking a break on the 72nd floor, the police stopped him on the 73rd floor at 313 m (1,027 ft) above ground by using a [building maintenance unit](/wiki/Building_maintenance_unit) at 8:47 am. After his arrest, he said that he had planned the climb for six months.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Cherry Blossom Festival
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=8)]

Even when Lotte World Tower was under construction, there were many people who celebrated the cherry blossom festival from April 10 to 12, 2015, and it is still possible to celebrate after its completion. In 2017, the Seokchon Lake cherry blossom festival was held from April 1 to 9. It was held at Seokchon Lake waterfront stages (East and West Lake), Seoul Playground, and Gallery Sue, and was free of charge. Recently, it was held from April 5 to 9 in 2023, and March 27 to 31 in 2024.[[24]](#cite_note-Lee_2024-03-21_StripesKorea-24)

The World Tower was completed in December 2016, and the fireworks festival was held for the first time on April 2, 2017. The venue is in the Lotte World Tower and Seokchon Lake area and is free of charge. There was a K-POP concert from 7:30 pm to 9 pm, a Lotte World Tower fireworks show from 9 pm to 9:10 pm, and recreation and giveaways from 9:10 to 9:30 pm. The Lotte World Tower Fireworks Show was held again on May 4, 2019, and was accompanied by an eight-hour public broadcast event in collaboration with CBS Music FM.[[25]](#cite_note-Choi_2019-04-26_mk-25) From 2020 to 2022, it is not progressing due to COVID-19.

The [Lotte Championship](/wiki/Lotte_Championship), an official event on the [LPGA Tour](/wiki/LPGA_Tour) held in Hawaii, has its trophy modeled after the World Tower.

### Citizen's evaluation
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lotte_World_Tower&action=edit&section=12)]

In 2014, Lotte held a 10-day pre- opening in the second week of September, commissioning experts to diagnose the structural safety of the building and offering preview tours to the public, which was generally well received, although it was pointed out that Lotte's actions were merely a publicity stunt for the building. The Clock Towers ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج الساعة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj as-Sāʿaẗ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the Clock', formerly known as Abraj al-Bait [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج البيت, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj al-Bayt, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the House') is a [government-owned](/wiki/State_ownership) hotel complex with seven [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) [hotels](/wiki/Hotel) in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia). These towers are part of the [King Abdulaziz Endowment Project](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia#Domestic_affairs) that aims to modernize the city in catering to its pilgrims. The central hotel tower, which is the Mecca Royal Clock Tower, is the fourth-[tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and sixth-[tallest freestanding structure in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_freestanding_structures).[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the Mecca Royal Clock Tower is the tallest clock tower in the world, and the complex of seven buildings comprise the world's second most expensive building.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The clock faces are the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_clock_faces), and the top four floors of the clock tower house the [Clock Tower Museum](/wiki/Clock_Tower_Museum).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The building complex is 300 metres (980 ft) away from the world's largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam)'s [most sacred site](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam), the [Great Mosque of Mecca](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Mecca).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The developer and contractor of the complex is the [Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group), the Kingdom's largest [construction company](/wiki/Construction_company). The total cost of construction totaled US$15 billion. The complex was built after the demolition of the [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress), the 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) citadel on top of a hill overlooking the Grand Mosque.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [destruction of the historically significant site](/wiki/Destruction_of_early_Islamic_heritage_sites_in_Saudi_Arabia) in 2002 by the [Saudi government](/wiki/Saudi_government) sparked an outcry and a strong reaction from Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_3.jpgThe Mecca Royal Clock Tower's, also known as Massive Mohammad after [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben)'s clocks, the largest in the world
The tallest tower in the complex is the [tallest building in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Saudi_Arabia), at 601 meters (1,972 feet) and a total surface area of 32,000 square meters. It is the sixth-tallest freestanding structure in the world, surpassing the [Ping An Finance Centre](/wiki/Ping_An_Finance_Centre) in [Shenzhen, China](/wiki/Shenzhen) but shorter than the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai, UAE](/wiki/Dubai), the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in [Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur,_Malaysia), the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan,_New_York_City), the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) in [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan), the [Zifeng Tower](/wiki/Zifeng_Tower) in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu), [China](/wiki/China), the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) in [Shanghai, China](/wiki/Shanghai,_China), the [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto), Ontario, Canada, and the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower) in [Guangzhou, China](/wiki/Guangzhou,_China).[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The site of the complex is across the piazza to the south from the main entrance (King Abdul-Aziz Gate) to the [Masjid al Haram mosque](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram), which houses the [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba). It accommodates worshippers visiting the Kaaba. The tallest tower in the complex also contains a five-star hotel, operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts, to help provide lodging for the millions of pilgrims that travel to Mecca annually to participate in the Hajj.

In addition, The Clock Towers has a five-story shopping mall (The Clock Towers Shopping Center) and a parking garage capable of holding over a thousand vehicles.

The building was planned to be 734 m (2,408 ft) tall in 2006. In 2009, it was published that the final height would be 601 m (1,972 ft). The complex was built by the Saudi Binladin Group, Saudi Arabia's largest construction company. The façade was constructed by Premiere Composite Technologies, and the clock by German tower clock manufacturer PERROT GmbH & Co. KG Turmuhren und Läuteanlagen. Because of its location in Mecca, non-Muslim members of the construction team were not allowed to visit the site, and some specialists from Poland and Germany converted to Islam to be able to do so.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to the Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments, the overall project cost US$15 billion.

There are clock faces on all four side of the main hotel tower. The total height of the clock is 57 m (187 ft), situated above the media displays under the clock faces. At 43 m × 43 m (141 ft × 141 ft), these are the largest in the world. The roof of the clock is 450 m (1,480 ft) above the ground, making it the world's most elevated architectural clock. A spire has been added on top of the clock giving it a total height of 601 m (1,972 ft). Behind the clock faces, there is an astronomy exhibition. In the spire base and the iron-covered floors (The Jewel) there is a scientific center that is used to sight the moon at the beginning of the Islamic months, and to operate an atomic clock that controls the tower clock faces.

### List of component towers

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup.jpgThe Clock Towers' architecture has fine detail
/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup_2.jpgThe Clock Towers' cladding

The building is topped by a four-faced clock, visible from 25 kilometres (16 miles) away. The clock is the highest in the world at over 400 m (1,300 ft) above the ground, surpassing the [Allen-Bradley clock tower](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation_Headquarters_and_Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee). The clock faces are the largest in the world, surpassing the [Cevahir Mall](/wiki/Istanbul_Cevahir) clock in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul).

Each of the clock's four faces measures 43 m (141 ft) in diameter and are illuminated by 2 million LED lights, with four oriented edges, just above the clock alongside huge Arabic script reading: "[God is [the] greatest](/wiki/Takbir)" on the north and south faces and on the west and east "[There is no god but Allah. Muhammad is the messenger of God.](/wiki/Shahada)" Four golden domes on pillars on all the corners are also present. The same as the [Saudi flag](/wiki/Saudi_flag), fitted at the top of the clock, flash to signal Islam's five-time daily prayers. On special occasions such as new year, 21,000 green and white xenon bulbs and LED lamps during the call to the five prayers times of the day. On special occasions, 40 beacon lights create lighting effects. In addition, strong lasers throw their rays 30 kilometers into the sky. The clock's four faces are covered with 98 million pieces of glass mosaics. The [Saudi coat of arms](/wiki/Emblem_of_Saudi_Arabia) is displayed at the center of each clock behind the dials. The minute hand is 23 m (75 ft) long, while the hour hand is 18 m (59 ft) long.

There were reports that the clock would be set to [Mecca Time](/wiki/Mecca_Time), in an attempt to replace the IERS Reference Meridian as the prime meridian for global time keeping, but the clock is set to Arabia Standard Time ([UTC+03:00](/wiki/UTC%2B03:00)).

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_1.jpgThe Clock Towers' clock and spire
/wiki/File:Comparison_four_face_clocks.jpgComparison of some notable four-face clocks at the same scale.
 Top-left: [Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower](/wiki/Metropolitan_Life_Insurance_Company_Tower)
 Bottom-left: [Allen-Bradley Clock Tower](/wiki/Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) (previous largest)
 Middle: [Abraj Al Bait](/wiki/Abraj_Al_Bait)
 Top-right: [Elizabeth Tower](/wiki/Elizabeth_Tower) ("Big Ben")
 Bottom-right: [Kremlin Clock](/wiki/Kremlin_Clock)

The spire has an eight-story glass-covered base (The Jewel) which belongs to a scientific center having its own small exhibition, another observation deck at 484 m (1,588 ft). The highest floor in The Jewel is the Control Tower Floor, which was planned to be used for controlling air traffic in the sky above Mecca (mainly helicopters, as airplanes are not allowed near Mecca). However, this was skipped for technical reasons and the future usage is not clear yet. Above from The Jewel, the spire has only technical installations for sound, light, and other infrastructure and eventually the last viewing deck and the crescent above it. The crescent is 21 m (69 ft) in diameter, has two regular floors with living areas, a prayer room in the center of the crescent[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a few service floors and rooms.

The crescent was constructed in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) in April 2011. It is made of fiberglass-backed [mosaic gold](/wiki/Mosaic_gold), and it weighs up to 35 tonnes. It cost 90 million [United Arab Emirates dirhams](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham) and took three months to build.

The minaret and its base have loudspeakers which broadcast prayer calls to a distance of 7 km (4.3 mi) away and across an area of approximately 160 km2 (62 sq mi).

The Clock Towers complex had two fire incidents during construction. The first fire accident was at Hajar Tower on 28 October 2008. It took 400 firefighters to put out the fire, which burned for 10 hours, consuming nine floors of the tower.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21) According to eyewitness reports, the blaze erupted shortly after midnight, and spread rapidly because of wood used for construction stored in the premises. Soon, the entire building was engulfed in smoke. Hospitals were put on high alert, but no injuries were reported. A civil defence spokesman claimed the fire started on the 32nd floor of the Hajar Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The second fire struck the Safa Tower on 1 May 2009. No deaths or injuries were reported in the blaze which was quickly contained by Civil Defence. Eyewitnesses said the fire broke out soon after [Asr prayer](/wiki
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="fc3b4578e9c7cc66">>>"
}

The Clock Towers
أبراج الساعة

/wiki/File:Abraj_Al_Bait_Tower_2017.jpg

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system)[21°25′08″N 39°49′35″E / 21.418888888889°N 39.826388888889°E](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=The_Clock_Towers&params=21.418888888889_N_39.826388888889_E_type:landmark_region:SA)

Structural engineer[SL Rasch GmbH](/wiki/SL_Rasch_GmbH_Special_and_Lightweight_Structures) and [Dar Al-Handasah](/wiki/Dar_Al-Handasah)

The Clock Towers ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج الساعة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj as-Sāʿaẗ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the Clock', formerly known as Abraj al-Bait [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج البيت, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj al-Bayt, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the House') is a [government-owned](/wiki/State_ownership) hotel complex with seven [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) [hotels](/wiki/Hotel) in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia). These towers are part of the [King Abdulaziz Endowment Project](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia#Domestic_affairs) that aims to modernize the city in catering to its pilgrims. The central hotel tower, which is the Mecca Royal Clock Tower, is the fourth-[tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and sixth-[tallest freestanding structure in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_freestanding_structures).[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the Mecca Royal Clock Tower is the tallest clock tower in the world, and the complex of seven buildings comprise the world's second most expensive building.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The clock faces are the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_clock_faces), and the top four floors of the clock tower house the [Clock Tower Museum](/wiki/Clock_Tower_Museum).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The building complex is 300 metres (980 ft) away from the world's largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam)'s [most sacred site](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam), the [Great Mosque of Mecca](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Mecca).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The developer and contractor of the complex is the [Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group), the Kingdom's largest [construction company](/wiki/Construction_company). The total cost of construction totaled US$15 billion. The complex was built after the demolition of the [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress), the 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) citadel on top of a hill overlooking the Grand Mosque.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [destruction of the historically significant site](/wiki/Destruction_of_early_Islamic_heritage_sites_in_Saudi_Arabia) in 2002 by the [Saudi government](/wiki/Saudi_government) sparked an outcry and a strong reaction from Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_3.jpgThe Mecca Royal Clock Tower's, also known as Massive Mohammad after [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben)'s clocks, the largest in the world
The tallest tower in the complex is the [tallest building in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Saudi_Arabia), at 601 meters (1,972 feet) and a total surface area of 32,000 square meters. It is the sixth-tallest freestanding structure in the world, surpassing the [Ping An Finance Centre](/wiki/Ping_An_Finance_Centre) in [Shenzhen, China](/wiki/Shenzhen) but shorter than the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai, UAE](/wiki/Dubai), the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in [Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur,_Malaysia), the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan,_New_York_City), the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) in [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan), the [Zifeng Tower](/wiki/Zifeng_Tower) in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu), [China](/wiki/China), the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) in [Shanghai, China](/wiki/Shanghai,_China), the [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto), Ontario, Canada, and the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower) in [Guangzhou, China](/wiki/Guangzhou,_China).[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The site of the complex is across the piazza to the south from the main entrance (King Abdul-Aziz Gate) to the [Masjid al Haram mosque](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram), which houses the [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba). It accommodates worshippers visiting the Kaaba. The tallest tower in the complex also contains a five-star hotel, operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts, to help provide lodging for the millions of pilgrims that travel to Mecca annually to participate in the Hajj.

In addition, The Clock Towers has a five-story shopping mall (The Clock Towers Shopping Center) and a parking garage capable of holding over a thousand vehicles.

The building was planned to be 734 m (2,408 ft) tall in 2006. In 2009, it was published that the final height would be 601 m (1,972 ft). The complex was built by the Saudi Binladin Group, Saudi Arabia's largest construction company. The façade was constructed by Premiere Composite Technologies, and the clock by German tower clock manufacturer PERROT GmbH & Co. KG Turmuhren und Läuteanlagen. Because of its location in Mecca, non-Muslim members of the construction team were not allowed to visit the site, and some specialists from Poland and Germany converted to Islam to be able to do so.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to the Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments, the overall project cost US$15 billion.

There are clock faces on all four side of the main hotel tower. The total height of the clock is 57 m (187 ft), situated above the media displays under the clock faces. At 43 m × 43 m (141 ft × 141 ft), these are the largest in the world. The roof of the clock is 450 m (1,480 ft) above the ground, making it the world's most elevated architectural clock. A spire has been added on top of the clock giving it a total height of 601 m (1,972 ft). Behind the clock faces, there is an astronomy exhibition. In the spire base and the iron-covered floors (The Jewel) there is a scientific center that is used to sight the moon at the beginning of the Islamic months, and to operate an atomic clock that controls the tower clock faces.

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup.jpgThe Clock Towers' architecture has fine detail
/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup_2.jpgThe Clock Towers' cladding

The building is topped by a four-faced clock, visible from 25 kilometres (16 miles) away. The clock is the highest in the world at over 400 m (1,300 ft) above the ground, surpassing the [Allen-Bradley clock tower](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation_Headquarters_and_Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee). The clock faces are the largest in the world, surpassing the [Cevahir Mall](/wiki/Istanbul_Cevahir) clock in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul).

Each of the clock's four faces measures 43 m (141 ft) in diameter and are illuminated by 2 million LED lights, with four oriented edges, just above the clock alongside huge Arabic script reading: "[God is [the] greatest](/wiki/Takbir)" on the north and south faces and on the west and east "[There is no god but Allah. Muhammad is the messenger of God.](/wiki/Shahada)" Four golden domes on pillars on all the corners are also present. The same as the [Saudi flag](/wiki/Saudi_flag), fitted at the top of the clock, flash to signal Islam's five-time daily prayers. On special occasions such as new year, 21,000 green and white xenon bulbs and LED lamps during the call to the five prayers times of the day. On special occasions, 40 beacon lights create lighting effects. In addition, strong lasers throw their rays 30 kilometers into the sky. The clock's four faces are covered with 98 million pieces of glass mosaics. The [Saudi coat of arms](/wiki/Emblem_of_Saudi_Arabia) is displayed at the center of each clock behind the dials. The minute hand is 23 m (75 ft) long, while the hour hand is 18 m (59 ft) long.

There were reports that the clock would be set to [Mecca Time](/wiki/Mecca_Time), in an attempt to replace the IERS Reference Meridian as the prime meridian for global time keeping, but the clock is set to Arabia Standard Time ([UTC+03:00](/wiki/UTC%2B03:00)).

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_1.jpgThe Clock Towers' clock and spire
/wiki/File:Comparison_four_face_clocks.jpgComparison of some notable four-face clocks at the same scale.
 Top-left: [Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower](/wiki/Metropolitan_Life_Insurance_Company_Tower)
 Bottom-left: [Allen-Bradley Clock Tower](/wiki/Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) (previous largest)
 Middle: [Abraj Al Bait](/wiki/Abraj_Al_Bait)
 Top-right: [Elizabeth Tower](/wiki/Elizabeth_Tower) ("Big Ben")
 Bottom-right: [Kremlin Clock](/wiki/Kremlin_Clock)

The spire has an eight-story glass-covered base (The Jewel) which belongs to a scientific center having its own small exhibition, another observation deck at 484 m (1,588 ft). The highest floor in The Jewel is the Control Tower Floor, which was planned to be used for controlling air traffic in the sky above Mecca (mainly helicopters, as airplanes are not allowed near Mecca). However, this was skipped for technical reasons and the future usage is not clear yet. Above from The Jewel, the spire has only technical installations for sound, light, and other infrastructure and eventually the last viewing deck and the crescent above it. The crescent is 21 m (69 ft) in diameter, has two regular floors with living areas, a prayer room in the center of the crescent[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a few service floors and rooms.

The crescent was constructed in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) in April 2011. It is made of fiberglass-backed [mosaic gold](/wiki/Mosaic_gold), and it weighs up to 35 tonnes. It cost 90 million [United Arab Emirates dirhams](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham) and took three months to build.

The minaret and its base have loudspeakers which broadcast prayer calls to a distance of 7 km (4.3 mi) away and across an area of approximately 160 km2 (62 sq mi).

The Clock Towers complex had two fire incidents during construction. The first fire accident was at Hajar Tower on 28 October 2008. It took 400 firefighters to put out the fire, which burned for 10 hours, consuming nine floors of the tower.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21) According to eyewitness reports, the blaze erupted shortly after midnight, and spread rapidly because of wood used for construction stored in the premises. Soon, the entire building was engulfed in smoke. Hospitals were put on high alert, but no injuries were reported. A civil defence spokesman claimed the fire started on the 32nd floor of the Hajar Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The second fire struck the Safa Tower on 1 May 2009. No deaths or injuries were reported in the blaze which was quickly contained by Civil Defence. Eyewitnesses said the fire broke out soon after [Asr prayer](/wiki

The Ping An Finance Center ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 平安金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn) is a 115-[storey](/wiki/Storey), 599.1-metre (1,966-foot) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall) in [Shenzhen, Guangdong](/wiki/Shenzhen,_Guangdong), China.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was completed in 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) and is the [tallest building in Shenzhen](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Shenzhen), the [2nd tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) (after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)), and the [5th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) The building contains office, hotel and retail spaces, a conference center, and a high-end shopping mall.

The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) is named Free Sky and is located on floor 116,[[9]](#cite_note-thetowerinfo-9) which broke the record of having the highest observation deck in a building at 562.5 m (1,845 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-10) The observation deck allows 360-degree views. Inside there are a transparent viewing area, interactive experience area, and special display area to showcase youth cultures with Shenzhen special characteristics through multimedia facilities. The opening hours are 10am to 8pm, last entry 7.15pm.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The building houses the headquarters of [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance), with their company name representing the building name. The building was commissioned by Ping An Insurance and designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates). The design of the building is meant to be unique and elegant, and to represent the history and achievements of the main tenant. A stainless-steel facade that weighs approximately 1,700 metric tons (3,700,000 lb) provides a modern design to the building.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

It is the second largest skyscraper in the world by floor area after [Azabudai Hills Main Tower](/wiki/Azabudai_Hills) in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo), Japan. The building has a gross floor area of 378,600 square metres (4,075,000 square feet). The 115-storey tower has a width-to-height aspect ratio of 1:10 and also has an 11-story podium. Including the podium, the building has 495,520 m2 (5,333,700 sq ft) of floor space. A five-level basement adds 90,000 m2 (970,000 sq ft). The 620,000-metric-ton (1.37×109 lb; 620,000,000 kg) tower has eight main columns which form the superstructure. The column dimensions range from approximately 6 by 3.2 m (19 ft 8 in by 10 ft 6 in) at the lowest level to 2.9 by 1.4 m (9 ft 6 in by 4 ft 7 in) at the top of the tower.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

The second building of the project, a 290-metre (950 ft), 47-story skyscraper known as the South Tower, has been completed. Construction began in April 2014 and it opened in 2018/19. The complex includes a 5-star [Park Hyatt](/wiki/Hyatt) hotel, a planned retail bridge connecting the two skyscrapers from levels 3 through 6.[[12]](#cite_note-tech-12)

The Ping An International Finance Center is equipped with 33 double deck elevators,[[2]](#cite_note-Schindler-2) travelling at maximum speeds of up to 10 m/s (2,000 ft/min).

/wiki/File:SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%B8%82_Shenzhen_%E7%A6%8F%E7%94%B0%E5%8D%80_Futian_Ping_An_Finance_Centre_%E5%B9%B3%E5%AE%89%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83%E5%95%86%E5%A0%B4_PAFC_Mall_interior_April_2019_IX2_22.jpgPAFC Mall in April 2019
The building is located within the [Central Business District](/wiki/Central_Business_District) of Shenzhen in [Futian](/wiki/Futian_District). Its 18,391 m2 (197,960 sq ft) lot was purchased by [Ping An Group](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance) via auction at a price of 1.6568 billion RMB on 6 November 2007. Design of the building began in 2008 with [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates) providing the architectural design and [Thornton Tomasetti](/wiki/Thornton_Tomasetti) providing structural design.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) Its [foundation stone](/wiki/Foundation_stone) was laid on 29 August 2009, and construction started in November the same year. [China Construction First Building Group](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering) was hired as the general contractor to construct the building.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

In March 2013, the construction process was temporarily halted, due to the suspected use of concrete made with unprocessed sea sand, which could corrode the steel structure.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Construction resumed on the building after sample testing.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

On the morning of 15 July 2014, upon a 10-metre (33 ft)-long steel column being lifted to place, the skyscraper exceeded 443.8 metres (1,456 ft) in height, surpassing the [KK100](/wiki/KK100) Tower to become the tallest building in Shenzhen.

The building was [topped out](/wiki/Topped_out) on 30 April 2015, and became the second tallest skyscraper in China at a height of 599.1 metres (1,966 ft). The original plan was to add a 60-metre (200 ft)-long antenna atop the building to surpass the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) and become the tallest building in China. However, in February 2015, it was decided that the antenna would not top the tower due to the possibility that it might obstruct flight paths.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The Ping An Financial Center building has been the subject of frequent [rooftopping](/wiki/Rooftopping) attempts. In January 2015, daredevil Malaysian photographer Keow Wee Loong climbed the then incomplete building and released video footage and a photo taken from a crane at the tower's top.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The structure was subsequently climbed during the period of the Chinese New Year on 19 February 2015 by two Russian and Ukrainian [urban explorers](/wiki/Urban_explorer), Vadim Makhorov and Vitaly Raskalov from [Ontheroofs](/wiki/Ontheroofs), who further climbed out to a crane above the under-construction tower and documented their ascent with video and photos.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

According to [South China Morning Post](/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post), almost 30 percent of the office space remained empty in the second quarter of 2019.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Ping An Finance Center ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 平安金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn) is a 115-[storey](/wiki/Storey), 599.1-metre (1,966-foot) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall) in [Shenzhen, Guangdong](/wiki/Shenzhen,_Guangdong), China.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was completed in 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) and is the [tallest building in Shenzhen](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Shenzhen), the [2nd tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) (after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)), and the [5th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) The building contains office, hotel and retail spaces, a conference center, and a high-end shopping mall.

The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) is named Free Sky and is located on floor 116,[[9]](#cite_note-thetowerinfo-9) which broke the record of having the highest observation deck in a building at 562.5 m (1,845 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-10) The observation deck allows 360-degree views. Inside there are a transparent viewing area, interactive experience area, and special display area to showcase youth cultures with Shenzhen special characteristics through multimedia facilities. The opening hours are 10am to 8pm, last entry 7.15pm.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The building houses the headquarters of [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance), with their company name representing the building name. The building was commissioned by Ping An Insurance and designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates). The design of the building is meant to be unique and elegant, and to represent the history and achievements of the main tenant. A stainless-steel facade that weighs approximately 1,700 metric tons (3,700,000 lb) provides a modern design to the building.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

It is the second largest skyscraper in the world by floor area after [Azabudai Hills Main Tower](/wiki/Azabudai_Hills) in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo), Japan. The building has a gross floor area of 378,600 square metres (4,075,000 square feet). The 115-storey tower has a width-to-height aspect ratio of 1:10 and also has an 11-story podium. Including the podium, the building has 495,520 m2 (5,333,700 sq ft) of floor space. A five-level basement adds 90,000 m2 (970,000 sq ft). The 620,000-metric-ton (1.37×109 lb; 620,000,000 kg) tower has eight main columns which form the superstructure. The column dimensions range from approximately 6 by 3.2 m (19 ft 8 in by 10 ft 6 in) at the lowest level to 2.9 by 1.4 m (9 ft 6 in by 4 ft 7 in) at the top of the tower.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

The second building of the project, a 290-metre (950 ft), 47-story skyscraper known as the South Tower, has been completed. Construction began in April 2014 and it opened in 2018/19. The complex includes a 5-star [Park Hyatt](/wiki/Hyatt) hotel, a planned retail bridge connecting the two skyscrapers from levels 3 through 6.[[12]](#cite_note-tech-12)

The Ping An International Finance Center is equipped with 33 double deck elevators,[[2]](#cite_note-Schindler-2) travelling at maximum speeds of up to 10 m/s (2,000 ft/min).

Tianjin CTF Finance Centre
[Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters)天津周大福滨海中心
[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)天津周大福濱海中心
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Tiānjīn Zhōu Dà Fú Bīnhǎi Zhōngxīn

Tianjin CTF Finance Centre is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall_building) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) located in the [Tianjin Economic-Technological Development Area](/wiki/Tianjin_Economic-Technological_Development_Area) [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district) of [Binhai](/wiki/Binhai_New_Area), [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin), [China](/wiki/China). Construction started in 2013 and was completed in 2019. At 530 m (1,740 ft), and with 97 floors, the tower is the second-tallest building in Municipal Tianjin after unfinished [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117), the [eighth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), and the tallest building in the world with fewer than 100 floors.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is located in the outer district of the [Tianjin Economic-Technological Development Area](/wiki/Tianjin_Economic-Technological_Development_Area). Tianjin CTF Finance Center is designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill LLP](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) in collaboration with [Ronald Lu & Partners](/wiki/Ronald_Lu_%26_Partners).

The building was honored with the Tall/Slender Structure award at the 2021 Awards Ceremony by the Institution of Structural Engineers (IStructE).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Tianjin CTF Finance Centre is designed with a gently curving glass facade that incorporates eight sloping mega-columns. The columns follow a line connecting the centers and corners of all four elevations. This design improves the building's seismic resilience and contributes to both its gravity and lateral systems. The columns are effective in increasing the stiffness of the building's perimeter frame, consequently attracting a larger portion of the seismic forces in compliance with the Chinese code requirements.

The façade reinforces the curvature of the tower form and creates a shimmering texture over the building's surface. The crystalline-like curtain wall stretches from the suspended glass canopies at each of the lobbies to the dematerialized, mega-column-looped crown and presents a bold expression of a comprehensive, integrated design on the Tianjin skyline.

/wiki/File:Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre,_4_of_24.jpgBase from street level showing concave and convex curves

By stacking reducing floor plates, the tower tapers dramatically to minimize the surface area exposed to wind, sun, and moisture. The gently-undulating curves of the facade subtly denote the integration of the three distinct programs within a singular smooth object. Square in plan with rounded corners, the floor plate geometry enables unique interior fit-outs and customization options for occupants. Research by the architect has shown that lateral forces due to vortex shedding can be controlled by tapering the vertical profile of the tower and softening any sharp corners in plan. The building's aerodynamic shape greatly reduces this vortex shedding by "confusing the wind" and disrupting the opportunity for any resonating wind forces and loads on the structure.

## Construction process
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre&action=edit&section=3)]

- On November 20, 2009, Tianjin Chow Tai Fook Financial Center held a groundbreaking ceremony. The then Tianjin Mayor [Huang Xingguo](/wiki/Huang_Xingguo) attended the ceremony.

- The Tianjin CTF Finance Centre was proposed as an idea in 2011.

- As of April 2016, one third of the entire building had been built.

- In July 2016, the core tube of the building was poured to 260 meters.

- In September 2016, the building was built as a transition truss between the office building and the serviced residential part.

- In April 2017, CTBUH confirmed that the building had been structurally topped.

- In May 2017, the steel structure on the top of the building began to be installed.

- On October 31, 2017, the steel structure crown was sealed.

- The building was completed in September 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Tianjin Chow Tai Fook Financial Center (old plan) is a polyline-shaped building with a square shape as the base, and the shape is tapered from bottom to top. In the design effect, in the daytime, the building will refract the sunlight to show various colors, and at night, the top of the inclined tower glows like a diamond. The new plan is rocket-shaped and dominated by arcs; the crown is like a cicada's wings.

- [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117)

- [Sino-Steel International Plaza](/wiki/Sino-Steel_Tower)

- [Jin Wan Plaza 1](/wiki/Jin_Wan_Plaza_1)

- [Rose Rock International Finance Center](/wiki/Rose_Rock_International_Finance_Center)

- [^](#cite_ref-2) Mwangasha, Jane (2020-12-31). ["Top 10 tallest buildings in world"](https://www.constructionkenya.com/8350/tallest-buildings-world/). CK. Retrieved 2021-05-22.

- [^](#cite_ref-3) ["Tianjin CTF wins IStructE Award for Structural Engineering Excellence"](https://www.som.com/news/tianjin-ctf-wins-istructe-award-for-structural-engineering-excellence/). SOM. November 9, 2021. Retrieved 2022-10-24.

- [^](#cite_ref-4) ["Façade engineering for Tianjin Chow Tai Fook Binhai Center - Arup"](https://www.arup.com/en/projects/tianjin-ctf-finance-centre). www.arup.com. Retrieved 2021-05-22.

[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Guǎngzhōu Dōngtǎ
[Yue: Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese)
[Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping)gwong2zau1 dung1taap3

The Guangzhou Chow Tai Fook Finance Centre ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): 广州周大福金融中心; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): 廣州周大福金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Guǎngzhōu zhōudàfú jīnróng zhōngxīn), also called East Tower, is a 530-metre (1,739 ft) [mixed-use](/wiki/Mixed-use) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall_skyscraper) in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong), [China](/wiki/China), which was completed in October 2016. It is the [tallest completed building in Guangzhou](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Guangzhou), the [third-tallest in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China), and the [eighth-tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre has a total of 111 above ground and five below ground floors and houses a shopping mall, offices, apartments, and a [hotel](/wiki/Hotel). The skyscraper has a [gross floor area](/wiki/Floor_area_(building)) of 507,681.0 square metres (5,464,633 sq ft), of which a little over 20% is not part of the skyscraper itself, but of the podium connected to it.

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is situated on a 27,000-square-metre (290,000 sq ft) lot along Zhujiang East Road in [Zhujiang New Town](/wiki/Zhujiang_New_Town), Guangzhou's [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district).[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6) In that neighbourhood, the skyscraper is located east of the central axis with an underground mall and connections to public transportation underneath it.[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) The Guangzhou CTF Centre is part of the [Guangzhou Twin Towers](/wiki/Guangzhou_Twin_Towers). The other tower of the pair, the 439-metre (1,439 ft) tall [Guangzhou International Finance Center](/wiki/Guangzhou_International_Finance_Center), is located on the other side of the axis and is also known under the name "West Tower". The Guangzhou CTF Centre is therefore known as the "East Tower" as well.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Both towers have a similar height, size, and function, and are situated close to the 604 m (1,982 ft) tall [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower).[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is owned by [Chow Tai Fook Enterprises](/wiki/Chow_Tai_Fook_Enterprises), and houses the world's fastest elevators, which can reach speeds up to 21 metres per second (69 feet per second).[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre consists of a skyscraper and an eight-floor podium, that is connected to the skyscraper. The podium features a 30 m (100 ft) high [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)) with a glass roof.[[12]](#cite_note-:4-12) The skyscraper is supported by a square core with sides of 32 m (105 ft) and eight concrete mega columns on the sides.[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) They are linked by outriggers and belt trusses, that are located exclusively on mechanical and refuge floors. The skyscraper has a total of four levels of steel outriggers and six sets of double-layer belt trusses.[[13]](#cite_note-:9-13) Smaller columns are in between the mega columns.[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) The mega columns have a length up to 5.0 m (161⁄2 ft) and a width up to 3.5 m (111⁄2 ft).[[14]](#cite_note-:12-14) They are thicker than usual to meet the Chinese [seismic codes](/wiki/Seismic_code) and they are supported by a [mat and pad foundation](/wiki/Shallow_foundation).[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8)[[13]](#cite_note-:9-13) This foundation sits on relatively high [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock).[[13]](#cite_note-:9-13)

The facade of the skyscraper is made out of glass and has vertical [mullions](/wiki/Mullion), made out of white [glazed](/wiki/Glazed_architectural_terra-cotta) [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta).[[15]](#cite_note-15) The mullions are projecting out from the windows to create shades.[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) The windows are operable and there are vents between them and the terracotta. Aluminum fins are located next to the vents to protect them from the wind. White LEDs are integrated into the fins to light the terracotta at night. The terracotta mullions make the exterior of the building lighter. It was not possible to create the brightness of the building by using lighter glass, since the local environmental codes do not allow more reflective glass.[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) More standard metal mullions would have created this effect as well, but architect Forth Bagley, who lead the design, said in an interview with [CNN](/wiki/CNN) they had chosen consciously for terracotta "to inject into the architecture an indigenous craft, that was specific to Guangzhou as a city."[[16]](#cite_note-:10-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Furthermore, he said about the design that the skyscraper was not only set apart from the surrounding towers "by virtue of its height [...], but also by its simplicity."[[16]](#cite_note-:10-16)

/wiki/File:ChowTaiFookFinanceCentreDecember2015.jpgThe skyscraper in December 2015
The shape of the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is defined by its mixed-usage: the different uses require different floor sizes and the building is shaped to accommodate those demands. Namely, the skyscraper has setbacks at points where the usage changes: the setbacks are located between the offices and the apartments, between the apartments and the hotel, and between the hotel and the crown of the building. The setbacks are angled and contain [parapets](/wiki/Parapet) with sky terraces. The top of the building is angled as well, but doesn't contain a sky terrace.[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) The angled parapets point at the tops of various nearby tall buildings.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18) Because of the setbacks, the building looks different from every angle.[[16]](#cite_note-:10-16)

Different techniques were used to make the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre more environmentally friendly. For example, it has efficient [chillers](/wiki/Chiller) and the podium's roof features solar panels. Moreover, the heat from the condensers of the chillers is recovered.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18) The office and retail parts of the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre together received a [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design) Gold certification in 2018 after having received a pre-certification in 2013.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The below ground floors and the podium of the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre feature a shopping mall and an underground parking facility with 1,705 spaces.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The shopping mall is called "K11 Art Mall" and is owned by K11, a subsidiary of Chow Tai Fook Enterprises.[[20]](#cite_note-20) It features a [4DX cinema](/wiki/4DX) with eight rooms and occupies the entire eight-floor podium and two below ground floors.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)[[12]](#cite_note-:4-12)[[21]](#cite_note-:5-21) The mall's surface area amounts to around 70,000 m2 (750,000 sq ft).[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6) The base of the actual skyscraper is occupied by the office lobby, the entrance hall of the apartments and a part of the hotel.[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is connected to other nearby buildings through outdoor pedestrian bridges and to subway lines [3](/wiki/Line_3,_Guangzhou_Metro), [5](/wiki/Line_5,_Guangzhou_Metro), and [APM](/wiki/Zhujiang_New_Town_Automated_People_Mover_System) through underground links.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18)

The offices occupy the floors 7 through 66, but are interrupted by two mechanical floors.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) Next to the 208,828.0 m2 (2,247,806 sq ft) ([GFA](/wiki/Floor_area_(building))) of [Class A](/wiki/Class_A_office_space) office space, the office floors house a [sky lobby](/wiki/Sky_lobby) and a 150-person auditorium.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The office floors are owned by K11.[[21]](#cite_note-:5-21) 24 floors with a total of 355 serviced apartments are situated above another mechanical floor and a setback.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) They are owned by [Rosewood Hotel Group](/wiki/Rosewood_Hotel_Group) and have a total area of 71,000 m2 (760,000 sq ft).[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[14]](#cite_note-:12-14) 23 of those "Rosewood Residences" are [duplex apartments](/wiki/Duplex_apartment) and some apartments have their own elevators and indoor courtyards.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) The apartments were designed by Perception Design.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

The highest sixteen floors are occupied by a [hotel](/wiki/Hotel), that is separated from the apartments by another mechanical floor.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7) The hotel, that opened in the second half of 2019,[[25]](#cite_note-25) is managed by [Rosewood Hotels & Resorts](/wiki/Rosewood_Hotels_%26_Resorts) and has a total of 251 rooms with a minimum size of 55.0 m2 (592 sq ft).[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The floor area of the entire hotel amounts to 41,000 m2 (440,000 sq ft).[[14]](#cite_note-:12-14) It also features meeting spaces, a ballroom, an indoor pool and seven cafes and restaurants. The sky bar and restaurant on the 107th floor are partly situated on an outdoor terrace. The meeting spaces cover an area of 2,250 m2 (24,200 sq ft) and the ballroom, that fits 700 people, covers an area of 970 m2 (10,440 sq ft). Most of the interior of the hotel was designed by design firm [Yabu Pushelberg](/wiki/Yabu_Pushelberg).[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The setback above the hotel is covered with three terraces. The strong winds are not felt on those terraces because of the chiseled [parapets](/wiki/Parapet).[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre has 95 elevators made by [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi), including two elevators that are the world's fastest. In 2014, Hitachi announced that those two elevators would be able to reach 1,200 m/min (3,900 ft/min) and would carry passengers through the 440-metre (1,450 ft) long shaft from the first to the 95th floor in approximately 43 seconds.[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10) These two elevators are used to bring hotel guests from the entrance to the lobby. In May 2016, the elevators were first tested and reached the promised maximum speed.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In June 2017, the elevators were tested after having modified them to be able to deliver an even faster speed above the rated speed of 20 m/s, and they managed to reach a top speed of 21 m/s (69 ft/s).[[27]](#cite_note-27) This exceeded the top speed [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)'s elevators could deliver which was a top speed of 20.5 m/s (67 ft/s),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) making the lifts within the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre the world's fastest. In September 2019, the elevator received a [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records) title as the world's fastest.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

To achieve their top speed, the elevators in the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre use permanent magnet synchronous motors.[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) The air pressure is adjusted in order to prevent ear blockages and the elevators have rollers to smoothen the ride.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Additionally, the emergency brakes can withstand temperatures up to 300 °C (572 °F).[[32]](#cite_note-32) Besides the two fastest elevators, the building has 28 [double-deck elevators](/wiki/Double-deck_elevator) (with speeds of 150–450 m/min.), 13 high-speed elevators (210–600 m/min.), and 52 medium- and low-speed elevators.[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) The designers chose for double-deck elevators in order to minimize the number of elevator shafts. The offices, apartments, and

The strong winds are not felt on those terraces because of the chiseled [parapets](/wiki/Parapet).[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre has 95 elevators made by [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi), including two elevators that are the world's fastest. In 2014, Hitachi announced that those two elevators would be able to reach 1,200 m/min (3,900 ft/min) and would carry passengers through the 440-metre (1,450 ft) long shaft from the first to the 95th floor in approximately 43 seconds.[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10) These two elevators are used to bring hotel guests from the entrance to the lobby. In May 2016, the elevators were first tested and reached the promised maximum speed.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In June 2017, the elevators were tested after having modified them to be able to deliver an even faster speed above the rated speed of 20 m/s, and they managed to reach a top speed of 21 m/s (69 ft/s).[[27]](#cite_note-27) This exceeded the top speed [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)'s elevators could deliver which was a top speed of 20.5 m/s (67 ft/s),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) making the lifts within the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre the world's fastest. In September 2019, the elevator received a [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records) title as the world's fastest.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

To achieve their top speed, the elevators in the Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre use permanent magnet synchronous motors.[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) The air pressure is adjusted in order to prevent ear blockages and the elevators have rollers to smoothen the ride.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Additionally, the emergency brakes can withstand temperatures up to 300 °C (572 °F).[[32]](#cite_note-32) Besides the two fastest elevators, the building has 28 [double-deck elevators](/wiki/Double-deck_elevator) (with speeds of 150–450 m/min.), 13 high-speed elevators (210–600 m/min.), and 52 medium- and low-speed elevators.[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) The designers chose for double-deck elevators in order to minimize the number of elevator shafts. The offices, apartments, and
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="2b82b6f75cb56285">>>
