One World Trade Center, also known as One WTC and the Freedom Tower,[[note 1]](#cite_note-15) is the main building of the [rebuilt World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present)) complex in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan), New York City. Designed by [David Childs](/wiki/David_Childs) of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), One World Trade Center is the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), the tallest [building](/wiki/Skyscraper) in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere), and the [seventh-tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). The [supertall structure](/wiki/Supertall_structure) has the same name as the [North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the [original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)), which was [destroyed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center) in the [terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks). The skyscraper stands on the northwest corner of the 16-acre (6.5 ha) [World Trade Center site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site), on the site of the original [6 World Trade Center](/wiki/6_World_Trade_Center_(1974%E2%80%932001)). It is bounded by [West Street](/wiki/West_Side_Highway) to the west, [Vesey Street](/wiki/Vesey_Street) to the north, [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Manhattan)) to the south, and [Washington Street](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Manhattan)) to the east.

The construction of below-ground utility relocations, footings, and foundations for the new building began on April 27, 2006. One World Trade Center became the [tallest structure in New York City](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City) on April 30, 2012, when it surpassed the height of the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building). The tower's steel structure was [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on August 30, 2012. On May 10, 2013, after the final component of the skyscraper's [spire](/wiki/Spire) was installed, it reached a height of 1,776 feet (541 m). Its height in feet references the signing of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence) in 1776. The building opened on November 3, 2014;[[12]](#cite_note-CNN_Money-12) the One World Observatory opened on May 29, 2015.[[13]](#cite_note-U.S._News-13)

On March 26, 2009, the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ) confirmed that the building would be officially known by its legal name of One World Trade Center, rather than its colloquial name of Freedom Tower.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) The building has 94 stories, with the top floor numbered 104.

The new World Trade Center complex will eventually include five high-rise office buildings built along [Greenwich Street](/wiki/Greenwich_Street), the [National September 11 Memorial & Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Memorial_%26_Museum), located just south of One World Trade Center where the original Twin Towers stood, and the [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub) to its east. The construction is part of an effort to memorialize and rebuild following the destruction of the original World Trade Center complex.

## History

### Original building (1971–2001)

/wiki/File:Photo_of_WTC1_(cropped).jpgThe original [One World Trade Center](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)), pictured in summer 2001
The [construction of the original World Trade Center](/wiki/Construction_of_the_World_Trade_Center) was conceived as an [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal) project and spearheaded by [David Rockefeller](/wiki/David_Rockefeller). The project was intended to help revitalize [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan).[[18]](#cite_note-Gillespie-chap1-19) The project was planned by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ), which hired architect [Minoru Yamasaki](/wiki/Minoru_Yamasaki).[[19]](#cite_note-20) The twin towers at 1 and 2 World Trade Center were designed as [framed tube structures](/wiki/Tube_(structure)), giving tenants open floor plans, unobstructed by columns or walls.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) [One World Trade Center](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) was the North Tower, and [Two World Trade Center](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) was the South Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-PBS-WTCChronology-23) Each tower was over 1,350 feet (410 m) high, and occupied about 1 acre (0.40 ha) of the total 16 acres (6.5 ha) of [the site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site)'s land.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Of the 110 stories in each tower, 8 were set aside as [mechanical floors](/wiki/Mechanical_floor). All the remaining floors were open for tenants. Each floor of the tower had 40,000 square feet (3,700 m2) of available space. The North and South tower had 3,800,000 square feet (350,000 m2) of total office space.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Construction of the North Tower began in August 1966; extensive use of prefabricated components sped up the construction process. The first tenants moved into the North Tower in October 1971.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) At the time, the original One World Trade Center became the tallest building in the world, at 1,368 feet (417 m) tall. After a 360-foot (110 m)-tall antenna was installed in 1978, the highest point of the North Tower reached 1,728 ft (527 m).[[27]](#cite_note-mcdowell1997-28) In the 1970s, four other low-level buildings were built as part of the World Trade Center complex.[[28]](#cite_note-nytimes2002-29)[[29]](#cite_note-findarticles2002-30) A [seventh](/wiki/Seven_World_Trade_Center) building was built in the mid-1980s.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) The entire complex of seven buildings had a combined total of 13,400,000 square feet (1,240,000 m2) of office space.[[28]](#cite_note-nytimes2002-29)[[29]](#cite_note-findarticles2002-30)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

At 8:46 a.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)) on [September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks), five hijackers affiliated with [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda) crashed [American Airlines Flight 11](/wiki/American_Airlines_Flight_11) into the northern facade of the North Tower.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) After burning for 102 minutes, the North Tower collapsed due to structural failure at 10:28 a.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)).[[35]](#cite_note-36) When the North Tower collapsed, debris fell on the nearby [7 World Trade Center](/wiki/7_World_Trade_Center_(1987%E2%80%932001)), which caught fire and collapsed at 5:21 p.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)).[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) Together with [a simultaneous attack](/wiki/American_Airlines_Flight_77) on [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon) in [Arlington, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia), and [a passenger revolt that resulted in a plane crash](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_93) in [Shanksville, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Shanksville,_Pennsylvania), the attacks resulted in the deaths of [2,996 people](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_September_11_attacks) (2,507 civilians, 343 [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter), 72 [law enforcement officers](/wiki/Law_enforcement_officer), 55 [military personnel](/wiki/Military_personnel), and [the 19 hijackers](/wiki/Hijackers_in_the_September_11_attacks)).[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-CBC-Winnipegger-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

### Planning

#### World Trade Center master plan

/wiki/File:WTC_Building_Arrangement_in_preliminary_site_plan.svgPreliminary site plans for the World Trade Center's reconstruction. [Comparison (background: pre-9/11, blue overlay: planned rebuild)](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c7/WTC_Building_Arrangement_and_Site_Plan_comparison.svg).
Following the destruction of the original World Trade Center, there was debate regarding the future of the World Trade Center site. There were proposals for its reconstruction almost immediately, and by 2002, the [Lower Manhattan Development Corporation](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan_Development_Corporation) had organized a competition to determine how to use the site.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The proposals were part of a larger plan to memorialize the September 11 attacks and rebuild the complex.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) Already the site was becoming a tourist attraction; in the year following the attacks the Ground Zero site became the most visited place in the United States. On September 10, 2002, the Viewing Wall, a temporary display containing information about the attacks and listing the names of the dead, opened to the public.[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45) The same year, then–New York Governor [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki) faced accusations of [cronyism](/wiki/Cronyism) for supposedly using his influence to get the winning architect's design picked as a personal favor for his friend and campaign contributor, [Ronald Lauder](/wiki/Ronald_Lauder).[[45]](#cite_note-46)

When the public rejected the first round of designs, a second, more open competition took place in December 2002, in which a design by [Daniel Libeskind](/wiki/Daniel_Libeskind) was selected as the winner in February 2003. Other designs were submitted by [Richard Meier](/wiki/Richard_Meier), [Peter Eisenman](/wiki/Peter_Eisenman), [Charles Gwathmey](/wiki/Charles_Gwathmey), and [Steven Holl](/wiki/Steven_Holl); [William Pedersen](/wiki/William_Pedersen_(architect)); and [Foster and Partners](/wiki/Foster_and_Partners).[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45) This design underwent many revisions, mainly because of disagreements with developer [Larry Silverstein](/wiki/Larry_Silverstein), who held the lease to the World Trade Center site at that time.[[46]](#cite_note-47) [Peter Walker](/wiki/Peter_Walker_(landscape_architect)) and [Michael Arad](/wiki/Michael_Arad)'s "Reflecting Absence" proposal was selected as the site's 9/11 Memorial in January 2004.[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45)

#### Tower design

The architectural firm of [Skidmore, Owings and Merrill](/wiki/SOM_(architectural_firm)) (SOM) subsequently replaced Libeskind as the new One World Trade Center's primary designer. SOM partner Ken Lewis recalled that the new building had to provide office space for various types of tenants while alluding to the Twin Towers and filling a metaphorical gap in New York City's skyline,.[[47]](#cite_note-Mafi_e764-48) [David Childs](/wiki/David_Childs) of SOM, the main architect of One World Trade Center, designed a symmetrical tower that tapered on upper floors. Childs's design contrasted with Libeskind's plans for an asymmetrical tower with an antenna at its western end; disagreements over the designs threatened to delay the project.[[48]](#cite_note-49) After Childs and Libeskind had 
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="625bdf7fde78a919">>>"
}

The [supertall structure](/wiki/Supertall_structure) has the same name as the [North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the [original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)), which was [destroyed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center) in the [terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks). The skyscraper stands on the northwest corner of the 16-acre (6.5 ha) [World Trade Center site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site), on the site of the original [6 World Trade Center](/wiki/6_World_Trade_Center_(1974%E2%80%932001)). It is bounded by [West Street](/wiki/West_Side_Highway) to the west, [Vesey Street](/wiki/Vesey_Street) to the north, [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Manhattan)) to the south, and [Washington Street](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Manhattan)) to the east.

The tower's steel structure was [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on August 30, 2012. On May 10, 2013, after the final component of the skyscraper's [spire](/wiki/Spire) was installed, it reached a height of 1,776 feet (541 m). Its height in feet references the signing of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence) in 1776. The building opened on November 3, 2014;[[12]](#cite_note-CNN_Money-12) the One World Observatory opened on May 29, 2015.[[13]](#cite_note-U.S._News-13)

On March 26, 2009, the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ) confirmed that the building would be officially known by its legal name of One World Trade Center, rather than its colloquial name of Freedom Tower.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) The building has 94 stories, with the top floor numbered 104.

The new World Trade Center complex will eventually include five high-rise office buildings built along [Greenwich Street](/wiki/Greenwich_Street), the [National September 11 Memorial & Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Memorial_%26_Museum), located just south of One World Trade Center where the original Twin Towers stood, and the [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub) to its east. The construction is part of an effort to memorialize and rebuild following the destruction of the original World Trade Center complex.

## History

### Original building (1971–2001)

/wiki/File:Photo_of_WTC1_(cropped).jpgThe original [One World Trade Center](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)), pictured in summer 2001
The [construction of the original World Trade Center](/wiki/Construction_of_the_World_Trade_Center) was conceived as an [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal) project and spearheaded by [David Rockefeller](/wiki/David_Rockefeller). The project was intended to help revitalize [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan).[[18]](#cite_note-Gillespie-chap1-19) The project was planned by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ), which hired architect [Minoru Yamasaki](/wiki/Minoru_Yamasaki).[[19]](#cite_note-20) The twin towers at 1 and 2 World Trade Center were designed as [framed tube structures](/wiki/Tube_(structure)), giving tenants open floor plans, unobstructed by columns or walls.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) [One World Trade Center](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) was the North Tower, and [Two World Trade Center](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) was the South Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-PBS-WTCChronology-23) Each tower was over 1,350 feet (410 m) high, and occupied about 1 acre (0.40 ha) of the total 16 acres (6.5 ha) of [the site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site)'s land.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Of the 110 stories in each tower, 8 were set aside as [mechanical floors](/wiki/Mechanical_floor). All the remaining floors were open for tenants. Each floor of the tower had 40,000 square feet (3,700 m2) of available space. The North and South tower had 3,800,000 square feet (350,000 m2) of total office space.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Construction of the North Tower began in August 1966; extensive use of prefabricated components sped up the construction process. The first tenants moved into the North Tower in October 1971.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) At the time, the original One World Trade Center became the tallest building in the world, at 1,368 feet (417 m) tall. After a 360-foot (110 m)-tall antenna was installed in 1978, the highest point of the North Tower reached 1,728 ft (527 m).[[27]](#cite_note-mcdowell1997-28) In the 1970s, four other low-level buildings were built as part of the World Trade Center complex.[[28]](#cite_note-nytimes2002-29)[[29]](#cite_note-findarticles2002-30) A [seventh](/wiki/Seven_World_Trade_Center) building was built in the mid-1980s.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) The entire complex of seven buildings had a combined total of 13,400,000 square feet (1,240,000 m2) of office space.[[28]](#cite_note-nytimes2002-29)[[29]](#cite_note-findarticles2002-30)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

At 8:46 a.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)) on [September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks), five hijackers affiliated with [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda) crashed [American Airlines Flight 11](/wiki/American_Airlines_Flight_11) into the northern facade of the North Tower.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) After burning for 102 minutes, the North Tower collapsed due to structural failure at 10:28 a.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)).[[35]](#cite_note-36) When the North Tower collapsed, debris fell on the nearby [7 World Trade Center](/wiki/7_World_Trade_Center_(1987%E2%80%932001)), which caught fire and collapsed at 5:21 p.m. ([EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone)).[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) Together with [a simultaneous attack](/wiki/American_Airlines_Flight_77) on [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon) in [Arlington, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia), and [a passenger revolt that resulted in a plane crash](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_93) in [Shanksville, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Shanksville,_Pennsylvania), the attacks resulted in the deaths of [2,996 people](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_September_11_attacks) (2,507 civilians, 343 [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter), 72 [law enforcement officers](/wiki/Law_enforcement_officer), 55 [military personnel](/wiki/Military_personnel), and [the 19 hijackers](/wiki/Hijackers_in_the_September_11_attacks)).[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-CBC-Winnipegger-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

### Planning

#### World Trade Center master plan

/wiki/File:WTC_Building_Arrangement_in_preliminary_site_plan.svgPreliminary site plans for the World Trade Center's reconstruction. [Comparison (background: pre-9/11, blue overlay: planned rebuild)](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c7/WTC_Building_Arrangement_and_Site_Plan_comparison.svg).
Following the destruction of the original World Trade Center, there was debate regarding the future of the World Trade Center site. There were proposals for its reconstruction almost immediately, and by 2002, the [Lower Manhattan Development Corporation](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan_Development_Corporation) had organized a competition to determine how to use the site.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The proposals were part of a larger plan to memorialize the September 11 attacks and rebuild the complex.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) Already the site was becoming a tourist attraction; in the year following the attacks the Ground Zero site became the most visited place in the United States. On September 10, 2002, the Viewing Wall, a temporary display containing information about the attacks and listing the names of the dead, opened to the public.[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45) The same year, then–New York Governor [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki) faced accusations of [cronyism](/wiki/Cronyism) for supposedly using his influence to get the winning architect's design picked as a personal favor for his friend and campaign contributor, [Ronald Lauder](/wiki/Ronald_Lauder).[[45]](#cite_note-46)

When the public rejected the first round of designs, a second, more open competition took place in December 2002, in which a design by [Daniel Libeskind](/wiki/Daniel_Libeskind) was selected as the winner in February 2003. Other designs were submitted by [Richard Meier](/wiki/Richard_Meier), [Peter Eisenman](/wiki/Peter_Eisenman), [Charles Gwathmey](/wiki/Charles_Gwathmey), and [Steven Holl](/wiki/Steven_Holl); [William Pedersen](/wiki/William_Pedersen_(architect)); and [Foster and Partners](/wiki/Foster_and_Partners).[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45) This design underwent many revisions, mainly because of disagreements with developer [Larry Silverstein](/wiki/Larry_Silverstein), who held the lease to the World Trade Center site at that time.[[46]](#cite_note-47) [Peter Walker](/wiki/Peter_Walker_(landscape_architect)) and [Michael Arad](/wiki/Michael_Arad)'s "Reflecting Absence" proposal was selected as the site's 9/11 Memorial in January 2004.[[44]](#cite_note-Dupré_2016-45)

#### Tower design

The architectural firm of [Skidmore, Owings and Merrill](/wiki/SOM_(architectural_firm)) (SOM) subsequently replaced Libeskind as the new One World Trade Center's primary designer. SOM partner Ken Lewis recalled that the new building had to provide office space for various types of tenants while alluding to the Twin Towers and filling a metaphorical gap in New York City's skyline,.[[47]](#cite_note-Mafi_e764-48) [David Childs](/wiki/David_Childs) of SOM, the main architect of One World Trade Center, designed a symmetrical tower that tapered on upper floors. Childs's design contrasted with Libeskind's plans for an asymmetrical tower with an antenna at its western end; disagreements over the designs threatened to delay the project.[[48]](#cite_note-49) After Childs and Libeskind had 
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="625bdf7fde78a919">>>

CITIC Tower
中国尊

[IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)[[ʈʂʊ́ŋ.kwǒ tswə́n]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)

CITIC Tower
[Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters)中信集团总部大楼
[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)中信集團總部大樓
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Zhōngxìn Jítuán Zǒngbù Dàlóu

CITIC Tower, also known as China Zun,[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in the [Central Business District](/wiki/Beijing_central_business_district) of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing), China. The 109-story, 528-metre (1,732 ft) building constructed by China Construction Third Engineering Bureau is the [tallest in the city](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Beijing), surpassing the [China World Trade Center Tower III](/wiki/China_World_Trade_Center_Tower_III) by 190 metres (620 ft).[[3]](#cite_note-Xinhua-4) On 18 August 2016, CITIC Tower surpassed [China World Trade Center Tower III](/wiki/China_World_Trade_Center_Tower_III) in height, becoming Beijing's tallest building.[[4]](#cite_note-ECNS-5) The tower structurally topped out on 9 July 2017,[[5]](#cite_note-6) fully topped out on 18 August 2017, and was completed in late 2018, making CITIC Tower the tallest completed building of 2018.[[6]](#cite_note-7) As of 2024, it is the tallest building with a rooftop helipad in the world.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The nickname "China Zun" comes from the [zun](/wiki/Zun), an ancient Chinese wine vessel which inspired the building design, according to the developers, the [CITIC Group](/wiki/CITIC_Group). The groundbreaking ceremony of the building took place in Beijing on 19 September 2011, and the constructors expected to finish the project within five years. CITIC Tower is [Northern China](/wiki/North_China)'s third-tallest building after [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117) and [Chow Tai Fook Binhai Center](/wiki/Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre) in [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin).

[Farrells](/wiki/Farrells) produced the tower's land bid concept design, with [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) assuming the project and completing a 14-month-long concept design process after the client had won the bid.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

China Zun is a mixed-use building, featuring 60 floors of office space, 20 floors of luxury apartments and 20 floors of hotel with 300 rooms.[[8]](#cite_note-9) There will be a rooftop garden on the top floor at 522 m (1,713 ft) high.

The tower is likely to remain the tallest building in Beijing for the foreseeable future, as in 2018 authorities capped new projects in the central business district to a height of no more than 180 m (590 ft) in a bid to reduce congestion.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

In April 2018, Hong Kong newspaper [Ming Pao](/wiki/Ming_Pao) reported that China Zun's top three floors, Levels 106–107 and an observatory on level 108, was to be expropriated by the national-security apparatus, since the entire [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai) complex, the headquarters of the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party) and the [State Council of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China), could be seen from the top of the skyscraper with the naked eye on a clear day. It is also said[[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions)] that with high-end [telescopes](/wiki/Telescope) and other monitoring equipment, the day-to-day lives and activities of the Party and State Leaders could be seen.[[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement) – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:China_Zun#Dubious)] The building was ordered to be rectified for "fire safety issues" by the authorities, but the CITIC Group was unable to reveal the specific reason for rectification. It is also said the top three floors of the building will be administered by the National Security authorities after rectification.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

- /wiki/File:Kina_Zun_2016-09-14.jpg
 CITIC Tower under construction in 2016.

- /wiki/File:China_Zun_skyscraper_under_construction_in_November_2017.jpg
 China Zun skyscraper under construction in November 2017.

- /wiki/File:China_Zun_and_Samsung_China_Headquarters.jpg
 China Zun skyscraper (right) under construction in April 2018.

The building has a total of seven office areas. Among them, Areas 1–3, Area 5 and Area 7 are occupied by China CITIC Bank, Alibaba and CITIC Group respectively, becoming the main tenants of China Zun; the remaining office areas are leased to [Fortune Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500) Enterprises and financial institutions, including [China Construction Bank](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

- [^](#cite_ref-10) ["Beijing's skyscraper arms race cut short by building height cap"](https://www.scmp.com/property/hong-kong-china/article/2142138/beijings-skyscraper-arms-race-draws-close-thanks-new-height). 18 April 2018.

- [^](#cite_ref-11) ["Zhongnanhai 'in crosshairs' of tallest building in Beijing"](https://asiatimes.com/article/zhongnanhai-in-crosshairs-of-tallest-building-in-beijing/). [Asia Times](/wiki/Asia_Times). May 2, 2018. Retrieved May 9, 2018.

- [^](#cite_ref-12) ["港媒：北京第一高楼"中国尊"可窥中南海 国安已接管顶楼三层"](https://www.zaobao.com.sg/realtime/china/story20180430-855017). [Lianhe Zaobao](/wiki/Lianhe_Zaobao) (in Chinese (Singapore)). 2018-04-30. Retrieved 2018-05-09.

China Zun is a mixed-use building, featuring 60 floors of office space, 20 floors of luxury apartments and 20 floors of hotel with 300 rooms.[[8]](#cite_note-9) There will be a rooftop garden on the top floor at 522 m (1,713 ft) high.

The tower is likely to remain the tallest building in Beijing for the foreseeable future, as in 2018 authorities capped new projects in the central business district to a height of no more than 180 m (590 ft) in a bid to reduce congestion.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

In April 2018, Hong Kong newspaper [Ming Pao](/wiki/Ming_Pao) reported that China Zun's top three floors, Levels 106–107 and an observatory on level 108, was to be expropriated by the national-security apparatus, since the entire [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai) complex, the headquarters of the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party) and the [State Council of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China), could be seen from the top of the skyscraper with the naked eye on a clear day. It is also said[[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions)] that with high-end [telescopes](/wiki/Telescope) and other monitoring equipment, the day-to-day lives and activities of the Party and State Leaders could be seen.[[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement) – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:China_Zun#Dubious)] The building was ordered to be rectified for "fire safety issues" by the authorities, but the CITIC Group was unable to reveal the specific reason for rectification. It is also said the top three floors of the building will be administered by the National Security authorities after rectification.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

- /wiki/File:Kina_Zun_2016-09-14.jpg
 CITIC Tower under construction in 2016.

- /wiki/File:China_Zun_skyscraper_under_construction_in_November_2017.jpg
 China Zun skyscraper under construction in November 2017.

- /wiki/File:China_Zun_and_Samsung_China_Headquarters.jpg
 China Zun skyscraper (right) under construction in April 2018.

The building has a total of seven office areas. Among them, Areas 1–3, Area 5 and Area 7 are occupied by China CITIC Bank, Alibaba and CITIC Group respectively, becoming the main tenants of China Zun; the remaining office areas are leased to [Fortune Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500) Enterprises and financial institutions, including [China Construction Bank](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International Commerce Centre
環球貿易廣場

/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_(Hong_Kong)_logo.jpg
/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_201008.jpg
Interactive map of the International Commerce Centre area

General information
StatusCompleted
Type[Commercial offices](/wiki/Commercial_office), [hotel](/wiki/Hotel)
Location1 [Austin Road West](/wiki/Austin_Road_West), [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon), Hong Kong
Construction started2002; 24 years ago
Completed2010; 16 years ago[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)

Height
Architectural484 m (1,588 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)

Technical details
Floor count108 above ground[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Design and construction
Architect[Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Developer[Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Structural engineer[Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Main contractorChina State Construction Engineering Corporation;[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1) Sanfield Building Contractors Limited [[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)

The International Commerce Centre ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): 環球貿易廣場; [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping): waan4kau4 mau6jik6 gwong2coeng4) is a 108-storey, 484 m (1,588 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [West Kowloon](/wiki/West_Kowloon), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong), resting atop the [Elements](/wiki/Elements,_Hong_Kong) mall and near two [MTR](/wiki/MTR) Stations ([Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon_station_(MTR)) and [Austin Station](/wiki/Austin_Station_(MTR))). It is [the world's 13th tallest building by height](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), [10th tallest by number of floors](/wiki/List_of_buildings_with_100_floors_or_more), and [Hong Kong's tallest](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Hong_Kong), as well as the only building in the city with over 100 storeys. The official height is 484 m (1,588 ft), which includes the 6 m (20 ft) tall [parapets](/wiki/Parapet) on the roof.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was the [fourth tallest building in Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Asia) and also the fourth tallest building in the world when completed in 2010.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_in_Asia.jpgInternational Commerce Centre compared with other tallest buildings in Asia.
The south side of the building faces [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

The height had been scaled back from earlier plans due to regulations that did not allow buildings to be taller than the surrounding mountains. The original proposal for this building was called Kowloon Station Phase 7 and it was designed to be 574 m (1,883 ft) tall with 102 floors.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The tower was designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates) (KPF) in association with [Wong & Ouyang](/wiki/Wong_%26_Ouyang). It was built by [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Sanfield building constructor limited, a construction subsidiary of [Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Construction work was temporarily halted[[6]](#cite_note-6) on 13 September 2009, due to a lift shaft accident that killed six workers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The top floor is numbered as "118". However, levels with "4" in the last digit were skipped because it sounds like "death" in [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese) and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese) ([tetraphobia](/wiki/Tetraphobia)), similar to Western superstition about the number 13 ([triskaidekaphobia](/wiki/Triskaidekaphobia)). As a result, the remaining storeys' floor numbers are also increased; e.g. the top floor of the 68-storey building, [The Cullinan](/wiki/The_Cullinan), is numbered 93.

Except for level 3, 103 and 113, floor numbers with "3" in the last digit were also skipped, floor numbers 5, 6, 7, 26, 28, 29 and 105 were skipped as well for unknown reasons. They are currently replaced by floor numbers with "M" & "R", which stand for "Mechanical" and "Refuge". Although the floor numbers are missing, they still show on the elevator's screen of Sky100 and Skydining 101 while going up and down.

28 floor numbers were skipped: 4, 5, 6, 7, 13, 14, 23, 24, 26, 28, 29, 33, 34, 43, 44, 53, 54, 63, 64, 73, 74, 83, 84, 93, 94, 104, 105, 114

18 floor numbers were added: UG, M1-1, M1-2, M1-3, M1-5, R1, R2, M2-1, M2-2, R3, M3-1, M3-2, R4, M4-1, M4-2, M4-3, M5, M6

## Sky100 & Skydining 101
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=3)]

An elevator ascending at speeds of 32 kilometres per hour (20 mph)[[8]](#cite_note-8) for 60-seconds[[9]](#cite_note-9) goes to the 393-metre high indoor observation deck [Sky100](/wiki/Sky100), on level 100. It is the 2nd highest observation deck in Hong Kong, after outdoor Sky Terrace 428 on [The Peak Tower](/wiki/The_Peak_Tower).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) It opens from 1000 to 2030 daily (last entry at 2000), but depends on the weather and sometimes for private only,[[12]](#cite_note-12) the admission fee of aged 12 to 64 is $198. The Hong Kong action film, [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War_(2012_film)), which stars [Aaron Kwok](/wiki/Aaron_Kwok) and [Tony Leung Ka-fai](/wiki/Tony_Leung_Ka-fai) as the main character, was also filmed here in 2011.

Skydining 101 (Inakaya, Odyssée, The Sky Boss and The Kitin) are on level 101, at 399 m (1,309 ft).

- /wiki/File:Sky100-wikimania2013-1.jpg
 Sky100

- /wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_Lift_Lobby_Overview_2008.jpg
 Office lobby with the passage to Skydining 101

## The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=4)]

A [five-star hotel](/wiki/Hotel_rating), [The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong) occupies floors 102 to 118. According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the "world's highest swimming pool in a building" is on the 118th floor at 468.8 meters.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The 2,800 m2 (30,000 sq ft) Presidential Suite, which costs 100,000 HKD per night, is on the 117th floor. The hotel's arrival lobby is on the 9th floor where guest are greeted by receptionists and taken to express elevators. The express elevators take guests 425 m (1,394 ft) above the ground in 50 seconds to the main lobby on the 103rd floor. Guest keycards are required to use the hotel elevators to access the hotel rooms on floors 104-117 and the swimming pool and gym on floor 118. An exclusive Club lounge for guests staying in club rooms and suites is located on floor 116 along with the spa. Three restaurants, Tosca, an Italian restaurant, a Chinese restaurant and the main restaurant are all located one floor below reception on floor 102.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## The ICC Light and Music Show
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=5)]

The LED [light show](/wiki/Laser_lighting_display) set a new [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Record) for the “largest light and sound show on a single building” using a total of 50,000 m2 on two facades of the ICC.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Show is designed by the lighting design supervisor, Hirohito Totsune,[[16]](#cite_note-16) who already designed the lighting system of the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree).[[17]](#cite_note-17) It creates a theme and story line by using lights and music elements, similar to "[A Symphony of Lights](/wiki/A_Symphony_of_Lights)" in [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

All-day:

[Mass Transit Railway](/wiki/Mass_Transit_Railway) (MTR): /wiki/Airport_Express_(MTR) [Airport Express](/wiki/Airport_Express_(MTR)) [Tung Chung line](/wiki/Tung_Chung_line) /wiki/MTR [Kowloon station](/wiki/Kowloon_station_(MTR)) / [Tuen Ma line](/wiki/Tuen_Ma_line) /wiki/MTR [Austin station](/wiki/Austin_station_(MTR)) / /wiki/China_Railway_High-speed [High-speed rail](/wiki/Guangzhou%E2%80%93Shenzhen%E2%80%93Hong_Kong_Express_Rail_Link) /wiki/MTR [Hong Kong West Kowloon station](/wiki/Hong_Kong_West_Kowloon_station)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International Commerce Centre
環球貿易廣場

/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_(Hong_Kong)_logo.jpg
/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_201008.jpg
Interactive map of the International Commerce Centre area

General information
StatusCompleted
Type[Commercial offices](/wiki/Commercial_office), [hotel](/wiki/Hotel)
Location1 [Austin Road West](/wiki/Austin_Road_West), [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon), Hong Kong
Construction started2002; 24 years ago
Completed2010; 16 years ago[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
ManagementHarbour Vantage[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Height
Architectural484 m (1,588 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Tip484 m (1,588 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Technical details
Floor count108 above ground[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Design and construction
Architect[Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Developer[Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Structural engineer[Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Main contractorChina State Construction Engineering Corporation;[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1) Sanfield Building Contractors Limited [[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)

The International Commerce Centre ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): 環球貿易廣場; [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping): waan4kau4 mau6jik6 gwong2coeng4) is a 108-storey, 484 m (1,588 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [West Kowloon](/wiki/West_Kowloon), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong), resting atop the [Elements](/wiki/Elements,_Hong_Kong) mall and near two [MTR](/wiki/MTR) Stations ([Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon_station_(MTR)) and [Austin Station](/wiki/Austin_Station_(MTR))). It is [the world's 13th tallest building by height](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), [10th tallest by number of floors](/wiki/List_of_buildings_with_100_floors_or_more), and [Hong Kong's tallest](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Hong_Kong), as well as the only building in the city with over 100 storeys. The official height is 484 m (1,588 ft), which includes the 6 m (20 ft) tall [parapets](/wiki/Parapet) on the roof.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was the [fourth tallest building in Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Asia) and also the fourth tallest building in the world when completed in 2010.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_in_Asia.jpgInternational Commerce Centre compared with other tallest buildings in Asia.
The south side of the building faces [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

The height had been scaled back from earlier plans due to regulations that did not allow buildings to be taller than the surrounding mountains. The original proposal for this building was called Kowloon Station Phase 7 and it was designed to be 574 m (1,883 ft) tall with 102 floors.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The tower was designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates) (KPF) in association with [Wong & Ouyang](/wiki/Wong_%26_Ouyang). It was built by [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Sanfield building constructor limited, a construction subsidiary of [Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Construction work was temporarily halted[[6]](#cite_note-6) on 13 September 2009, due to a lift shaft accident that killed six workers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The top floor is numbered as "118". However, levels with "4" in the last digit were skipped because it sounds like "death" in [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese) and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese) ([tetraphobia](/wiki/Tetraphobia)), similar to Western superstition about the number 13 ([triskaidekaphobia](/wiki/Triskaidekaphobia)). As a result, the remaining storeys' floor numbers are also increased; e.g. the top floor of the 68-storey building, [The Cullinan](/wiki/The_Cullinan), is numbered 93.

Except for level 3, 103 and 113, floor numbers with "3" in the last digit were also skipped, floor numbers 5, 6, 7, 26, 28, 29 and 105 were skipped as well for unknown reasons. They are currently replaced by floor numbers with "M" & "R", which stand for "Mechanical" and "Refuge". Although the floor numbers are missing, they still show on the elevator's screen of Sky100 and Skydining 101 while going up and down.

28 floor numbers were skipped: 4, 5, 6, 7, 13, 14, 23, 24, 26, 28, 29, 33, 34, 43, 44, 53, 54, 63, 64, 73, 74, 83, 84, 93, 94, 104, 105, 114

18 floor numbers were added: UG, M1-1, M1-2, M1-3, M1-5, R1, R2, M2-1, M2-2, R3, M3-1, M3-2, R4, M4-1, M4-2, M4-3, M5, M6

## Sky100 & Skydining 101
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=3)]

An elevator ascending at speeds of 32 kilometres per hour (20 mph)[[8]](#cite_note-8) for 60-seconds[[9]](#cite_note-9) goes to the 393-metre high indoor observation deck [Sky100](/wiki/Sky100), on level 100. It is the 2nd highest observation deck in Hong Kong, after outdoor Sky Terrace 428 on [The Peak Tower](/wiki/The_Peak_Tower).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) It opens from 1000 to 2030 daily (last entry at 2000), but depends on the weather and sometimes for private only,[[12]](#cite_note-12) the admission fee of aged 12 to 64 is $198. The Hong Kong action film, [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War_(2012_film)), which stars [Aaron Kwok](/wiki/Aaron_Kwok) and [Tony Leung Ka-fai](/wiki/Tony_Leung_Ka-fai) as the main character, was also filmed here in 2011.

Skydining 101 (Inakaya, Odyssée, The Sky Boss and The Kitin) are on level 101, at 399 m (1,309 ft).

## The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=4)]

A [five-star hotel](/wiki/Hotel_rating), [The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong) occupies floors 102 to 118. According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the "world's highest swimming pool in a building" is on the 118th floor at 468.8 meters.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The 2,800 m2 (30,000 sq ft) Presidential Suite, which costs 100,000 HKD per night, is on the 117th floor. The hotel's arrival lobby is on the 9th floor where guest are greeted by receptionists and taken to express elevators. The express elevators take guests 425 m (1,394 ft) above the ground in 50 seconds to the main lobby on the 103rd floor. Guest keycards are required to use the hotel elevators to access the hotel rooms on floors 104-117 and the swimming pool and gym on floor 118. An exclusive Club lounge for guests staying in club rooms and suites is located on floor 116 along with the spa. Three restaurants, Tosca, an Italian restaurant, a Chinese restaurant and the main restaurant are all located one floor below reception on floor 102.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## The ICC Light and Music Show
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Commerce_Centre&action=edit&section=5)]

The LED [light show](/wiki/Laser_lighting_display) set a new [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Record) for the “largest light and sound show on a single building” using a total of 50,000 m2 on two facades of the ICC.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Show is designed by the lighting design supervisor, Hirohito Totsune,[[16]](#cite_note-16) who already designed the lighting system of the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree).[[17]](#cite_note-17) It creates a theme and story line by using lights and music elements, similar to "[A Symphony of Lights](/wiki/A_Symphony_of_Lights)" in [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

Central Park Tower is a residential [supertall](/wiki/Supertall) skyscraper at 217 West [57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_(Manhattan)), along [Billionaires' Row](/wiki/Billionaires%27_Row), in the [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan) neighborhood of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City), New York, United States. Designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture), the building rises 1,550 feet (472.4 m) with 98 above-ground stories and three basement stories, although the top story is numbered 136. Central Park Tower is the [second-tallest building in New York City](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City) (behind [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center)), [the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), and the Western Hemisphere; the [15th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings); the [tallest primarily residential building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_residential_buildings); and the tallest building outside Asia by roof height.

Central Park Tower was developed by [Extell Development Company](/wiki/Extell_Development_Company) and [Shanghai Municipal Investment Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Investment_Group). The basement and first five above-ground stories contain a large [Nordstrom](/wiki/Nordstrom) store, which opened in 2019. The eastern portion of the tower contains a [cantilever](/wiki/Cantilever) above the [Art Students League of New York](/wiki/Art_Students_League_of_New_York)'s building at [215 West 57th Street](/wiki/American_Fine_Arts_Society), intended to maximize views of nearby [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park). The residential portion of the tower contains 179 condominiums, spanning on average 5,000 ft2 (460 m2), with interiors designed by [Rottet Studio](/wiki/Rottet_Studio). There are also amenities spaces on floors 14 through 16 as well as a private club on floor 100.

The site of Central Park Tower was assembled during the first decade of the 21st century; during the acquisition process, the tower was delayed after two buildings at 225 West 57th Street and 1780 Broadway were considered for [New York City landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission) status. Despite uncertainty about the final design and complications relating to financing, excavations at the site started in May 2014 and above-ground construction started in early 2015. There were several incidents and controversies during the building's construction, including a controversy over the tower's cantilever and the death of a security guard. The building was [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) during September 2019, and completed in 2020. In total, Central Park Tower cost $3 billion to construct.[[3]](#cite_note-NBC_New_York_2019_i279-4)

Central Park Tower is in the [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan) neighborhood of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City), United States. It is on the east side of [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan)) between [57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_(Manhattan)) to the south and 58th Street to the north. It is one block south of [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park) and [Columbus Circle](/wiki/Columbus_Circle). The northwestern corner of the building abuts [1790 Broadway](/wiki/1790_Broadway), while the southwestern corner is adjacent to 1776 Broadway. Central Park Tower is also near [240 Central Park South](/wiki/240_Central_Park_South) and [220 Central Park South](/wiki/220_Central_Park_South) to the north; the [American Fine Arts Society](/wiki/American_Fine_Arts_Society) (also known as the [Art Students League of New York](/wiki/Art_Students_League_of_New_York) building) and [the Osborne](/wiki/The_Osborne) apartment building to the east; and [224 West 57th Street](/wiki/224_West_57th_Street) and [218 West 57th Street](/wiki/218_West_57th_Street) (the former [American Society of Civil Engineers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Civil_Engineers) clubhouse) to the south.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Central Park Tower is one of several major developments around 57th Street and Central Park that are collectively dubbed [Billionaires' Row](/wiki/Billionaires%27_Row) by the media. Other buildings along Billionaires' Row include [432 Park Avenue](/wiki/432_Park_Avenue) four blocks east, [111 West 57th Street](/wiki/111_West_57th_Street) and [One57](/wiki/One57) one block east, and the adjacent 220 Central Park South.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-Vanity_Fair-7)

In the 20th century, the area was part of Manhattan's "[Automobile Row](/wiki/Automobile_Row)", a stretch of [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan)) extending mainly between [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square) at [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan)) and [Sherman Square](/wiki/Sherman_Square) at [72nd Street](/wiki/72nd_Street_(Manhattan)).[[7]](#cite_note-8) Before the first decade of the 20th century, the area was occupied mostly by [horse](/wiki/Horse)-related industries and was described by [The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times) as "thoroughly lifeless".[[8]](#cite_note-nyt19070106-9) By 1907, the Times characterized this section of Broadway as having "almost a solid line of motor vehicle signs all the way from Times Square to Sherman Square".[[8]](#cite_note-nyt19070106-9) In the late 1900s and early 1910s, several large automobile showrooms, stores, and garages were built on Broadway, including the [United States Rubber Company](/wiki/United_States_Rubber_Company) building at [5 Columbus Circle](/wiki/5_Columbus_Circle) and the Demarest and [Peerless](/wiki/Peerless_Motor_Company) Company showrooms at [224 West 57th Street](/wiki/224_West_57th_Street).[[9]](#cite_note-nyt19090221-10)

On the western side of the building's base is the 12-story facade of the former 1780 Broadway, which formerly contained the [B. F. Goodrich Company](/wiki/B._F._Goodrich_Company) showroom.[[10]](#cite_note-monster-11)[[11]](#cite_note-Horsley_2009-12)[[12]](#cite_note-nyt20081116-13) There was also an 8-story building at 225 West 57th Street,[[12]](#cite_note-nyt20081116-13)[[13]](#cite_note-NYCL_p._5-14) which contained the [Stoddard-Dayton](/wiki/Stoddard-Dayton) showroom.[[11]](#cite_note-Horsley_2009-12)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Both structures were built in 1909[[9]](#cite_note-nyt19090221-10) to designs by [Howard Van Doren Shaw](/wiki/Howard_Van_Doren_Shaw),[[15]](#cite_note-Barron_2011-16)[[13]](#cite_note-NYCL_p._5-14) and they shared a [freight elevator](/wiki/Freight_elevator).[[16]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._28-17) 1780 Broadway was one of the most visible buildings on Automobile Row because of the site's elevated topography,[[13]](#cite_note-NYCL_p._5-14) and it was leased to various automotive firms during the early 20th century.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Goodrich came to own both 1780 Broadway and 225 West 57th Street;[[16]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._28-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Clark_1928-19) when the company sold the structures in 1928, they were collectively called the "Goodrich Building".[[18]](#cite_note-Clark_1928-19) A single-story annex was built for the Lincoln Art Theatre in 1962–1964, and the Goodrich Building became a [supermarket](/wiki/Supermarket) in the 1990s before being acquired by Central Park Tower's developer, the [Extell Development Company](/wiki/Extell_Development_Company), in 2006.[[19]](#cite_note-NYCL_p._7-20) In addition, the [Hard Rock Cafe](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Cafe) and [Broadway Dance Center](/wiki/Broadway_Dance_Center) occupied the structure at 221 West 57th Street before that building was purchased by Extell, also in 2006.[[10]](#cite_note-monster-11)

Central Park Tower was developed jointly by Extell and [Shanghai Municipal Investment Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Investment_Group).[[20]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) It was designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture),[[22]](#cite_note-Dezeen_2019-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) with [AAI Architects](/wiki/Adamson_Associates) as the [architect of record](/wiki/Architect_of_record). [WSP Global](/wiki/WSP_Global) was the structural engineer for the project, while [Lendlease](/wiki/Lendlease) was the main contractor.

The building has 98 physical above-ground floors[[24]](#cite_note-Warren_2020-25) and three basement levels.[[25]](#cite_note-Extell_pp._311-317-26) The top story is numbered 136,[[2]](#cite_note-Extell_p._317-2) and several floor numbers are skipped.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[b]](#cite_note-marketing_floors-28) The tallest habitable story is 1,417 feet (432 m) high, while the roof and architectural tip are 1,550 feet (470 m) above ground level.[[27]](#cite_note-Emporis-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) The use of [development rights](/wiki/Air_rights) transferred from other structures nearby, as well as about 300 feet (91 m) of mechanical spaces, enabled Central Park Tower to reach a taller height than would have ordinarily been allowed.[[29]](#cite_note-nyt20190614-31) Central Park Tower is the [tallest primarily residential building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_residential_buildings); the much taller [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) has 900 residential units but is mixed-use.[[30]](#cite_note-32) Central Park Tower is also the [second-tallest skyscraper in the United States and the Western Hemisphere](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), the [15th-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), and the tallest building outside Asia by roof height.[[31]](#cite_note-33) The building's top floor and roof height are higher than that of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan). Due to its high [slenderness ratio](/wiki/Slenderness_ratio), the building has been characterized as part of a new breed of New York City "[pencil towers](/wiki/Pencil_tower)".[[32]](#cite_note-34)

/wiki/File:W_57th_St_Dec_2020_39.jpgCantilever over the [American Fine Arts Society](/wiki/American_Fine_Arts_Society), headquarters of the Art Students League of New York
The facade of Central Park Tower was designed by James Carpenter Design Associates and Permasteelisa Group. According to Gill and Smith, the glass cladding was intended to help the tower fit into the skyline.[[33]](#cite_note-The_Architects_Newspaper_2019-35) The entrance for Nordstrom is on 57th Street, as is the main residential entrance. A secondary residents' entrance is on 58th Street.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-Extell_pp._324-325-37)

To maximize views of Central Park, Extell built a [cantilever](/wiki/Cantilever) extending 28 feet (8.5 m) from the eastern side of the tower.[[36]](#cite_note-nyt202106042-38)The cantilever starts roughly 290 feet (88 m) above the Art Students League building[[36]](#cite_note-nyt202106042-38)[[37]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._194-39) and covers roughly one-third of the total space above that building.[[38]](#cite_note-nyt20140923-40) Without the cantilever, [Vornado Realty Trust](/wiki/Vornado_Realty_Trust)'s under-construction [220 Central Park South](/wiki/220_Central_Park_South) would have blocked the lowest 950 feet (290 m) of the tower.[[39]](#cite_note-nyt20131015-41) The Art Students League building itself could not be modified because it was a [New York City designated landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_designated_landmark) and listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places).[[37]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._194-39) The cantilever was constructed in conjunction with an [air rights](/wiki/Air_rights) sale in 2013, which shifted 220 Central Park South slightly west. This agreement, in turn, ended a years-long dispute over the site of 220 Central Park South.[[39]](#cite_note-nyt20131015-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[6]](#cite_note-Vanity_Fair-7) Since 2019, Central Park Tower has held the [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records) for being the tallest cantilevered building.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

The base of the tower along 57th and 58th Streets is made of glass, arranged into fluted panels that are insulated and laminated. The panels are installed in a serpentine "wave" pattern.[[42]](#cite_note-Surface_Design_2019-44)[[43]](#cite_note-www.permasteelisagroup.com-45)[[44]](#cite_note-Gannon_2019-46) These glass panels have a total combined surface area of 52,700 square feet (4,900 m2).[[43]](#cite_note-www.permasteelisagroup.com-45) The panels a
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="67b089e897df7648">>>"
}

Central Park Tower was developed jointly by Extell and [Shanghai Municipal Investment Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Investment_Group).[[20]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) It was designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture),[[22]](#cite_note-Dezeen_2019-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) with [AAI Architects](/wiki/Adamson_Associates) as the [architect of record](/wiki/Architect_of_record). [WSP Global](/wiki/WSP_Global) was the structural engineer for the project, while [Lendlease](/wiki/Lendlease) was the main contractor.

The building has 98 physical above-ground floors[[24]](#cite_note-Warren_2020-25) and three basement levels.[[25]](#cite_note-Extell_pp._311-317-26) The top story is numbered 136,[[2]](#cite_note-Extell_p._317-2) and several floor numbers are skipped.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[b]](#cite_note-marketing_floors-28) The tallest habitable story is 1,417 feet (432 m) high, while the roof and architectural tip are 1,550 feet (470 m) above ground level.[[27]](#cite_note-Emporis-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) The use of [development rights](/wiki/Air_rights) transferred from other structures nearby, as well as about 300 feet (91 m) of mechanical spaces, enabled Central Park Tower to reach a taller height than would have ordinarily been allowed.[[29]](#cite_note-nyt20190614-31) Central Park Tower is the [tallest primarily residential building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_residential_buildings); the much taller [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) has 900 residential units but is mixed-use.[[30]](#cite_note-32) Central Park Tower is also the [second-tallest skyscraper in the United States and the Western Hemisphere](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), the [15th-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), and the tallest building outside Asia by roof height.[[31]](#cite_note-33) The building's top floor and roof height are higher than that of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan). Due to its high [slenderness ratio](/wiki/Slenderness_ratio), the building has been characterized as part of a new breed of New York City "[pencil towers](/wiki/Pencil_tower)".[[32]](#cite_note-34)

/wiki/File:W_57th_St_Dec_2020_39.jpgCantilever over the [American Fine Arts Society](/wiki/American_Fine_Arts_Society), headquarters of the Art Students League of New York
The facade of Central Park Tower was designed by James Carpenter Design Associates and Permasteelisa Group. According to Gill and Smith, the glass cladding was intended to help the tower fit into the skyline.[[33]](#cite_note-The_Architects_Newspaper_2019-35) The entrance for Nordstrom is on 57th Street, as is the main residential entrance. A secondary residents' entrance is on 58th Street.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-Extell_pp._324-325-37)

To maximize views of Central Park, Extell built a [cantilever](/wiki/Cantilever) extending 28 feet (8.5 m) from the eastern side of the tower.[[36]](#cite_note-nyt202106042-38)The cantilever starts roughly 290 feet (88 m) above the Art Students League building[[36]](#cite_note-nyt202106042-38)[[37]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._194-39) and covers roughly one-third of the total space above that building.[[38]](#cite_note-nyt20140923-40) Without the cantilever, [Vornado Realty Trust](/wiki/Vornado_Realty_Trust)'s under-construction [220 Central Park South](/wiki/220_Central_Park_South) would have blocked the lowest 950 feet (290 m) of the tower.[[39]](#cite_note-nyt20131015-41) The Art Students League building itself could not be modified because it was a [New York City designated landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_designated_landmark) and listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places).[[37]](#cite_note-Clarke_p._194-39) The cantilever was constructed in conjunction with an [air rights](/wiki/Air_rights) sale in 2013, which shifted 220 Central Park South slightly west. This agreement, in turn, ended a years-long dispute over the site of 220 Central Park South.[[39]](#cite_note-nyt20131015-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[6]](#cite_note-Vanity_Fair-7) Since 2019, Central Park Tower has held the [Guinness World Record](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records) for being the tallest cantilevered building.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

The base of the tower along 57th and 58th Streets is made of glass, arranged into fluted panels that are insulated and laminated. The panels are installed in a serpentine "wave" pattern.[[42]](#cite_note-Surface_Design_2019-44)[[43]](#cite_note-www.permasteelisagroup.com-45)[[44]](#cite_note-Gannon_2019-46) These glass panels have a total combined surface area of 52,700 square feet (4,900 m2).[[43]](#cite_note-www.permasteelisagroup.com-45) The panels a
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="67b089e897df7648">>>

Merdeka 118, formerly known as Menara Warisan Merdeka,[[a]](#cite_note-13) KL 118, and PNB 118, is a 118-story [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia. At 678.9 metres (2,227 feet) tall,[[1]](#cite_note-Merdeka_118-1) it is the [second-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and [structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures) in the world, only behind the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) (Dubai, UAE), at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). Construction was controversially entirely funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad), an investment management company [owned by the Malaysian government](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise).[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) The structure was completed in November of 2023, celebrated its grand opening on 10 January 2024, but remains closed and under further construction as of 1 July 2025.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)] The Park Hyatt Kuala Lumpur Hotel, located on level 75, began operation on 7 August 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-parkhyatt-15)

The building's name, Merdeka, which means "independence" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language), is inspired by its proximity to [Stadium Merdeka](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka).[[15]](#cite_note-future-16) The spire of the building was completed in December 2022, which marked its final height of 678.9 m (2,227 ft) above ground and 700.9 m (2,300 ft) above sea level.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-17)

It is the [tallest building in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia)—surpassing the 451.9-metre (1,483 ft) [Petronas Twin Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers)—and in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), surpassing the 462-metre (1,516 ft) [Landmark 81](/wiki/Landmark_81).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18) The building will also be the first in Malaysia to receive a triple platinum rating from worldwide sustainability certifications such as Green Building Index (GBI), including [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18)

When it was completed in 2023,[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) the tower became the tallest building in Malaysia.[[20]](#cite_note-21) It was constructed in three phases and consists of 400,000 square metres (4,300,000 sq ft) of residential, hotel and commercial space.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The building is a mix of office spaces, hotels, and retail outlets and an observatory floor which will be the highest observation deck in Southeast Asia. It has two observation decks, the first inside the building and the second inside the spire, and a retail business center ([118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall)). It will be surrounded by four acres of urban and linear parks.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The non-rentable space consists of elevators, recreational and maintenance facilities, as well as parking spaces for up to 8,500 cars. Sixty out of the 80 storeys of office space will be reserved for [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB), the developer of the project, and its subsidiaries.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Merdeka 118 precinct is a 7.7-hectare (19-acre) land development funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise) of the Malaysian government.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14)

The budget of RM5 billion[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27) has faced criticism from many Malaysians, saying that the money was in essence public money and could have been better used elsewhere, such as for basic infrastructure and a raise in the minimum wage.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) In 2016, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia) [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak), who would eventually be directly involved with the [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad) scandal, claimed that the project was not a waste and it would "bring more benefits" by generating "economic opportunities".[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The building is situated on [Jalan Hang Jebat](/wiki/Jalan_Hang_Jebat), on the location of the former Merdeka Park (subsequently repurposed into an open-air car park). The site lies within the vicinity of landmarks such as [Petaling Street](/wiki/Petaling_Street), sporting venues including [Merdeka Stadium](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka), [Stadium Negara](/wiki/Stadium_Negara) and the Chin Woo Stadium, three schools (the [Victoria Institution](/wiki/Victoria_Institution), the [Methodist Boys' School](/wiki/Methodist_Boys%27_School,_Kuala_Lumpur) and the [Chinese-type Jalan Davidson Primary School](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Kuala_Lumpur)), and the stalled [Plaza Rakyat](/wiki/Plaza_Rakyat) project (across the [Ampang Line](/wiki/Ampang_Line)).[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27)

The Merdeka 118 development also has access to the newly built [Merdeka MRT station](/wiki/Merdeka_MRT_station) on the [Kajang Line](/wiki/Kajang_line) (SBK)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) and is directly linked from three major roads via the Belfield Tunnel, which is a 2-storey underground tunnel passing underneath Kampung Attap and [Jalan Maharajalela](/wiki/Jalan_Maharajalela) to the basement of the precinct.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

/wiki/File:Merdeka_1957_tunku_abdul_rahman.jpgMerdeka 118 was designed to resemble [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s stature as he proclaimed Malaysian independence on 31 August 1957, chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!" seven times.
The building is designed with a mixture of diamond-shaped glass facades to signify the diversity of Malaysians.[[4]](#cite_note-Abdelrazaq2020-4) The design was made to resemble and inspired by [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s outstretched hand gesture while chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!",[[31]](#cite_note-32) when he proclaimed the [independence of Malaya](/wiki/Independence_of_Malaya) on 31 August 1957. The building's cladding was to comprise 18,144 panels, 114,000 square-meter of glass, and 1,600 tonnes of window frame extrusions. It was to contain the [118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall), Grade-A offices, hotels, and residential areas. The structural engineers were [Leslie E. Robertson](/wiki/Leslie_E._Robertson) Associates and [Robert Bird Group](/wiki/Robert_Bird_Group) while the civil and structural engineer of record for this tower is [Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group).[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The building has been equipped and illuminated at night with 8.4 kilometres of LED light strips which would gradually move from one corner to another.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The Neapoli Group, an environmental design and engineering firm, was employed to provide consultancy services towards achieving platinum rating with three Green Building certification bodies: [Green Building Index](/w/index.php?title=Green_Building_Index&action=edit&redlink=1) (GBI), [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design](/wiki/LEED) (LEED), and GreenRE.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

/wiki/File:PNB118_Jalan_Hang_Jebat_entrance.pngConstruction of the tower's base as viewed from Jalan Hang Jebat in September 2021
The piling and foundation work for the project was awarded to Pintaras Geotechnics Sdn Bhd.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The Permodalan Nasional Berhad shortlisted six groups for various construction jobs: [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) and [UEM Group](/wiki/UEM_Group) Bhd; [IJM Corp Bhd](/wiki/IJM_Corporation), Norwest Holdings Sdn Bhd, and Shimizu Corp; [Malaysian Resources Corp Bhd](/wiki/MRCB) and State Construction Engineering Corp; WCT Bhd and Arabtec Construction LLC; TSR Capital Bhd and Daewoo Group; Seacera Group Bhd with Spaz Sdn Bhd, [Sinohydro Corp](/wiki/SinoHydro_Corp), and Shanghai Construction Group. These companies submitted their bids by 28 January 2015.[[39]](#cite_note-40) [KONE](/wiki/KONE), a Finnish group, is supplying around 87 elevators and escalators for the project.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

On 23 November 2015, PNB announced a contract worth RM3.4 billion has been awarded to the joint venture of South Korea's Samsung C&T and UEM Group Berhad.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Furthermore, on 9 November 2017, PNB planned to raise up to RM5 billion fund for its project via a green [sukuk](/wiki/Sukuk), the Merdeka Asean Green SRI Sukuk, with a 15-year tenure. The sukuk covered the development of its 83-storey office space, which forms part of the tower. It is the first adopter of the Asean Green Bond Standards launched by the [Securities Commission Malaysia](/wiki/Securities_Commission_Malaysia) th
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="b93b2bb9ea2bcc6e">>>"

Merdeka 118, formerly known as Menara Warisan Merdeka,[[a]](#cite_note-13) KL 118, and PNB 118, is a 118-story [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia. At 678.9 metres (2,227 feet) tall,[[1]](#cite_note-Merdeka_118-1) it is the [second-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and [structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures) in the world, only behind the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) (Dubai, UAE), at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). Construction was controversially entirely funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad), an investment management company [owned by the Malaysian government](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise).[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) The structure was completed in November of 2023, celebrated its grand opening on 10 January 2024, but remains closed and under further construction as of 1 July 2025.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)] The Park Hyatt Kuala Lumpur Hotel, located on level 75, began operation on 7 August 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-parkhyatt-15)

The building's name, Merdeka, which means "independence" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language), is inspired by its proximity to [Stadium Merdeka](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka).[[15]](#cite_note-future-16) The spire of the building was completed in December 2022, which marked its final height of 678.9 m (2,227 ft) above ground and 700.9 m (2,300 ft) above sea level.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-17)

It is the [tallest building in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia)—surpassing the 451.9-metre (1,483 ft) [Petronas Twin Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers)—and in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), surpassing the 462-metre (1,516 ft) [Landmark 81](/wiki/Landmark_81).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18) The building will also be the first in Malaysia to receive a triple platinum rating from worldwide sustainability certifications such as Green Building Index (GBI), including [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18)

When it was completed in 2023,[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) the tower became the tallest building in Malaysia.[[20]](#cite_note-21) It was constructed in three phases and consists of 400,000 square metres (4,300,000 sq ft) of residential, hotel and commercial space.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The building is a mix of office spaces, hotels, and retail outlets and an observatory floor which will be the highest observation deck in Southeast Asia. It has two observation decks, the first inside the building and the second inside the spire, and a retail business center ([118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall)). It will be surrounded by four acres of urban and linear parks.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The non-rentable space consists of elevators, recreational and maintenance facilities, as well as parking spaces for up to 8,500 cars. Sixty out of the 80 storeys of office space will be reserved for [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB), the developer of the project, and its subsidiaries.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Merdeka 118 precinct is a 7.7-hectare (19-acre) land development funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise) of the Malaysian government.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14)

The budget of RM5 billion[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27) has faced criticism from many Malaysians, saying that the money was in essence public money and could have been better used elsewhere, such as for basic infrastructure and a raise in the minimum wage.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) In 2016, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia) [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak), who would eventually be directly involved with the [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad) scandal, claimed that the project was not a waste and it would "bring more benefits" by generating "economic opportunities".[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The building is situated on [Jalan Hang Jebat](/wiki/Jalan_Hang_Jebat), on the location of the former Merdeka Park (subsequently repurposed into an open-air car park). The site lies within the vicinity of landmarks such as [Petaling Street](/wiki/Petaling_Street), sporting venues including [Merdeka Stadium](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka), [Stadium Negara](/wiki/Stadium_Negara) and the Chin Woo Stadium, three schools (the [Victoria Institution](/wiki/Victoria_Institution), the [Methodist Boys' School](/wiki/Methodist_Boys%27_School,_Kuala_Lumpur) and the [Chinese-type Jalan Davidson Primary School](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Kuala_Lumpur)), and the stalled [Plaza Rakyat](/wiki/Plaza_Rakyat) project (across the [Ampang Line](/wiki/Ampang_Line)).[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27)

The Merdeka 118 development also has access to the newly built [Merdeka MRT station](/wiki/Merdeka_MRT_station) on the [Kajang Line](/wiki/Kajang_line) (SBK)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) and is directly linked from three major roads via the Belfield Tunnel, which is a 2-storey underground tunnel passing underneath Kampung Attap and [Jalan Maharajalela](/wiki/Jalan_Maharajalela) to the basement of the precinct.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

/wiki/File:Merdeka_1957_tunku_abdul_rahman.jpgMerdeka 118 was designed to resemble [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s stature as he proclaimed Malaysian independence on 31 August 1957, chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!" seven times.
The building is designed with a mixture of diamond-shaped glass facades to signify the diversity of Malaysians.[[4]](#cite_note-Abdelrazaq2020-4) The design was made to resemble and inspired by [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman)'s outstretched hand gesture while chanting "[Merdeka](/wiki/Merdeka)!",[[31]](#cite_note-32) when he proclaimed the [independence of Malaya](/wiki/Independence_of_Malaya) on 31 August 1957. The building's cladding was to comprise 18,144 panels, 114,000 square-meter of glass, and 1,600 tonnes of window frame extrusions. It was to contain the [118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall), Grade-A offices, hotels, and residential areas. The structural engineers were [Leslie E. Robertson](/wiki/Leslie_E._Robertson) Associates and [Robert Bird Group](/wiki/Robert_Bird_Group) while the civil and structural engineer of record for this tower is [Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group).[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The building has been equipped and illuminated at night with 8.4 kilometres of LED light strips which would gradually move from one corner to another.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The Neapoli Group, an environmental design and engineering firm, was employed to provide consultancy services towards achieving platinum rating with three Green Building certification bodies: [Green Building Index](/w/index.php?title=Green_Building_Index&action=edit&redlink=1) (GBI), [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design](/wiki/LEED) (LEED), and GreenRE.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

The piling and foundation work for the project was awarded to Pintaras Geotechnics Sdn Bhd.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The Permodalan Nasional Berhad shortlisted six groups for various construction jobs: [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) and [UEM Group](/wiki/UEM_Group) Bhd; [IJM Corp Bhd](/wiki/IJM_Corporation), Norwest Holdings Sdn Bhd, and Shimizu Corp; [Malaysian Resources Corp Bhd](/wiki/MRCB) and State Construction Engineering Corp; WCT Bhd and Arabtec Construction LLC; TSR Capital Bhd and Daewoo Group; Seacera Group Bhd with Spaz Sdn Bhd, [Sinohydro Corp](/wiki/SinoHydro_Corp), and Shanghai Construction Group. These companies submitted their bids by 28 January 2015.[[39]](#cite_note-40) [KONE](/wiki/KONE), a Finnish group, is supplying around 87 elevators and escalators for the project.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

On 23 November 2015, PNB announced a contract worth RM3.4 billion has been awarded to the joint venture of South Korea's Samsung C&T and UEM Group Berhad.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Furthermore, on 9 November 2017, PNB planned to raise up to RM5 billion fund for its project via a green [sukuk](/wiki/Sukuk), the Merdeka Asean Green SRI Sukuk, with a 15-year tenure. The sukuk covered the development of its 83-storey office space, which forms part of the tower.
