Wuhan Greenland Center[[3]](#cite_note-3) is a 476-metre (1,562 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan), China. It is the [ninth-tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) and the [fourteenth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings).[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The tower was originally planned to be 636 metres (2,087 ft), but was redesigned mid-construction due to [airspace](/wiki/Airspace) regulations so its height does not exceed 502 metres (1,650 ft) above ground level.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The building was designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture) in conjunction with [Thornton Tomasetti](/wiki/Thornton_Tomasetti) Engineers won the design competition[[6]](#cite_note-ASGG2-6) to build the tower for [Greenland Group](/wiki/Greenland_Group), a real estate developer owned by the Shanghai city government. Construction started in 2012 and had been put on-hold numerous times following the redesign in mid-2017 ranging from financial problems, to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_mainland_China). The building finally [topped-out](/wiki/Topping_out) in late 2020 and was completed in 2022. The Wuhan Greenland Center is Central China's tallest building with a cost of US$4.5 billion, mostly due to the number of times it had been put on-hold.

The original plan for the building was to have it rise 636 metres (2,087 ft),[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) surpassing the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) by only 4.3 metres (14 ft) and the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) by 2.1 metres (7 ft), making it the second tallest man-made structure in the world. The tower was also supposed to have 126 floors, the second most of any building in the world, as well. When the Wuhan Greenland Center reached its 96th floor, construction was halted due to airspace restrictions which led to its subsequent redesign to a 476 metres (1,562 ft)[[7]](#cite_note-7) building instead of a 636 metres (2,087 ft) building. The Wuhan Greenland Center is currently the [14th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_future_tallest_buildings).

Wuhan Greenland Center

[[1]](#cite_note-skyscraper-center-1)[[2]](#cite_note-ASGG1-2)

Wuhan Greenland Center[[3]](#cite_note-3) is a 476-metre (1,562 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan), China. It is the [ninth-tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) and the [fourteenth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings).[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The tower was originally planned to be 636 metres (2,087 ft), but was redesigned mid-construction due to [airspace](/wiki/Airspace) regulations so its height does not exceed 502 metres (1,650 ft) above ground level.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The building was designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture) in conjunction with [Thornton Tomasetti](/wiki/Thornton_Tomasetti) Engineers won the design competition[[6]](#cite_note-ASGG2-6) to build the tower for [Greenland Group](/wiki/Greenland_Group), a real estate developer owned by the Shanghai city government. Construction started in 2012 and had been put on-hold numerous times following the redesign in mid-2017 ranging from financial problems, to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_mainland_China). The building finally [topped-out](/wiki/Topping_out) in late 2020 and was completed in 2022. The Wuhan Greenland Center is Central China's tallest building with a cost of US$4.5 billion, mostly due to the number of times it had been put on-hold.

The original plan for the building was to have it rise 636 metres (2,087 ft),[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) surpassing the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) by only 4.3 metres (14 ft) and the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) by 2.1 metres (7 ft), making it the second tallest man-made structure in the world. The tower was also supposed to have 126 floors, the second most of any building in the world, as well. When the Wuhan Greenland Center reached its 96th floor, construction was halted due to airspace restrictions which led to its subsequent redesign to a 476 metres (1,562 ft)[[7]](#cite_note-7) building instead of a 636 metres (2,087 ft) building. The Wuhan Greenland Center is currently the [14th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_future_tallest_buildings).

Lakhta Center
Лахта-центр

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Centre_logo_en.png
/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0_%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%80_2021.jpgThe Lakhta Center in 2021

[[3]](#cite_note-Skyscrapercenter-3)

The Lakhta Center ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language): Ла́хта-це́нтр, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian): Lahta tsentr) is an 87-story [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper)[[5]](#cite_note-5) built in the northwestern neighborhood of [Lakhta](/wiki/Lakhta,_Saint_Petersburg) in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg), [Russia](/wiki/Russia). Standing 462 metres (1,516 ft) tall, it is the [tallest building in both Russia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Russia) and [Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Europe), and the [sixteenth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings).[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is also the second-tallest structure in Russia and [Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Europe), behind the [Ostankino Tower](/wiki/Ostankino_Tower) in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow), in addition to being the [second-tallest twisted building](/wiki/List_of_twisted_buildings) and the northernmost skyscraper in the world.

Construction of the Lakhta Center started on 30 October 2012, with the building [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) on 29 January 2018.[[7]](#cite_note-installation-7) It surpassed the Vostok Tower of the [Federation Towers](/wiki/Federation_Tower) in Moscow as the tallest building in Russia and Europe on 5 October 2017. The center is designed for large-scale [mixed-use development](/wiki/Mixed-use_development), consisting of public facilities and offices. First designed by British architectural firm [RMJM](/wiki/RMJM), the project was then continued by Gorproject (2011–2017) under the main contractor, Turkish company [Rönesans Holding](/wiki/R%C3%B6nesans_Holding).

On 24 December 2018, the Lakhta Center was certified according to the criteria of ecological efficiency at [LEED](/wiki/LEED) Platinum.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In August 2021, [Gazprom](/wiki/Gazprom), formerly headquartered in Moscow, completed its re-registration process in Saint Petersburg. The new address of the company is at the Lakhta Center Multifunctional Complex.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The predecessor of the tower, the Okhta Center, was originally planned to be more centrally located in the city center of St. Petersburg. As the [historical center](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Saint_Petersburg_and_Related_Groups_of_Monuments) has been a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site) since 1990, the [World Heritage Committee](/wiki/World_Heritage_Committee) opposed the construction of the 400-meter tower as it would affect the cityscape of historic Saint Petersburg.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In December 2006 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO) World Heritage Centre Director [Francesco Bandarin](/wiki/Francesco_Bandarin) reminded Russia about its obligations to preserve it and expressed concern over the project. In 2007, the [World Monuments Fund](/wiki/World_Monuments_Fund) placed the historic skyline of St. Petersburg on its 2008 Watch List of 100 Most Endangered Sites due to the potential construction of the building.[[12]](#cite_note-BBCUNESCO-12) Due to this sizeable backlash on the original proposition, the Okhta Center, which had been planned by Gazprom to be in front of [Smolny Cathedral](/wiki/Smolny_Convent), was moved to Lakhta.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Plans for a new [metro station](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro) in the area were announced in March 2012, the station was originally included in the 1980 city development plan of Leningrad, but had not been built.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The permit for construction of the first stage of Lakhta Center, which included the skyscraper and [stylobate](/wiki/Stylobate), was obtained on 17 August 2012.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_at_night_in_December.jpgThe Lakhta Center lit at dusk

Project management is being done by [AECOM](/wiki/AECOM).[[16]](#cite_note-16) German company Josef Gartner was in charge of the glazing of the skyscraper.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Zero cycle works started on 30 October 2012. The main contractor for the construction of Lakhta Center, [Rönesans Holding](/wiki/R%C3%B6nesans_Holding), was selected on 22 April 2014.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Construction schedule
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=4)]

- March 2013 – zero cycle works are in progress. Piles are being installed. According to the schedule of construction a [diaphragm wall](/wiki/Diaphragm_wall) is to be completed by the end of April while piling works will continue until 15 August 2013.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

- April 2014 – construction of the skyscraper's foundation pit is complete.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

- June 2014 – piling is completed. 264 piles were mounted for the tower of Lakhta Center, 848 piles were mounted for the mixed-use building and entrance arch and 968 piles were mounted for the stylobate (underground parking). All in all 2080 piles were dug in.

- February and March 2015 – the pouring of the bottom slab of the skyscraper's box shaped foundation is over. 19,624 cubic meters of concrete were cast in the foundation.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

- September 2015 – all works below zero elevation are finished. The construction of the first floors of the tower core is underway.

- April 2017 – height exceeds 300 metres (980 ft), making Lakhta Center a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall) building (international classification).

- May 2017 – the skyscraper reached a height of 327 metres (1,073 ft), making it the tallest structure in St. Petersburg.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

- 5 October 2017 – Lakhta center became the tallest building in Europe as it reached a height of 374 metres (1,227 ft).[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- 29 January 2018 – the height of the tower is 462 metres (1,516 ft) and the assembly of the spire structure is complete.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In addition to the tower, the complex contains multiple other buildings. The multi-functional building (MFZ) consists of two buildings, North and South, which are united by a common foundation, stylobate and roof. The building resembles a boomerang. It has a variable number of storeys with a height difference from 7 to 17 floors, with the peak height being a little more than 80 meters. The structure is approximately 300 meters long.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The complex also has a third building,[[26]](#cite_note-26) which consists of two separate wings connected by a courtyard.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The MFZ contains a planetarium, which has a holding capacity of 140 people.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_seen_from_the_shore_of_the_Neva_Bay_in_Olgino.jpgThe Lakhta Centre seen from the shore of the Neva Bay in Olgino
/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_Jun_24.jpgSculpture the “Guardian Angel of St. Petersburg” on the 88th floor of the Lakhta Centre, a replica of the angel on top of the [Alexander Column](/wiki/Alexander_Column) in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg)
/wiki/File:Views_from_Lakhta_Center_observation_deck_16.jpgView from the Lakhta Centre observation deck

The Lakhta Centre contains offices, a [co-working](/wiki/Co-working) center, a sports center, a children's science center, and a conference center. The project includes 1,500 square metres (16,000 sq ft) of indoor exhibition space. Part of the open area will be used to demonstrate art, installations and sculptures.
There is a public observation deck at the top of the skyscraper at a height of 357 metres (1,171 ft).[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Environmental technologies
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=8)]

The design of the tower contains several green and energy-saving technologies, which earned it a [LEED](/wiki/LEED) Platinum certificate. Excess heat generated by technical equipment is used to heat the premises. The skyscraper also uses equipment with reduced noise levels along with noise suppressors, sound-proof curtain walls and [floating floors](/wiki/Floating_floor).

Due to the peculiarities of the highly humid and windy climate in the North Western region of Russia, the possibility of icing up of buildings is relatively high. In order to prevent complete icing up of the tower's spire, engineers changed glassing to metal gauze. Glass at high floors will be heated to prevent ice accumulation, ensuring good visibility.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In order to provide Lakhta Center and adjoining areas with electricity without imposing any extra burden on existing infrastructure, a new standalone power substation will be constructed.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Transportation development
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=9)]

Two traffic circles are to be built near Lakhta Center, which will become part of the М32А highway in the future.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
A light rail service from [Finland Railway Station](/wiki/Finland_Railway_Station) and a new tram line from [Primorskaya underground station](/wiki/Primorskaya_(Saint_Petersburg_Metro)) will be built to serve Lakhta Center. Plans are ongoing to build a new underground station with the working title of "Lakhta". Bicycle lanes near Lakhta Center with 90 parking places for bicycles are also planned.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Lakhta Center received the Emporis Skyscraper Awards' "Skyscraper of the Year"-award in 2020.[[36]](#cite_note-36) On 20 May 2021, the skyscraper won the facade engineering category award at the CTBUH Awards. The building also received 2 other Awards of Excellence that year: the structural engineering and geotechnical engineering awards. In October 2021, IFC Lakhta Center won the Grand Prix of Russia's largest engineering and architectural award 100 + Awards.

## Future developments
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=11)]

/wiki/File:Lakhta-2_sravnenie.pngLakhta Centre II (centre) as compared with Burj Khalifa and Lakhta Centre

In May 2021 plans were announced for a second building to be built next to Lakhta Centre called Lakhta Centre II.[[37]](#cite_note-37) If built, Lakhta Centre II would rise 703 metres (2,306 ft) and be 150 floors. It would also be the second tallest building in the world along with the tallest twisted building and the first megatall building outside Asia. As of 2023, a small yacht club has been demolished on the site of Lakhta Centre II and construction was rumoured to begin in 2024.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

In December 2021 further plans were revealed for Lakhta Centre III. As planned, Lakhta Centre III would rise 555 metres (1,821 ft).[[38]](#cite_note-38) No major news for Lakhta Centre III has been released since its initial announcement. If built, Lakhta Centre III would be the second tallest building in Europe behind Lakhta Centre II, assuming Lakhta Centre II is completed before the third tower.

Lakhta Center

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Centre_logo_en.png
/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0_%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%80_2021.jpgThe Lakhta Center in 2021

The Lakhta Center ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language): Ла́хта-це́нтр, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian): Lahta tsentr) is an 87-story [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper)[[5]](#cite_note-5) built in the northwestern neighborhood of [Lakhta](/wiki/Lakhta,_Saint_Petersburg) in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg), [Russia](/wiki/Russia). Standing 462 metres (1,516 ft) tall, it is the [tallest building in both Russia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Russia) and [Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Europe), and the [sixteenth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings).[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is also the second-tallest structure in Russia and [Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Europe), behind the [Ostankino Tower](/wiki/Ostankino_Tower) in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow), in addition to being the [second-tallest twisted building](/wiki/List_of_twisted_buildings) and the northernmost skyscraper in the world.

Construction of the Lakhta Center started on 30 October 2012, with the building [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) on 29 January 2018.[[7]](#cite_note-installation-7) It surpassed the Vostok Tower of the [Federation Towers](/wiki/Federation_Tower) in Moscow as the tallest building in Russia and Europe on 5 October 2017. The center is designed for large-scale [mixed-use development](/wiki/Mixed-use_development), consisting of public facilities and offices. First designed by British architectural firm [RMJM](/wiki/RMJM), the project was then continued by Gorproject (2011–2017) under the main contractor, Turkish company [Rönesans Holding](/wiki/R%C3%B6nesans_Holding).

On 24 December 2018, the Lakhta Center was certified according to the criteria of ecological efficiency at [LEED](/wiki/LEED) Platinum.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In August 2021, [Gazprom](/wiki/Gazprom), formerly headquartered in Moscow, completed its re-registration process in Saint Petersburg. The new address of the company is at the Lakhta Center Multifunctional Complex.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The predecessor of the tower, the Okhta Center, was originally planned to be more centrally located in the city center of St. Petersburg. As the [historical center](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Saint_Petersburg_and_Related_Groups_of_Monuments) has been a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site) since 1990, the [World Heritage Committee](/wiki/World_Heritage_Committee) opposed the construction of the 400-meter tower as it would affect the cityscape of historic Saint Petersburg.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In December 2006 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO) World Heritage Centre Director [Francesco Bandarin](/wiki/Francesco_Bandarin) reminded Russia about its obligations to preserve it and expressed concern over the project. In 2007, the [World Monuments Fund](/wiki/World_Monuments_Fund) placed the historic skyline of St. Petersburg on its 2008 Watch List of 100 Most Endangered Sites due to the potential construction of the building.[[12]](#cite_note-BBCUNESCO-12) Due to this sizeable backlash on the original proposition, the Okhta Center, which had been planned by Gazprom to be in front of [Smolny Cathedral](/wiki/Smolny_Convent), was moved to Lakhta.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Plans for a new [metro station](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro) in the area were announced in March 2012, the station was originally included in the 1980 city development plan of Leningrad, but had not been built.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The permit for construction of the first stage of Lakhta Center, which included the skyscraper and [stylobate](/wiki/Stylobate), was obtained on 17 August 2012.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_at_night_in_December.jpgThe Lakhta Center lit at dusk

Project management is being done by [AECOM](/wiki/AECOM).[[16]](#cite_note-16) German company Josef Gartner was in charge of the glazing of the skyscraper.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Zero cycle works started on 30 October 2012. The main contractor for the construction of Lakhta Center, [Rönesans Holding](/wiki/R%C3%B6nesans_Holding), was selected on 22 April 2014.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Construction schedule
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=4)]

- March 2013 – zero cycle works are in progress. Piles are being installed. According to the schedule of construction a [diaphragm wall](/wiki/Diaphragm_wall) is to be completed by the end of April while piling works will continue until 15 August 2013.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

- April 2014 – construction of the skyscraper's foundation pit is complete.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

- June 2014 – piling is completed. 264 piles were mounted for the tower of Lakhta Center, 848 piles were mounted for the mixed-use building and entrance arch and 968 piles were mounted for the stylobate (underground parking). All in all 2080 piles were dug in.

- February and March 2015 – the pouring of the bottom slab of the skyscraper's box shaped foundation is over. 19,624 cubic meters of concrete were cast in the foundation.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

- September 2015 – all works below zero elevation are finished. The construction of the first floors of the tower core is underway.

- April 2017 – height exceeds 300 metres (980 ft), making Lakhta Center a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall) building (international classification).

- May 2017 – the skyscraper reached a height of 327 metres (1,073 ft), making it the tallest structure in St. Petersburg.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

- 5 October 2017 – Lakhta center became the tallest building in Europe as it reached a height of 374 metres (1,227 ft).[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- 29 January 2018 – the height of the tower is 462 metres (1,516 ft) and the assembly of the spire structure is complete.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In addition to the tower, the complex contains multiple other buildings. The multi-functional building (MFZ) consists of two buildings, North and South, which are united by a common foundation, stylobate and roof. The building resembles a boomerang. It has a variable number of storeys with a height difference from 7 to 17 floors, with the peak height being a little more than 80 meters. The structure is approximately 300 meters long.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The complex also has a third building,[[26]](#cite_note-26) which consists of two separate wings connected by a courtyard.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The MFZ contains a planetarium, which has a holding capacity of 140 people.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_seen_from_the_shore_of_the_Neva_Bay_in_Olgino.jpgThe Lakhta Centre seen from the shore of the Neva Bay in Olgino
/wiki/File:Lakhta_Center_Jun_24.jpgSculpture the “Guardian Angel of St. Petersburg” on the 88th floor of the Lakhta Centre, a replica of the angel on top of the [Alexander Column](/wiki/Alexander_Column) in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg)
/wiki/File:Views_from_Lakhta_Center_observation_deck_16.jpgView from the Lakhta Centre observation deck

The Lakhta Centre contains offices, a [co-working](/wiki/Co-working) center, a sports center, a children's science center, and a conference center. The project includes 1,500 square metres (16,000 sq ft) of indoor exhibition space. Part of the open area will be used to demonstrate art, installations and sculptures.
There is a public observation deck at the top of the skyscraper at a height of 357 metres (1,171 ft).[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Environmental technologies
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=8)]

The design of the tower contains several green and energy-saving technologies, which earned it a [LEED](/wiki/LEED) Platinum certificate. Excess heat generated by technical equipment is used to heat the premises. The skyscraper also uses equipment with reduced noise levels along with noise suppressors, sound-proof curtain walls and [floating floors](/wiki/Floating_floor).

Due to the peculiarities of the highly humid and windy climate in the North Western region of Russia, the possibility of icing up of buildings is relatively high. In order to prevent complete icing up of the tower's spire, engineers changed glassing to metal gauze. Glass at high floors will be heated to prevent ice accumulation, ensuring good visibility.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In order to provide Lakhta Center and adjoining areas with electricity without imposing any extra burden on existing infrastructure, a new standalone power substation will be constructed.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Transportation development
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=9)]

Two traffic circles are to be built near Lakhta Center, which will become part of the М32А highway in the future.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
A light rail service from [Finland Railway Station](/wiki/Finland_Railway_Station) and a new tram line from [Primorskaya underground station](/wiki/Primorskaya_(Saint_Petersburg_Metro)) will be built to serve Lakhta Center. Plans are ongoing to build a new underground station with the working title of "Lakhta". Bicycle lanes near Lakhta Center with 90 parking places for bicycles are also planned.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Lakhta Center received the Emporis Skyscraper Awards' "Skyscraper of the Year"-award in 2020.[[36]](#cite_note-36) On 20 May 2021, the skyscraper won the facade engineering category award at the CTBUH Awards. The building also received 2 other Awards of Excellence that year: the structural engineering and geotechnical engineering awards. In October 2021, IFC Lakhta Center won the Grand Prix of Russia's largest engineering and architectural award 100 + Awards.

## Future developments
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lakhta_Center&action=edit&section=11)]

/wiki/File:Lakhta-2_sravnenie.pngLakhta Centre II (centre) as compared with Burj Khalifa and Lakhta Centre

In May 2021 plans were announced for a second building to be built next to Lakhta Centre called Lakhta Centre II.[[37]](#cite_note-37) If built, Lakhta Centre II would rise 703 metres (2,306 ft) and be 150 floors. It would also be the second tallest building in the world along with the tallest twisted building and the first megatall building outside Asia. As of 2023, a small yacht club has been demolished on the site of Lakhta Centre II and construction was rumoured to begin in 2024.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

In December 2021 further plans were revealed for Lakhta Centre III. As planned, Lakhta Centre III would rise 555 metres (1,821 ft).[[38]](#cite_note-38) No major news for Lakhta Centre III has been released since its initial announcement. If built, Lakhta Centre III would be the second tallest building in Europe behind Lakhta Centre II, assuming Lakhta Centre II is completed before the third tower.

Landmark 81 is a [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Ho Chi Minh City](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh_City), Vietnam.[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) It is primarily invested in and developed by [Vinhomes](/wiki/Vinhomes), the largest Vietnamese real-estate developer. Landmark 81 holds the position of [the tallest building in Vietnam](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Vietnam), [the second-tallest building in Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), as well as the seventeenth-[tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) overall.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The 461.2-metre-tall (1,513 ft), 81-story building is located where the [Newport](/wiki/Newport_(Saigon)) logistics base was on the western banks of the [Saigon River](/wiki/Saigon_River) in [Thạnh Mỹ Tây](/wiki/Th%E1%BA%A1nh_M%E1%BB%B9_T%C3%A2y,_Ho_Chi_Minh_City) ward, located in the northeast side of Ho Chi Minh City's historic center and to the immediate southern side of [Saigon Bridge](/wiki/Saigon_Bridge). The tower is built at the heart of Vinhomes Central Park, a $1.5 billion high-end [mixed-use](/wiki/Mixed-use_development) [urban development](/wiki/Urban_development). The development comprises an [Autograph Collection](/wiki/Autograph_Collection) hotel (developed by [Vinpearl](/wiki/Vinpearl) and originally known as Vinpearl Landmark 81 Hotel) with conference facilities, luxury apartments, the Vincom Center Landmark 81 Mall (the second high-end retail spaces of Vingroup in Ho Chi Minh City after the [Vincom Center Đồng Khởi](/wiki/Vincom_Center_%C4%90%E1%BB%93ng_Kh%E1%BB%9Fi)), [officetel](/wiki/Officetel), restaurants, bars, and a multi-story observation deck.

The ground-breaking opening ceremony for the tower was held on 13 December 2014. In October 2017, construction reached floor 69, and with a height of 270 metres (890 ft), it already surpassed the [Bitexco Financial Tower](/wiki/Bitexco_Financial_Tower) to become the tallest building in Ho Chi Minh City. By January 2018, all floor construction was completed, with only the spire and crown remaining unfinished. On 10 April 2018, the last segment of the crown spire was added, completing Landmark 81.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The building's base, which takes up 6 floors with a total space of 50,000 m2 (540,000 sq ft), was opened on 26 July 2018 to mark the 25th anniversary of its owner's parent company, [Vingroup](/wiki/Vingroup).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The observation deck, named SkyView, opened on 28 April 2019. The deck, spanning from floor 79 at 369.65 m (1,212.8 ft) to floor 81 at 382.65 m (1,255.4 ft), is currently Vietnam's highest observation deck. Tickets range from 403,000 Vietnamese dong ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar)$17.40) for children to 810,000 dong (US$34.80) for adults.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

On 21 September 2022, an [Autograph Collection](/wiki/Autograph_Collection) hotel opened in Landmark 81. The hotel commences from the 47th floor to 71st floor of the skyscraper, housing 223 rooms.

The building's shape is partially inspired by the appearance of a bundle of bamboo sticks, which in Vietnamese culture is a metaphor for strength.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The following is a breakdown of floors.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Black smoke incident
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Landmark_81&action=edit&section=3)]

On 11 August 2018, around 15:48, black smoke was emitted from the 64th floor due to welding activities. The smoke was quickly controlled and did not cause any property damage.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The development comprises an [Autograph Collection](/wiki/Autograph_Collection) hotel (developed by [Vinpearl](/wiki/Vinpearl) and originally known as Vinpearl Landmark 81 Hotel) with conference facilities, luxury apartments, the Vincom Center Landmark 81 Mall (the second high-end retail spaces of Vingroup in Ho Chi Minh City after the [Vincom Center Đồng Khởi](/wiki/Vincom_Center_%C4%90%E1%BB%93ng_Kh%E1%BB%9Fi)), [officetel](/wiki/Officetel), restaurants, bars, and a multi-story observation deck.

The ground-breaking opening ceremony for the tower was held on 13 December 2014. By January 2018, all floor construction was completed, with only the spire and crown remaining unfinished. On 10 April 2018, the last segment of the crown spire was added, completing Landmark 81.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The building's base, which takes up 6 floors with a total space of 50,000 m2 (540,000 sq ft), was opened on 26 July 2018 to mark the 25th anniversary of its owner's parent company, [Vingroup](/wiki/Vingroup).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The observation deck, named SkyView, opened on 28 April 2019. The deck, spanning from floor 79 at 369.65 m (1,212.8 ft) to floor 81 at 382.65 m (1,255.4 ft), is currently Vietnam's highest observation deck. Changsha IFS Tower is a pair of [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan), China, with Tower 1 rising 452 meters and Tower 2 rising 315 meters.[[2]](#cite_note-wtp-2)[[3]](#cite_note-bco-3) Construction started in 2013 and completed in 2017. It is the [20th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and the tallest in Hunan.[[2]](#cite_note-wtp-2) IFS stands for "International Finance Square".

This twin-tower development is based on [Harbour City](/wiki/Harbour_City_(Hong_Kong)), a hyper-connected retail development in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong). The Changsha complex sports an underground network of linkages to an interchange hub ([Wuyi Square Station](/wiki/Wuyi_Square_Station)) for [Changsha metro](/wiki/Changsha_Metro) lines [1](/wiki/Line_1_(Changsha_Metro)) and [2](/wiki/Line_2_(Changsha_Metro)). The same underground passageway connects with one of the busiest pedestrian streets in China — Huangxing Road Pedestrian Shopping Street.

The rectangular form of the glass-clad towers is interrupted by a series of metal fins that add a hint of complexity to their appearance and reduce glare for the interior office spaces. Tower 1 features a crown that incrementally sets back by several meters at three points. Like many towers that break the 400-meter barrier, reduced floor plate sizes at the top are best suited for hotel uses—with guest willing to pay a premium for accommodations at such heights.

At the base, a block-sized podium contains a mega mall of 230,000 square meters.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Changsha IFS Tower 1 also has 700 meters of retail street frontage, greater than that of its likeness, Harbour City. The retail mall, among the largest in Changsha and Central China, offers amenities spanning entertainment, lifestyle, culture, and dining.

- [List of tallest buildings in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China)

Changsha IFS Tower is a pair of [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan), China, with Tower 1 rising 452 meters and Tower 2 rising 315 meters.[[2]](#cite_note-wtp-2)[[3]](#cite_note-bco-3) Construction started in 2013 and completed in 2017. It is the [20th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and the tallest in Hunan.[[2]](#cite_note-wtp-2) IFS stands for "International Finance Square".

This twin-tower development is based on [Harbour City](/wiki/Harbour_City_(Hong_Kong)), a hyper-connected retail development in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong). The Changsha complex sports an underground network of linkages to an interchange hub ([Wuyi Square Station](/wiki/Wuyi_Square_Station)) for [Changsha metro](/wiki/Changsha_Metro) lines [1](/wiki/Line_1_(Changsha_Metro)) and [2](/wiki/Line_2_(Changsha_Metro)). The same underground passageway connects with one of the busiest pedestrian streets in China — Huangxing Road Pedestrian Shopping Street.

The rectangular form of the glass-clad towers is interrupted by a series of metal fins that add a hint of complexity to their appearance and reduce glare for the interior office spaces. Tower 1 features a crown that incrementally sets back by several meters at three points. Like many towers that break the 400-meter barrier, reduced floor plate sizes at the top are best suited for hotel uses—with guest willing to pay a premium for accommodations at such heights.

At the base, a block-sized podium contains a mega mall of 230,000 square meters.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Changsha IFS Tower 1 also has 700 meters of retail street frontage, greater than that of its likeness, Harbour City. The retail mall, among the largest in Changsha and Central China, offers amenities spanning entertainment, lifestyle, culture, and dining.

The Petronas Towers ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language): Menara Berkembar Petronas), also known as the Petronas Twin Towers and colloquially the KLCC Twin Towers, are an [interlinked pair](/wiki/Twin_towers_(architecture)) of 88-storey [supertall skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia, standing at 451.9 m (1,483 ft). From 1996 to 2004, they were the [tallest buildings in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) until they were surpassed by the [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) building. The Petronas Towers remain the world's [tallest twin skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_twin_buildings_and_structures), surpassing the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers in New York City, and were the [tallest buildings in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia) until 2021, when they were surpassed by [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118). The Petronas Towers are a major landmark of Kuala Lumpur, along with the nearby [Kuala Lumpur Tower](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Tower) and Merdeka 118, and are visible in many places across the city.

## History and architecture
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petronas_Towers&action=edit&section=1)]

The Petronas Towers' [structural system](/wiki/Structural_system) is a [tube in tube design](/wiki/Tube_(structure)), invented by Bangladeshi-American architect [Fazlur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Fazlur_Rahman_Khan).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Applying a tube-structure for extremely tall buildings is a common phenomenon.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
The 88-floor towers are constructed largely of reinforced concrete, with a steel and glass facade designed to resemble motifs found in [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art), a reflection of Malaysia's [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim) religion.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Another Islamic influence on the design is that the [cross section](/wiki/Cross_section_(geometry)) of the towers is based on a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb), albeit with circular sectors added to meet office space requirements.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The circular sectors are similar to the bottom part of the [Qutb Minar](/wiki/Qutb_Minar).

/wiki/File:Petronas_Towers_level_43_plan.svgDevelopment of the Petronas Towers Tower 1 level 43 floor plan from a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb) symbol[[13]](#cite_note-13)
/wiki/File:Qutub_Minar_danibaba5.jpgThe cross section of the Petronas Towers is based on a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb), albeit with circular sectors similar to the bottom part of the [Qutb Minar](/wiki/Qutb_Minar).
/wiki/File:Petronas_Panorama_II_(4to3).jpgThe Petronas Towers at night, 2008
The towers were designed by [Argentine-American](/wiki/Argentine_Americans) architect [César Pelli](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Pelli). A distinctive [postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture) style was chosen to create a 21st-century icon for Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. Planning on the Petronas Towers started on 1 January 1992 and included rigorous tests and simulations of wind and structural loads on the design. Seven years of construction followed at the former site of the original [Selangor Turf Club](/wiki/Selangor_Turf_Club), beginning on 1 March 1993 with excavation, which involved moving 500 truckloads of earth every night to dig down 30 m (98 ft) below the surface.
The construction of the superstructure commenced on 1 April 1994. Interiors with furniture were completed on 1 January 1996, the spires of Tower 1 and Tower 2 were completed on 1 March 1996, 3 years after its construction was started, and the first batch of [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas) personnel moved into the building on 1 January 1997. The building was officially opened by the [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia), [Tun Dr. Mahathir bin Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad), on 31 August 1999.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The twin towers were built on the site of Kuala Lumpur's race track. It was the tallest structure in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia) at the time of its completion.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Test boreholes found that the original construction site effectively sat on the edge of a cliff. One half of the site was decayed limestone while the other half was soft rock. The entire site was moved 61 m (200 ft) to allow the buildings to sit entirely on the soft rock.[[16]](#cite_note-Anstey-16) Because of the depth of the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock), the buildings were built on the world's deepest foundations.[[17]](#cite_note-17) 104 concrete piles, ranging from 60 to 114 m (197 to 374 ft) deep, were bored into the ground. The concrete raft foundation, comprising 13,200 m3 (470,000 cu ft) of concrete was continuously poured through a period of 54 hours for each tower. The raft is 4.6 m (15 ft) thick, weighs 32,500 t (35,800 tons) and held the world record for the largest concrete pour until 2007.[[16]](#cite_note-Anstey-16) The foundations were completed within 12 months by Bachy Soletanche and required massive amounts of concrete.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As a result of the Malaysian government specifying that the buildings be completed in six years, two construction consortia were hired to meet the deadline, one for each tower. Tower 1, the west tower (left in the top-right photograph) was built by a Japanese consortium led by the [Hazama Corporation](/wiki/Hazama_Corporation) ([JA Jones Construction Co.](/wiki/J.A._Jones_Construction), MMC Engineering Services Sdn Bhd, Ho Hup Construction Co. Bhd and [Mitsubishi Corp](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Corporation)) while Tower 2, the east tower (right in the top-right photograph) was built by a South Korean consortium led by the [Samsung C&T Corporation](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) (Kukdong Engineering & Construction and Syarikat Jasatera Sdn Bhd).

Early into construction a batch of concrete failed a routine strength test causing construction to come to a complete halt. All the completed floors were tested but it was found that only one had used a bad batch and it was demolished. As a result of the concrete failure, each new batch was tested before being poured. The halt in construction had cost US$700,000 per day and led to three separate [concrete plants](/wiki/Concrete_plant) being set up on the site to ensure that if one produced a bad batch, the other two could continue to supply concrete. The sky bridge contract was completed by Kukdong Engineering & Construction. Tower 2 (Samsung C&T) became the first to reach the world's tallest building at the time.

Due to the huge cost of importing steel, the towers were constructed on a cheaper radical design of super high-strength reinforced concrete.[[19]](#cite_note-19) High-strength concrete is a material familiar to Asian contractors and twice as effective as steel in sway reduction; however, it makes the building twice as heavy on its foundation as a comparable steel building. Supported by 23-by-23 metre concrete cores[[20]](#cite_note-20) and an outer ring of widely spaced super columns, the towers use a sophisticated structural system that accommodates its slender profile and provides 560,000 square metres of column-free office space.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Below the twin towers is [Suria KLCC](/wiki/Suria_KLCC), a shopping mall, and [Petronas Philharmonic Hall](/wiki/Petronas_Philharmonic_Hall), the home of the [Malaysian Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Malaysian_Philharmonic_Orchestra).

- On 15 April 1999, [Felix Baumgartner](/wiki/Felix_Baumgartner) set the world record for [BASE jumping](/wiki/BASE_jumping) (since broken) by jumping off a window cleaning crane on the Petronas Towers.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- Thousands of people were evacuated on 12 September 2001 after a bomb threat the day after the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) destroyed the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers in New York City. Bomb disposal squads found no explosives in the towers, but they nevertheless evacuated the premises. Workers and shoppers were allowed to return three hours later, around noon. No one was hurt during the evacuation.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

- On the evening of 4 November 2005, a fire broke out in the [cinema complex](/wiki/Tanjong_Golden_Village) of the Suria KLCC shopping centre below the Petronas Towers, triggering panic among patrons. There were no reports of injuries. The buildings were largely empty, except the shopping mall, Suria KLCC, because of the late hour; the only people involved were moviegoers and some diners in restaurants.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

- On the morning of 1 September 2009, French urban climber [Alain "Spiderman" Robert](/wiki/Alain_Robert), using only his bare hands and feet and with no safety devices, scaled to the top of Tower Two in just under 2 hours after two previous efforts had ended in arrest.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In his first attempt on 20 March 1997, police arrested him at the 60th floor, 28 floors away from the "summit". His second attempt, on 20 March 2007, exactly 10 years later, was also stopped on the same floor, though on the other tower.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Tower One is fully occupied by [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas) and a number of its subsidiaries and associate companies, while the office spaces in Tower Two are mostly available for lease to other companies.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources)][[29]](#cite_note-29) A number of companies have offices in Tower Two, including SapuraOMV Upstream (Sarawak) Inc., [Huawei Technologies](/wiki/Huawei_Technologies), [AVEVA](/wiki/Aveva), [Al Jazeera English](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English), Carigali Hess, [Bloomberg News](/wiki/Bloomberg_News), [Bloomberg Television](/wiki/Bloomberg_Television), [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing), [IBM](/wiki/IBM), [Khazanah Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Khazanah_Nasional), [McKinsey & Co](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company), [WIPRO Limited](/wiki/Wipro), [TCS](/wiki/Tata_Consultancy_Services), [HCLTech](/wiki/HCLTech), Krawler, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft), The Agency (a modelling company) and [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters).

The Petronas Towers ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language): Menara Berkembar Petronas), also known as the Petronas Twin Towers and colloquially the KLCC Twin Towers, are an [interlinked pair](/wiki/Twin_towers_(architecture)) of 88-storey [supertall skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia, standing at 451.9 m (1,483 ft). From 1996 to 2004, they were the [tallest buildings in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) until they were surpassed by the [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) building. The Petronas Towers remain the world's [tallest twin skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_twin_buildings_and_structures), surpassing the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers in New York City, and were the [tallest buildings in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia) until 2021, when they were surpassed by [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118). The Petronas Towers are a major landmark of Kuala Lumpur, along with the nearby [Kuala Lumpur Tower](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Tower) and Merdeka 118, and are visible in many places across the city.

## History and architecture
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Petronas_Towers&action=edit&section=1)]

The Petronas Towers' [structural system](/wiki/Structural_system) is a [tube in tube design](/wiki/Tube_(structure)), invented by Bangladeshi-American architect [Fazlur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Fazlur_Rahman_Khan).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Applying a tube-structure for extremely tall buildings is a common phenomenon.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
The 88-floor towers are constructed largely of reinforced concrete, with a steel and glass facade designed to resemble motifs found in [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art), a reflection of Malaysia's [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim) religion.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Another Islamic influence on the design is that the [cross section](/wiki/Cross_section_(geometry)) of the towers is based on a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb), albeit with circular sectors added to meet office space requirements.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The circular sectors are similar to the bottom part of the [Qutb Minar](/wiki/Qutb_Minar).

/wiki/File:Petronas_Towers_level_43_plan.svgDevelopment of the Petronas Towers Tower 1 level 43 floor plan from a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb) symbol[[13]](#cite_note-13)
/wiki/File:Qutub_Minar_danibaba5.jpgThe cross section of the Petronas Towers is based on a [Rub el Hizb](/wiki/Rub_el_Hizb), albeit with circular sectors similar to the bottom part of the [Qutb Minar](/wiki/Qutb_Minar).
/wiki/File:Petronas_Panorama_II_(4to3).jpgThe Petronas Towers at night, 2008
The towers were designed by [Argentine-American](/wiki/Argentine_Americans) architect [César Pelli](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Pelli). A distinctive [postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture) style was chosen to create a 21st-century icon for Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. Planning on the Petronas Towers started on 1 January 1992 and included rigorous tests and simulations of wind and structural loads on the design. Seven years of construction followed at the former site of the original [Selangor Turf Club](/wiki/Selangor_Turf_Club), beginning on 1 March 1993 with excavation, which involved moving 500 truckloads of earth every night to dig down 30 m (98 ft) below the surface.
The construction of the superstructure commenced on 1 April 1994. Interiors with furniture were completed on 1 January 1996, the spires of Tower 1 and Tower 2 were completed on 1 March 1996, 3 years after its construction was started, and the first batch of [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas) personnel moved into the building on 1 January 1997. The building was officially opened by the [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia), [Tun Dr. Mahathir bin Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad), on 31 August 1999.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The twin towers were built on the site of Kuala Lumpur's race track. It was the tallest structure in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia) at the time of its completion.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Test boreholes found that the original construction site effectively sat on the edge of a cliff. One half of the site was decayed limestone while the other half was soft rock. The entire site was moved 61 m (200 ft) to allow the buildings to sit entirely on the soft rock.[[16]](#cite_note-Anstey-16) Because of the depth of the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock), the buildings were built on the world's deepest foundations.[[17]](#cite_note-17) 104 concrete piles, ranging from 60 to 114 m (197 to 374 ft) deep, were bored into the ground. The concrete raft foundation, comprising 13,200 m3 (470,000 cu ft) of concrete was continuously poured through a period of 54 hours for each tower. The raft is 4.6 m (15 ft) thick, weighs 32,500 t (35,800 tons) and held the world record for the largest concrete pour until 2007.[[16]](#cite_note-Anstey-16) The foundations were completed within 12 months by Bachy Soletanche and required massive amounts of concrete.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As a result of the Malaysian government specifying that the buildings be completed in six years, two construction consortia were hired to meet the deadline, one for each tower. Tower 1, the west tower (left in the top-right photograph) was built by a Japanese consortium led by the [Hazama Corporation](/wiki/Hazama_Corporation) ([JA Jones Construction Co.](/wiki/J.A._Jones_Construction), MMC Engineering Services Sdn Bhd, Ho Hup Construction Co. Bhd and [Mitsubishi Corp](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Corporation)) while Tower 2, the east tower (right in the top-right photograph) was built by a South Korean consortium led by the [Samsung C&T Corporation](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T_Corporation) (Kukdong Engineering & Construction and Syarikat Jasatera Sdn Bhd).

Early into construction a batch of concrete failed a routine strength test causing construction to come to a complete halt. All the completed floors were tested but it was found that only one had used a bad batch and it was demolished. As a result of the concrete failure, each new batch was tested before being poured. The halt in construction had cost US$700,000 per day and led to three separate [concrete plants](/wiki/Concrete_plant) being set up on the site to ensure that if one produced a bad batch, the other two could continue to supply concrete. The sky bridge contract was completed by Kukdong Engineering & Construction. Tower 2 (Samsung C&T) became the first to reach the world's tallest building at the time.

Due to the huge cost of importing steel, the towers were constructed on a cheaper radical design of super high-strength reinforced concrete.[[19]](#cite_note-19) High-strength concrete is a material familiar to Asian contractors and twice as effective as steel in sway reduction; however, it makes the building twice as heavy on its foundation as a comparable steel building. Supported by 23-by-23 metre concrete cores[[20]](#cite_note-20) and an outer ring of widely spaced super columns, the towers use a sophisticated structural system that accommodates its slender profile and provides 560,000 square metres of column-free office space.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Below the twin towers is [Suria KLCC](/wiki/Suria_KLCC), a shopping mall, and [Petronas Philharmonic Hall](/wiki/Petronas_Philharmonic_Hall), the home of the [Malaysian Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Malaysian_Philharmonic_Orchestra).

- On 15 April 1999, [Felix Baumgartner](/wiki/Felix_Baumgartner) set the world record for [BASE jumping](/wiki/BASE_jumping) (since broken) by jumping off a window cleaning crane on the Petronas Towers.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- Thousands of people were evacuated on 12 September 2001 after a bomb threat the day after the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) destroyed the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers in New York City. Bomb disposal squads found no explosives in the towers, but they nevertheless evacuated the premises. Workers and shoppers were allowed to return three hours later, around noon. No one was hurt during the evacuation.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

- On the evening of 4 November 2005, a fire broke out in the [cinema complex](/wiki/Tanjong_Golden_Village) of the Suria KLCC shopping centre below the Petronas Towers, triggering panic among patrons. There were no reports of injuries. The buildings were largely empty, except the shopping mall, Suria KLCC, because of the late hour; the only people involved were moviegoers and some diners in restaurants.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

- On the morning of 1 September 2009, French urban climber [Alain "Spiderman" Robert](/wiki/Alain_Robert), using only his bare hands and feet and with no safety devices, scaled to the top of Tower Two in just under 2 hours after two previous efforts had ended in arrest.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In his first attempt on 20 March 1997, police arrested him at the 60th floor, 28 floors away from the "summit". His second attempt, on 20 March 2007, exactly 10 years later, was also stopped on the same floor, though on the other tower.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Tower One is fully occupied by [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas) and a number of its subsidiaries and associate companies, while the office spaces in Tower Two are mostly available for lease to other companies.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources)][[29]](#cite_note-29) A number of companies have offices in Tower Two, including SapuraOMV Upstream (Sarawak) Inc., [Huawei Technologies](/wiki/Huawei_Technologies), [AVEVA](/wiki/Aveva), [Al Jazeera English](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English), Carigali Hess, [Bloomberg News](/wiki/Bloomberg_News), [Bloomberg Television](/wiki/Bloomberg_Television), [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing), [IBM](/wiki/IBM), [Khazanah Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Khazanah_Nasional), [McKinsey & Co](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company), [WIPRO Limited](/wiki/Wipro), [TCS](/wiki/Tata_Consultancy_Services), [HCLTech](/wiki/HCLTech), Krawler, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft), The Agency (a modelling company) and [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters).

Taipei 101 ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 台北101; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Táiběi Yīlíngyī; stylized in [all caps](/wiki/All_caps)), formerly known as the Taipei World Financial Center, is a 508-metre (1,667 ft), 101-story skyscraper in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei), Taiwan. It is owned by the [Taipei Financial Center Corporation](/wiki/Taipei_Financial_Center_Corporation). It was [officially classified](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) as the [world's tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) from its opening on 31 December 2004, until it was dethroned by the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in 2009.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Upon completion, it became the world's first skyscraper to exceed half a kilometer. It is the [tallest building in Taiwan](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Taiwan) and the eleventh tallest in the world.[[12]](#cite_note-Bor-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

The building's high-speed elevators were manufactured by [Toshiba](/wiki/Toshiba) of Japan and held the record for the fastest in the world at the time of completion, transporting passengers from the 5th to the 89th floor in 37 seconds (attaining 60.6 kilometres per hour or 37.7 miles per hour).[[12]](#cite_note-Bor-12)[[a]](#cite_note-37_sec-14) In 2011, Taipei 101 was awarded a Platinum certificate rating under the [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design) certification system for energy efficiency and environmental design, becoming the tallest and largest [green building](/wiki/Green_building) in the world.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-15) The structure regularly appears as an icon of Taipei in international media, and the Taipei 101 [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks) displays are a regular feature of New Year's Eve broadcasts and celebrations.

Taipei 101's [postmodernist](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture) and [neo-futurist](/wiki/Neo-futurism) architectural style evokes traditional Asian aesthetics in a modern structure employing industrial materials. Its design incorporates a number of features that enable the structure to withstand the [Pacific Ring of Fire](/wiki/Pacific_Ring_of_Fire)'s earthquakes and the region's [tropical storms](/wiki/Tropical_storm). The tower houses offices, restaurants, shops, and indoor and outdoor [observatories](/wiki/Observatories). The tower is adjoined by a multilevel shopping mall that has the world's largest [ruyi](/wiki/Ruyi_(scepter)) symbol as an exterior feature.

The [Taipei Financial Center Corporation](/wiki/Taipei_Financial_Center_Corporation), a team led by several Taiwan banks and insurance companies, won the rights to lease the site for 70 years and develop a building, placing the winning bid of NT$20,688,890,000 for the [Build Operate Transfer](/wiki/Build_Operate_Transfer) agreement with the city government.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-16)

Planning for Taipei 101 began in July 1997[[2]](#cite_note-Report13-2) during [Chen Shui-bian](/wiki/Chen_Shui-bian)'s term as Taipei mayor. Talks between merchants and city government officials initially centered on a proposal for a 66-story tower to serve as an anchor for new development in Taipei's 101 business district. Planners were considering taking the new structure to a more ambitious height only after an expat suggested it, along with many of the other features used in the design of the building. It was not until the summer of 2000 that the city granted a license for the construction of a 101-story tower on the site. In the meantime, construction proceeded and the first tower column was erected in the summer of 2001.[[2]](#cite_note-Report13-2)[[16]](#cite_note-NYT-17)

A [major earthquake](/wiki/2002_Taiwan_earthquake) struck Taiwan on 31 March 2002, sending two construction cranes falling from the 56th floor onto streets near Xinyi (Hsinyi) Road. The cranes crushed several vehicles and caused five deaths - two crane operators and three other construction workers.[[17]](#cite_note-TT20020402-18)[[18]](#cite_note-TT20020401-19) However, the project’s backers said the building itself had not been structurally damaged.[[18]](#cite_note-TT20020401-19) Construction work was halted pending safety inspections,[[18]](#cite_note-TT20020401-19) and later restarted in stages, with full resumption approved in late June and early July 2002.[[19]](#cite_note-TT20020706-20)

Taipei 101's roof was completed three years later on 1 July 2003. Taipei 101 was completed in 2004. The construction was a joint venture led by [Kumagai Gumi](/wiki/Kumagai_Gumi), a Japanese construction company, in cooperation with [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T), a South Korean construction company. Samsung C&T was responsible for overseeing the construction of the main structural framework, and RESE was responsible for the construction logistics and main foundation.[[20]](#cite_note-21) [Ma Ying-jeou](/wiki/Ma_Ying-jeou), in his first term as Taipei mayor, fastened a golden bolt to signify the achievement.[[8]](#cite_note-emporis-8) The formal opening of the tower took place on 31 December 2004. President Chen Shui-bian, Taipei Mayor Ma Ying-jeou and Legislative Speaker [Wang Jin-pyng](/wiki/Wang_Jin-pyng) cut the ribbon. Open-air concerts featured a number of popular performers, including singers [A-Mei](/wiki/A-Mei) and [Stefanie Sun](/wiki/Stefanie_Sun). Visitors rode the elevators to the Observatory for the first time. A few hours later the first fireworks show at Taipei 101 heralded the arrival of a new year.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) It replaced the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) in Kuala Lumpur as the world's tallest building.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Taipei Financial Center Corporation (TFCC) announced plans on 2 November 2009 to make Taipei 101 "the world's tallest building" by summer of 2011 as measured by [LEED](/wiki/LEED) standards. The structure was already designed to be energy-efficient, with double-pane windows blocking external heat by 50% and recycled water meeting 20–30% of the building's needs. LEED certification would entail inspections and upgrades in wiring, water and lighting equipment at a cost of NT$60 million (US$1.8 million). Estimates showed the savings resulting from the modifications paid for the cost of making them within three years.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-15) The project was carried out under the guidance of an international team composed of [Siemens Building Technologies](/wiki/Siemens_Building_Technologies), architect and interior designer Steven Leach Group and the LEED advisory firm EcoTech International.[[25]](#cite_note-http://www.buildingtechnologies.siemens.com/bt/global/en/Pages/home.aspx-26) The company applied for a platinum-degree certification with LEED in early 2011.[[26]](#cite_note-27) On 28 July 2011, Taipei 101 received LEED platinum certification under "Existing Buildings: Operations and Maintenance". Although the project cost NT$60 million (US$2.08 million), it is expected to save 14.4 million kilowatt-hours of electricity, or an 18% energy-saving, equivalent to NT$36 million (US$1.2 million) in energy costs each year.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-15) In 2019, it was named among the 50 most influential skyscrapers in the world by the [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat).[[27]](#cite_note-28)

On 4 January 2020, the building had a condolence message in lights for [the victims of a helicopter crash](/wiki/2020_ROCAF_UH-60M_crash), which included a number of senior government officials.[[28]](#cite_note-29) On 8 February 2020, it was reported that some passengers of the [Diamond Princess](/wiki/Diamond_Princess_(ship)) cruise liner, [quarantined for an outbreak of COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_on_Diamond_Princess), had visited Taipei 101 on 31 January at which point none exhibited symptoms.[[29]](#cite_note-30) On 1 April 2020, the shopping center said it was reducing business hours due to the coronavirus pandemic.[[30]](#cite_note-Reference1C-31) It had started checking shopper's temperatures in February.[[30]](#cite_note-Reference1C-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) On 21 May 2020, the building said it would resume normal business hours in June, as the country had effectively limited the spread of COVID-19.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### Events and celebrity appearances
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taipei_101&action=edit&section=6)]

Taipei 101 is the site of many special events. Art exhibits, as noted above, regularly take place in the Observatory. A few noteworthy dates since the tower's opening include these below:

- On 25 December 2004, French [rock](/wiki/Rock_climbing) and [urban climber](/wiki/Buildering) [Alain Robert](/wiki/Alain_Robert) made an authorized climb to the top of the pinnacle in four hours.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

- On 28 February 2005, former President of the United States [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton) visited and signed copies of his autobiography.[[34]](#cite_note-Clinton2005-35)

- On 19 April 2005, the tower displayed the formula "[E=mc2](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence)" in lights to celebrate the 100th anniversary of the publication of [Einstein's](/wiki/Albert_Einstein) [theory of relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity). The display, the largest of 65,000 such displays in 47 countries, was part of the international celebration [World Year of Physics 2005](/wiki/World_Year_of_Physics_2005).[[35]](#cite_note-eNewsletter-36)

- On 20 November 2005, the First annual [Taipei 101 Run Up](/wiki/Taipei_101_Run_Up) featured a race up the 2,046 steps from floors 1 to 91. Proceeds w
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="c7de4483ff5c6368">>>"
}

Shanghai World Financial Center
上海环球金融中心

[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-skyscraperpage-3)[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4)[[5]](#cite_note-chinadaily-5)

The Shanghai World Financial Center (SWFC; [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): [上海](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7)[环球](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8E%AF%E7%90%83)[金融](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D)[中心](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Shànghǎi Huánqiú Jīnróng Zhōngxīn, [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese): Zånhae Guejieu Cinyon Tsonsin) is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall_building) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) located in the [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong) district of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai). It was designed by [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) and developed by the [Mori Building Company](/wiki/Mori_Building_Company), with Leslie E. Robertson Associates as its structural engineer and [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Shanghai Construction (Group) General Co. as its main contractor. It is a mixed-use skyscraper, consisting of offices, hotels, conference rooms, and a ground-floor shopping mall.

On 14 September 2007, the skyscraper was [topped out](/wiki/Topped_out) at 492 meters (1,614.2 ft),[[1]](#cite_note-ssc010720-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6) making it the [2nd tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings)[[7]](#cite_note-7) on completion (the tallest at the time being [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101)), the tallest building in the world by roof height only, and [the tallest in mainland China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China).[[8]](#cite_note-8) The SWFC opened to the public on 28 August 2008, with its (since closed) [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) opening on 30 August. The observation deck offered views from 474 m (1,555 ft) above ground level.[[9]](#cite_note-bbc_open-9)

[Park Hyatt Shanghai](/wiki/Hyatt) is the tower's hotel component, comprising 174 rooms and suites occupying the 79th to the 93rd floors, which at the time of completion was the highest hotel in the world. It is now the third-highest hotel in the world after the [Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong), which occupies floors 102 to 118 of the [International Commerce Centre](/wiki/International_Commerce_Centre).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items)]

The SWFC has been lauded for its design, and in 2008 it was named by architects as the year's best-completed skyscraper.[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2013, the SWFC was exceeded in height by the adjacent [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower),[[13]](#cite_note-500Metres-13) which is China's tallest structure as of 2023. Together, The Shanghai World Financial Center, The [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) and The [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) form the world's first adjacent grouping of three supertall skyscrapers.

Designed by American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox), the 100-story tower was originally planned for construction in 1997, but work was temporarily interrupted by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis), and was later paused to accommodate design changes by the Mori Building Company. The building of the tower was financed by several multinational firms, including Chinese, Japanese, and Hong Kong banks, as well as by the Japanese developer and American and European investors. The American investment bank [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley) coordinated the tower's financing for Mori Building.

/wiki/File:SWFC_200602.jpgUnder construction in February 2006.
The tower's foundation stone was laid on 27 August 1997. In the late 1990s, the Pierre de Smet Building Corporation suffered a funding shortage caused by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis), which halted the project after the foundations were completed. On 13 February 2003, the Mori Group increased the building's height to 492 m (1,614 ft) and 101 stories, from the initial plans for a 460-metre (1,509 ft), 94-story building. The new building used the foundations of the original design, and construction work was resumed on 16 November 2003.[[14]](#cite_note-pdonline-14)

A fire broke out in the incomplete SWFC on 14 August 2007. The fire was first noticed on the 40th floor, around 16:30 (GMT +8), and soon the smoke was clearly seen outside the building. By 17:45, the fire had been extinguished. The damage was reported to be slight, and nobody was injured in the accident.[[15]](#cite_note-forbes-15) The cause of the fire remains unknown, but according to some sources the preliminary investigation suggested workers' electric weldings caused the fire.[[16]](#cite_note-windowofchina-16)

The building reached its full height of 492 m (1,614 ft) on 14 September 2007 after the installation of the final steel girder.[[17]](#cite_note-guardian-17) The final cladding panels were installed in mid-June 2008, and elevator installation was finished in mid-July. The Shanghai World Financial Center was declared complete on 17 July 2008, and was officially opened on 28 August.[[9]](#cite_note-bbc_open-9) On 30 August 2008, the tower's observation floors were opened to the public.

/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_in_Asia.jpgThe Shanghai World Financial Center compared with other Asian skyscrapers.
Since the completion of the adjacent [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) in 2016, the World Financial Center observation deck was no longer the tallest in Shanghai and consequently saw decreasing visitor numbers. This situation was exacerbated by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic), which in 2023 led to the deck's permanent closing.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

/wiki/File:Shanghai_World_Financial_Center_Far.jpgShanghai World Financial Center.
Shanghai World Financial Center
[Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters)上海环球
金融中心
[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)上海環球
金融中心
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Shànghǎi huánqiú jīnróng zhōngxīn
[Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles)Shanghai huanch'iu chinjung chunghsin
[IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)[[ʂâŋxàɪ xwǎntɕʰjǒʊ tɕínɻʊ̌ŋ ʈʂʊ́ŋɕín]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)
[Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese)
[Romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Wu_Chinese)Zaanhe guejjieu jinhhion tsonxin [[zɑ̃²³hɛ³⁴ ɡuɛ²³dʑiɤ²³ tɕin⁵³ɦioŋ²³ tsoŋ⁵³ɕin⁵³]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Wu_Chinese)

/wiki/File:Top_of_the_Shanghai_World_Financial_Center.jpgThe top of the building, with the aperture clearly visible.
The most distinctive feature of the SWFC's design is the trapezoid aperture at the peak. The original design specified a circular aperture, 46 m (151 ft) in diameter, to reduce the stresses of wind pressure[[19]](#cite_note-nytimes-19) and to reference the Chinese mythological depiction of the sky as a circle.[[19]](#cite_note-nytimes-19) It also resembled a Chinese [moon gate](/wiki/Moon_gate) due to its circular form in [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture). However, this initial design began facing protests from some Chinese, including the mayor of Shanghai, [Chen Liangyu](/wiki/Chen_Liangyu), who considered it too similar to the [rising sun](/wiki/Names_of_Japan) design of the [Japanese flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Japan). This was controversial due to Japan's ties to war crimes in China and other parts of Asia. Pedersen then suggested that a bridge be placed at the bottom of the aperture to make it less circular.[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4) On 18 October 2005, KPF submitted an alternative design to Mori Building and a trapezoidal hole replaced the circle at the top of the tower, which in addition to changing the controversial design, would also be cheaper and easier to implement, according to the architects.[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Foreigners and Chinese alike informally refer to the building as "the [bottle opener](/wiki/Bottle_opener)".[[21]](#cite_note-21) Metal replicas of the building that function as actual bottle openers are sold in the tower's gift shop.

The tower features three separate [observation decks](/wiki/Observation_deck) which constitute the floors above and below the aperture opening. The height of the lowest observation deck, located on the 94th floor, is 423 m (1,388 ft); the second, on the 97th floor, is at a height of 439 m (1,440 ft); and the highest, on the 100th floor, is 474 m (1,555 ft) high.

The skyscraper's roof height is set at 492 m (1,614 ft), and was at one point the highest roof in the world. Before construction on the roof was completed, the SWFC's total height was scheduled to be 509.2 m (1,671 ft) so that it would exceed the height of the [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), but a height limit was imposed, allowing the roof to reach a maximum height of 492 metres. Architect [William Pedersen](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox#History) and developer [Minoru Mori](/wiki/Minoru_Mori) resisted suggestions to add a spire that would surpass that of Taipei 101 and perhaps [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center), calling the SWFC a "broad-shouldered building". The SWFC boasts a gross floor area of more than 377,300 m2 (4,061,200 sq ft), 31 elevators, and 33 escalators.

### Structural efficiency
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_World_Financial_Center&action=edit&section=4)]

The tower's trapezoid aperture is made up of structural steel and reinforced concrete. A large number of forces, such as wind loads, the people in the building and heavy equipment housed in the building, act on the SWFC's structure. These compressive and bending forces are carried down to the ground by the diagonal-braced frame (with added outrigger trusses). The design employs an effective use of material, because it decreases the thickness of the outer core shear walls and the weight of the structural steel in the perimeter.

Shanghai World Financial Center hosts the office building for many international financial companies, including those involved in [banking](/wiki/Banking), [insurance](/wiki/Insurance), [securities](/wiki/Securities) and [fund management](/wiki/Investment_management), such as [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young), [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley), [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas), [Commerzbank](/wiki/Commerzbank), [Bank of Yokohama](/wiki/Bank_of_Yokohama), [Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation](/wiki/Sumitomo_Mitsui_Banking_Corporation) and [Korea Development Bank](/wiki/Korea_Development_Bank).[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Google's Shanghai branch is located on the 60th-61st floors.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

- [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro): [Line 2](/wiki/Line_2,_Shanghai_Metro) at [Lujiazui Station](/wiki/Lujiazui_Station) is a 10-minute walk to the center.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Shanghai World Financial Center was named by architects as the best skyscraper completed in 2008, receiving both the Best Tall Building Overall and Asia & Australasia awards from the [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) (CTBUH).[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11)[[26]](#cite_note-CTBUHAwards-26) CTBUH's Carol Willis, head of New York's [Skyscraper Museum](/wiki/Skyscraper_Museum), stated: "The simplicity of its form as well as its size dramatizes the idea of the skyscraper."[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11) Architect Tim Johnson noted its innovative structural design: "Steel trusses guard against the forces of wind and earthquake and made the building lighter, made it use less steel, and contributed to its sustainability."[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11) Johnson described the SWFC's structure as "nothing short of genius."[[26]](#cite_note-CTBUHAwards-26)

- /wiki/File:Observation_deck_of_the_Shanghai_World_Financial_Center.jpg
 Inside the tower's observation deck.

- /wiki/File:0352_20090626_Shanghai.jpg
 Shanghai World Financial Center (left) and the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower).

- /wiki/File:Shanghai_World_Financial_Center_%2B_Jin_Mao_Tower.jpg
 The Shanghai World Financial Center and Jin Mao Tower adjacent to each other.

- /wiki/File:Night_shot_of_the_Shanghai_World_Financial_Centre.jpg

- /wiki/File:Shanghai_world_financial_centre.JPG
