The most distinctive feature of the SWFC's design is the trapezoid aperture at the peak. The original design specified a circular aperture, 46 m (151 ft) in diameter, to reduce the stresses of wind pressure[[19]](#cite_note-nytimes-19) and to reference the Chinese mythological depiction of the sky as a circle.[[19]](#cite_note-nytimes-19) It also resembled a Chinese [moon gate](/wiki/Moon_gate) due to its circular form in [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture). However, this initial design began facing protests from some Chinese, including the mayor of Shanghai, [Chen Liangyu](/wiki/Chen_Liangyu), who considered it too similar to the [rising sun](/wiki/Names_of_Japan) design of the [Japanese flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Japan). This was controversial due to Japan's ties to war crimes in China and other parts of Asia. Pedersen then suggested that a bridge be placed at the bottom of the aperture to make it less circular.[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4) On 18 October 2005, KPF submitted an alternative design to Mori Building and a trapezoidal hole replaced the circle at the top of the tower, which in addition to changing the controversial design, would also be cheaper and easier to implement, according to the architects.[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Foreigners and Chinese alike informally refer to the building as "the [bottle opener](/wiki/Bottle_opener)".[[21]](#cite_note-21) Metal replicas of the building that function as actual bottle openers are sold in the tower's gift shop.

The tower features three separate [observation decks](/wiki/Observation_deck) which constitute the floors above and below the aperture opening. The height of the lowest observation deck, located on the 94th floor, is 423 m (1,388 ft); the second, on the 97th floor, is at a height of 439 m (1,440 ft); and the highest, on the 100th floor, is 474 m (1,555 ft) high.

The skyscraper's roof height is set at 492 m (1,614 ft), and was at one point the highest roof in the world. Before construction on the roof was completed, the SWFC's total height was scheduled to be 509.2 m (1,671 ft) so that it would exceed the height of the [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), but a height limit was imposed, allowing the roof to reach a maximum height of 492 metres. Architect [William Pedersen](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox#History) and developer [Minoru Mori](/wiki/Minoru_Mori) resisted suggestions to add a spire that would surpass that of Taipei 101 and perhaps [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center), calling the SWFC a "broad-shouldered building". The SWFC boasts a gross floor area of more than 377,300 m2 (4,061,200 sq ft), 31 elevators, and 33 escalators.

### Structural efficiency
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_World_Financial_Center&action=edit&section=4)]

The tower's trapezoid aperture is made up of structural steel and reinforced concrete. A large number of forces, such as wind loads, the people in the building and heavy equipment housed in the building, act on the SWFC's structure. These compressive and bending forces are carried down to the ground by the diagonal-braced frame (with added outrigger trusses). The design employs an effective use of material, because it decreases the thickness of the outer core shear walls and the weight of the structural steel in the perimeter.

Shanghai World Financial Center hosts the office building for many international financial companies, including those involved in [banking](/wiki/Banking), [insurance](/wiki/Insurance), [securities](/wiki/Securities) and [fund management](/wiki/Investment_management), such as [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young), [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley), [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas), [Commerzbank](/wiki/Commerzbank), [Bank of Yokohama](/wiki/Bank_of_Yokohama), [Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation](/wiki/Sumitomo_Mitsui_Banking_Corporation) and [Korea Development Bank](/wiki/Korea_Development_Bank).[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Google's Shanghai branch is located on the 60th-61st floors.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

- [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro): [Line 2](/wiki/Line_2,_Shanghai_Metro) at [Lujiazui Station](/wiki/Lujiazui_Station) is a 10-minute walk to the center.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Shanghai World Financial Center was named by architects as the best skyscraper completed in 2008, receiving both the Best Tall Building Overall and Asia & Australasia awards from the [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) (CTBUH).[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11)[[26]](#cite_note-CTBUHAwards-26) CTBUH's Carol Willis, head of New York's [Skyscraper Museum](/wiki/Skyscraper_Museum), stated: "The simplicity of its form as well as its size dramatizes the idea of the skyscraper."[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11) Architect Tim Johnson noted its innovative structural design: "Steel trusses guard against the forces of wind and earthquake and made the building lighter, made it use less steel, and contributed to its sustainability."[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11) Johnson described the SWFC's structure as "nothing short of genius."[[26]](#cite_note-CTBUHAwards-26)

Zifeng Tower (Greenland Center-Zifeng Tower or Greenland Square Zifeng Tower, formerly Nanjing Greenland Financial Center)[[4]](#cite_note-som-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a 450-meter (1,480 ft) supertall [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) (special class of skyscraper) in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu). The 66-story building completed in 2010 comprises retail and office. Floors 49/71 feature a hotel, numerous restaurants, and a [public observatory](/wiki/Public_observatory). The tower's stepping is functional; office, hotel, and retail are located above grade, while the restaurants and the public observatory are housed at the top of the tower.[[4]](#cite_note-som-4) The building is currently the [tallest in Nanjing](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Nanjing) and Jiangsu province, the [eleventh tallest in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) and the [twentieth tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Architectural firm [Skidmore, Owings and Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_and_Merrill) designed the building, led by [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)).[[4]](#cite_note-som-4)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Tower occupies an area of 18,721 square meters. The Nanjing Greenland [InterContinental](/wiki/InterContinental) Hotel is located within the tower.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

/wiki/File:Zifeng_Tower_in_Sunset.jpgZifeng Tower during sunset
/wiki/File:Nanjing_Zifeng_Tower_%E7%B4%AB%E5%B3%B0%E5%A4%A7%E5%8E%A6.jpgZifeng Tower during sunset

The tower's stepping is functional; office, hotel, and retail are located above grade, while restaurants and a public observatory are housed at the top of the tower, which is crowned with a spire.

Jin Mao Tower

References
[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Jin Mao Tower ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): [金](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91)[茂](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8C%82)[大厦](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%8E%A6); [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): [金](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91)[茂](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8C%82)[大廈](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%BB%88); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Jīnmào Dàshà; [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese): Cinmeu Dagho; lit. 'Golden Prosperity Building'), also known as the Jinmao Building or Jinmao Tower, is a 420.5-meter-tall (1,380 ft), 88-story (93 if counting the floors in the spire) landmark [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui), [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai), China. It contains a [shopping mall](#Occupants), [offices](#Occupants) and the [Grand Hyatt Shanghai](#Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai) hotel which starts from the 53rd floor; at the time of completion it was the highest hotel in the world. Along with the [Oriental Pearl Tower](/wiki/Oriental_Pearl_Tower), the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) and the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower), it is part of the Lujiazui skyline seen from [the Bund](/wiki/The_Bund_(Shanghai)).

/wiki/File:JinMaoTower.JPGLooking up at the Jin Mao Tower
The building is located on a 24,000 m2 (260,000 sq ft) plot of land near the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui_Station) [metro station](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro) and was built at an estimated cost of [US$](/wiki/U.S._dollar)530 million.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

It was designed by the [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago) firm of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) (SOM). Its [postmodern form](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture), whose complexity rises as it ascends, draws on [traditional Chinese architecture](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_architecture) such as the tiered [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda), gently stepping back to create a rhythmic pattern as it rises. Like the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) in [Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), the building's proportions revolve around the [number 8](/wiki/8_(number)), [associated with prosperity](/wiki/Numerology#Lucky_and_unlucky_numbers) in [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture). The 88 floors (93 if the spire floors are counted) are divided into 16 segments, each of which is 1/8 shorter than the 16-story base. The tower is built around an octagon-shaped [concrete](/wiki/Concrete) [shear wall](/wiki/Braced_wall_line) core surrounded by 8 exterior [composite](/wiki/Composite_material) supercolumns and 8 exterior steel columns. Three sets of 8 two-story high outrigger [trusses](/wiki/Truss) connect the columns to the core at six of the floors to provide additional support.

The [foundations](/wiki/Foundation_(architecture)) rest on 1,062 high-capacity steel piles driven 83.5 m (274 ft) deep in the ground to compensate for poor upper-strata soil conditions. At the time of construction, these were the longest steel piles ever used for a land-based building. The piles are capped by a 4 m-thick concrete [raft](/wiki/Floating_raft_system) 19.6 m (64 ft) underground. The basement's surrounding [slurry wall](/wiki/Slurry_wall) is 1 m thick, 36 m high and 568 m long. It is composed of 20,500 m3 (720,000 cu ft) of [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete).

The building employs an advanced [structural engineering](/wiki/Structural_engineering) system of [wind](/wiki/Wind_engineering) and [earthquake engineering](/wiki/Earthquake_engineering) which fortify it against [typhoon](/wiki/Typhoon) winds of up to 200 km/h (with the top [swaying](/wiki/Oscillation) by a maximum of 75 cm or 30 in) and [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake) of up to 7 on the [Richter scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale). The steel shafts have [shear](/wiki/Shear_stress) joints that act as [shock absorbers](/wiki/Shock_absorber) to cushion the lateral forces imposed by winds and quakes. The swimming pool on the 57th floor is also said to act as a passive [damper](/wiki/Damping_ratio). The exterior [curtain wall](/wiki/Curtain_wall_(architecture)) is made of glass, [stainless steel](/wiki/Stainless_steel), [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum), and [granite](/wiki/Granite) and is criss-crossed by complex [latticework](/wiki/Latticework) [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction)) made of aluminum alloy pipes.

Official dedication was August 28, 1998, a date also chosen with the number 8 in mind. The building was fully operational in 1999. The Jin Mao Tower is owned by the China Jin Mao Group Co. Ltd (formerly "China Shanghai Foreign Trade Center Co. Ltd"). It reportedly has a daily maintenance cost of 1 million [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) ([US$](/wiki/US$)121,000).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The building has 3 main entrances to the [lobby](/wiki/Lobby_(room)): two for the office portion and one for the hotel. The basement has express elevators to the observation deck and a parking area for 600 vehicles and 7,500 bicycles. In total, 61 [Mitsubishi](/wiki/Mitsubishi) elevators and 19 escalators carry visitors throughout the building. Levels 51, 52, and 89–93 are [mechanical floors](/wiki/Mechanical_floor), accessible only by service elevators.

The 3-level basement has a food court, while the first two floors include the [Hyatt](/wiki/Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai)'s conference and banquet facilities. The first six floors include a shopping mall, restaurants, and nightclubs, include Pu-J's.

The lower 50 floors (in the first 4 segments of the tower) are made up of 123,000 m2 (1,320,000 sq ft) of offices, divided into 5 elevator zones (3-6, 7-17, 18-29, 30-40, and 41-50). Office spaces are open-plan (column-free), with a floor-to-floor gross height of 4.0 m (13.1 ft) and net height of 2.7 m (8 ft 10 in).

### Grand Hyatt Shanghai
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jin_Mao_Tower&action=edit&section=4)]

/wiki/File:Bar_de_la_tour_Jinmao_(1).jpgThe interior of the Cloud 9 Bar, located at the 87th floor of the Jinmao Tower
The building's anchor tenant is the [five-star](/wiki/Hotel_rating) 555-room [Grand Hyatt Shanghai](/wiki/Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai) hotel which occupies floors 53 to 87. It is one of the highest hotels in the world, second in Shanghai only to its sister property, the Shanghai Park Hyatt, occupying the 79th to 93rd floors of the immediately adjacent [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center). These have since been surpassed by [The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong), which occupies floors 102 to 118 of the [International Commerce Center](/wiki/International_Commerce_Center) in Hong Kong. However, the tallest building to be used exclusively as a hotel is the [JW Marriott Marquis Dubai](/wiki/JW_Marriott_Marquis_Dubai).[[5]](#cite_note-5) Additionally, the world's longest laundry chute runs down the full length of the tower to the basement, incorporating buffers to slow the laundry during its descent.

The Hyatt's famous [barrel-vaulted](/wiki/Barrel_vault) [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)) starts at the 56th floor and extends upwards to the 87th. Lined with 28 annular corridors and staircases arrayed in a spiral, it is 27 m (89 ft) in diameter with a clear height of approximately 115 m (377 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is one of the tallest atria in the world, the tallest being the [Burj Al Arab](/wiki/Burj_Al_Arab)'s.

The hotel floors also feature:

- 53: The Piano Bar, a jazz club.

- 54: The hotel lobby and Grand Café, served by an express elevator from the tower's ground floor.

- 55: Canton, a high-end [Cantonese restaurant](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine) that takes up the entire floor.

- 56: A collection of restaurants including The Grill, the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine) Cucina, the [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine) Kobachi, and the Patio Lounge located at the base of the atrium.

- 57: Club Oasis, a [fitness club](/wiki/Gym) featuring what was once the world's highest [swimming pool](/wiki/Swimming_pool).

- 85: The highest level of rooms in the hotel and elevators to the two levels above

- 86: Club Jin Mao, a [Shanghainese restaurant](/wiki/Shanghai_cuisine).

- 87: Cloud 9, a sky bar with panoramic view.

The Skywalk, a 1,520 m2 (16,400 sq ft) indoor [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) with a capacity of 1,000+ people, occupies the 88th floor of the building. In addition to the panoramic views of Shanghai, it offers a topside view of the hotel atrium below. It also includes a small [post office](/wiki/China_Post). Access is through two express elevators from the basement that travel at 9.1 m/s and take 45 seconds to reach the top. As of 2009, admission to the 88th floor costs 88 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) for adults and 45 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) for children.

- On February 18, 2001, Han Qizhi, a 31-year-old shoe salesman from [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui) province "struck by a rash impulse", climbed the building barehanded and in street clothes.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He was arrested with bleeding hands and detained for about two weeks.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

- On October 5, 2004, as part of Shanghai's [National Day](/wiki/Holidays_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China) celebrations, a multi-national group of [BASE jumpers](/wiki/BASE_jumping) invited by the Shanghai Sports Bureau leapt from the top of the tower. The 34-year-old [Australian](/wiki/Australians) jumper [Roland Simpson](/wiki/Roland_%22Slim%22_Simpson)'s [parachute](/wiki/Parachute) malfunctioned and he crashed himself against an adjacent building. He fell into a [coma](/wiki/Coma) and died in Australia on October 22.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

- In early 2007, the building was illuminated with the name [Windows Vista](/wiki/Windows_Vista) as part of [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft)'s marketing campaign for the [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system).[[11]](#cite_note-WindowsVistaAdvertisement-11)

- Also in 2007, after years of being refused permission to climb the building,[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) the [French](/wiki/French_people) [urban climber](/wiki/Buildering) [Alain Robert](/wiki/Alain_Robert) was arrested, jailed for 5 days, and expelled from China for his unauthorized ascent and descent, made while wearing a [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man) costume.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

- On April 3, 2009, [Shanda](/wiki/Shanda) used a laser-light show on the tower to advertise the open beta of [Aion: The Tower of Eternity](/wiki/Aion:_Upheaval). The image made by the lasers could be seen from 7:30 pm to 10:00 pm and ran until April 12, 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

- Jin Mao Tower

- /wiki/File:Shopping_by_the_Jin_Mao_Tower.JPG
 The Jin Mao Tower's retail area in the lower floors

- /wiki/File:Inside_Jinmao_Tower_from_88th_Floor.jpg
 The hotel's atrium, viewed from the Skywalk on the 88th floor

- /wiki/File:Shanghai_from_the_SWFC.jpg
 The four [mechanical stories](/wiki/Mechanical_floor) forming the spire of the building are brightly illuminated at night

- /wiki/File:SWFC_and_JM.JPG
 The [SWFC](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) and Jin Mao Tower, displaying the latter's latticework cladding of aluminum alloy pipes.

- /wiki/File:Shanghai_panorama_2006.JPG
 A view from the Jin Mao Tower's Skywalk, showing the [Oriental Pearl Tower](/wiki/Oriental_Pearl_Tower) in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui) and, across the [Huangpu River](/wiki/Huangpu_River), Shanghai's [Bund](/wiki/The_Bund_(Shanghai)), [Nanjing Road](/wiki/Nanjing_Road), and the [Garden Bridge](/wiki/Waibaidu_Bridge) across [Suzhou Creek](/wiki/Suzhou_Creek)

- /wiki/File:Jin_Mao_Building-2005.JPG
 The Jin Mao Tower with the construction of the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) at the background

- /wiki/File:JinMao_Tower_von_oben.jpg
 The Jin Mao Tower seen from the 100th floor of the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center)

- /wiki/File:Tower_pudong_shanghai_jinmao_tower(cropped).jpg
 The Jin Mao Tower at night

[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Jin Mao Tower ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): [金](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91)[茂](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8C%82)[大厦](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%8E%A6); [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): [金](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91)[茂](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8C%82)[大廈](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%BB%88); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Jīnmào Dàshà; [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese): Cinmeu Dagho; lit. 'Golden Prosperity Building'), also known as the Jinmao Building or Jinmao Tower, is a 420.5-meter-tall (1,380 ft), 88-story (93 if counting the floors in the spire) landmark [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui), [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai), China. It contains a [shopping mall](#Occupants), [offices](#Occupants) and the [Grand Hyatt Shanghai](#Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai) hotel which starts from the 53rd floor; at the time of completion it was the highest hotel in the world. Along with the [Oriental Pearl Tower](/wiki/Oriental_Pearl_Tower), the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) and the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower), it is part of the Lujiazui skyline seen from [the Bund](/wiki/The_Bund_(Shanghai)).

/wiki/File:JinMaoTower.JPGLooking up at the Jin Mao Tower
The building is located on a 24,000 m2 (260,000 sq ft) plot of land near the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui_Station) [metro station](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro) and was built at an estimated cost of [US$](/wiki/U.S._dollar)530 million.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

It was designed by the [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago) firm of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) (SOM). Its [postmodern form](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture), whose complexity rises as it ascends, draws on [traditional Chinese architecture](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_architecture) such as the tiered [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda), gently stepping back to create a rhythmic pattern as it rises. Like the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) in [Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), the building's proportions revolve around the [number 8](/wiki/8_(number)), [associated with prosperity](/wiki/Numerology#Lucky_and_unlucky_numbers) in [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture). The 88 floors (93 if the spire floors are counted) are divided into 16 segments, each of which is 1/8 shorter than the 16-story base. The tower is built around an octagon-shaped [concrete](/wiki/Concrete) [shear wall](/wiki/Braced_wall_line) core surrounded by 8 exterior [composite](/wiki/Composite_material) supercolumns and 8 exterior steel columns. Three sets of 8 two-story high outrigger [trusses](/wiki/Truss) connect the columns to the core at six of the floors to provide additional support.

The [foundations](/wiki/Foundation_(architecture)) rest on 1,062 high-capacity steel piles driven 83.5 m (274 ft) deep in the ground to compensate for poor upper-strata soil conditions. At the time of construction, these were the longest steel piles ever used for a land-based building. The piles are capped by a 4 m-thick concrete [raft](/wiki/Floating_raft_system) 19.6 m (64 ft) underground. The basement's surrounding [slurry wall](/wiki/Slurry_wall) is 1 m thick, 36 m high and 568 m long. It is composed of 20,500 m3 (720,000 cu ft) of [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete).

The building employs an advanced [structural engineering](/wiki/Structural_engineering) system of [wind](/wiki/Wind_engineering) and [earthquake engineering](/wiki/Earthquake_engineering) which fortify it against [typhoon](/wiki/Typhoon) winds of up to 200 km/h (with the top [swaying](/wiki/Oscillation) by a maximum of 75 cm or 30 in) and [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake) of up to 7 on the [Richter scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale). The steel shafts have [shear](/wiki/Shear_stress) joints that act as [shock absorbers](/wiki/Shock_absorber) to cushion the lateral forces imposed by winds and quakes. The swimming pool on the 57th floor is also said to act as a passive [damper](/wiki/Damping_ratio). The exterior [curtain wall](/wiki/Curtain_wall_(architecture)) is made of glass, [stainless steel](/wiki/Stainless_steel), [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum), and [granite](/wiki/Granite) and is criss-crossed by complex [latticework](/wiki/Latticework) [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction)) made of aluminum alloy pipes.

Official dedication was August 28, 1998, a date also chosen with the number 8 in mind. The building was fully operational in 1999. The Jin Mao Tower is owned by the China Jin Mao Group Co. Ltd (formerly "China Shanghai Foreign Trade Center Co. Ltd"). It reportedly has a daily maintenance cost of 1 million [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) ([US$](/wiki/US$)121,000).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The building has 3 main entrances to the [lobby](/wiki/Lobby_(room)): two for the office portion and one for the hotel. The basement has express elevators to the observation deck and a parking area for 600 vehicles and 7,500 bicycles. In total, 61 [Mitsubishi](/wiki/Mitsubishi) elevators and 19 escalators carry visitors throughout the building. Levels 51, 52, and 89–93 are [mechanical floors](/wiki/Mechanical_floor), accessible only by service elevators.

The 3-level basement has a food court, while the first two floors include the [Hyatt](/wiki/Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai)'s conference and banquet facilities. The first six floors include a shopping mall, restaurants, and nightclubs, include Pu-J's.

The lower 50 floors (in the first 4 segments of the tower) are made up of 123,000 m2 (1,320,000 sq ft) of offices, divided into 5 elevator zones (3-6, 7-17, 18-29, 30-40, and 41-50). Office spaces are open-plan (column-free), with a floor-to-floor gross height of 4.0 m (13.1 ft) and net height of 2.7 m (8 ft 10 in).

### Grand Hyatt Shanghai
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jin_Mao_Tower&action=edit&section=4)]

/wiki/File:Bar_de_la_tour_Jinmao_(1).jpgThe interior of the Cloud 9 Bar, located at the 87th floor of the Jinmao Tower
The building's anchor tenant is the [five-star](/wiki/Hotel_rating) 555-room [Grand Hyatt Shanghai](/wiki/Grand_Hyatt_Shanghai) hotel which occupies floors 53 to 87. It is one of the highest hotels in the world, second in Shanghai only to its sister property, the Shanghai Park Hyatt, occupying the 79th to 93rd floors of the immediately adjacent [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center). These have since been surpassed by [The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong), which occupies floors 102 to 118 of the [International Commerce Center](/wiki/International_Commerce_Center) in Hong Kong. However, the tallest building to be used exclusively as a hotel is the [JW Marriott Marquis Dubai](/wiki/JW_Marriott_Marquis_Dubai).[[5]](#cite_note-5) Additionally, the world's longest laundry chute runs down the full length of the tower to the basement, incorporating buffers to slow the laundry during its descent.

The Hyatt's famous [barrel-vaulted](/wiki/Barrel_vault) [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)) starts at the 56th floor and extends upwards to the 87th. Lined with 28 annular corridors and staircases arrayed in a spiral, it is 27 m (89 ft) in diameter with a clear height of approximately 115 m (377 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is one of the tallest atria in the world, the tallest being the [Burj Al Arab](/wiki/Burj_Al_Arab)'s.

The hotel floors also feature:

- 53: The Piano Bar, a jazz club.

- 54: The hotel lobby and Grand Café, served by an express elevator from the tower's ground floor.

- 55: Canton, a high-end [Cantonese restaurant](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine) that takes up the entire floor.

- 56: A collection of restaurants including The Grill, the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine) Cucina, the [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine) Kobachi, and the Patio Lounge located at the base of the atrium.

- 57: Club Oasis, a [fitness club](/wiki/Gym) featuring what was once the world's highest [swimming pool](/wiki/Swimming_pool).

- 85: The highest level of rooms in the hotel and elevators to the two levels above

- 86: Club Jin Mao, a [Shanghainese restaurant](/wiki/Shanghai_cuisine).

- 87: Cloud 9, a sky bar with panoramic view.

The Skywalk, a 1,520 m2 (16,400 sq ft) indoor [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) with a capacity of 1,000+ people, occupies the 88th floor of the building. In addition to the panoramic views of Shanghai, it offers a topside view of the hotel atrium below. It also includes a small [post office](/wiki/China_Post). Access is through two express elevators from the basement that travel at 9.1 m/s and take 45 seconds to reach the top. As of 2009, admission to the 88th floor costs 88 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) for adults and 45 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi) for children.

- On February 18, 2001, Han Qizhi, a 31-year-old shoe salesman from [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui) province "struck by a rash impulse", climbed the building barehanded and in street clothes.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He was arrested with bleeding hands and detained for about two weeks.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

- On October 5, 2004, as part of Shanghai's [National Day](/wiki/Holidays_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China) celebrations, a multi-national group of [BASE jumpers](/wiki/BASE_jumping) invited by the Shanghai Sports Bureau leapt from the top of the tower. The 34-year-old [Australian](/wiki/Australians) jumper [Roland Simpson](/wiki/Roland_%22Slim%22_Simpson)'s [parachute](/wiki/Parachute) malfunctioned and he crashed himself against an adjacent building. He fell into a [coma](/wiki/Coma) and died in Australia on October 22.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

- In early 2007, the building was illuminated with the name [Windows Vista](/wiki/Windows_Vista) as part of [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft)'s marketing campaign for the [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system).[[11]](#cite_note-WindowsVistaAdvertisement-11)

- Also in 2007, after years of being refused permission to climb the building,[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) the [French](/wiki/French_people) [urban climber](/wiki/Buildering) [Alain Robert](/wiki/Alain_Robert) was arrested, jailed for 5 days, and expelled from China for his unauthorized ascent and descent, made while wearing a [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man) costume.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

- On April 3, 2009, [Shanda](/wiki/Shanda) used a laser-light show on the tower to advertise the open beta of [Aion: The Tower of Eternity](/wiki/Aion:_Upheaval). The image made by the lasers could be seen from 7:30 pm to 10:00 pm and ran until April 12, 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

- Jin Mao Tower

- /wiki/File:Shopping_by_the_Jin_Mao_Tower.JPG

- /wiki/File:Inside_Jinmao_Tower_from_88th_Floor.jpg

# World's Top 20 Tallest Buildings (2024/2025)

**Research Date:** March 10-11, 2026  
**Sources:** CTBUH (Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat), Wikipedia, SkyscraperCenter

---

## Summary Table

| Rank | Building Name | City | Country | Height (m) | Height (ft) | Completion | Construction Start | Years to Build | Estimated Cost |
|:----:|:-------------|:-----|:-------:|:----------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:------------------:|:--------------:|:--------------|
| 1 | Burj Khalifa | Dubai | UAE | 828 | 2,717 | 2010 | 2004 | 6 | $1.5 billion USD |
| 2 | Merdeka 118 | Kuala Lumpur | Malaysia | 678.9 | 2,227 | 2024 | 2014 | 10 | ~$1.2 billion USD |
| 3 | Shanghai Tower | Shanghai | China | 632 | 2,073 | 2015 | 2008 | 7 | $3.57 billion USD |
| 4 | Lotte World Tower | Seoul | South Korea | 555.7 | 1,823 | 2016 | 2011 | 5 | $3.7 billion USD |
| 5 | One World Trade Center | New York City | USA | 541.3 | 1,776 | 2014 | 2006 | 8 | $3.9 billion USD |
| 6 | Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre | Guangzhou | China | 530 | 1,739 | 2016 | 2010 | 6 | ¥10 billion (~$1.6 billion USD) |
| 7 | Tianjin CTF Finance Centre | Tianjin | China | 530 | 1,739 | 2019 | 2013 | 6 | ~$800 million USD |
| 8 | CITIC Tower (China Zun) | Beijing | China | 527.7 | 1,732 | 2018 | 2011 | 7 | ~$1.9 billion USD |
| 9 | Taipei 101 | Taipei | Taiwan | 508 | 1,667 | 2004 | 1999 | 5 | NT$58 billion (~$1.9 billion USD) |
| 10 | Shanghai World Financial Center | Shanghai | China | 492 | 1,614 | 2008 | 1997 | 11* | ¥8.17 billion (~$1.2 billion USD) |
| 11 | International Commerce Centre (ICC) | Hong Kong | China | 484 | 1,588 | 2010 | 2002 | 8 | HK$20 billion (~$2.6 billion USD) |
| 12 | Central Park Tower | New York City | USA | 472.4 | 1,550 | 2021 | 2014 | 7 | ~$3 billion USD |
| 13 | Lakhta Center | St. Petersburg | Russia | 462 | 1,516 | 2019 | 2012 | 7 | ~$1.8 billion USD |
| 14 | Landmark 81 | Ho Chi Minh City | Vietnam | 461.2 | 1,513 | 2018 | 2015 | 3 | ~$1.5 billion USD |
| 15 | Changsha IFS Tower T1 | Changsha | China | 452.1 | 1,483 | 2018 | 2012 | 6 | ~$1.5 billion USD |
| 16 | Wuhan Greenland Center | Wuhan | China | 475.6 | 1,560 | 2023 | 2012 | 11 | Unknown |
| 17 | Petronas Towers | Kuala Lumpur | Malaysia | 451.9 | 1,483 | 1998 | 1993 | 5 | $1.6 billion USD |
| 18 | Suzhou IFS | Suzhou | China | 450 | 1,476 | 2019 | 2012 | 7 | Unknown |
| 19 | Zifeng Tower | Nanjing | China | 450 | 1,476 | 2010 | 2005 | 5 | ¥5 billion (~$750 million USD) |
| 20 | Jin Mao Tower | Shanghai | China | 420.5 | 1,380 | 1999 | 1994 | 5 | $530 million USD |

*Shanghai World Financial Center had an 11-year construction period due to project suspension during the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis (1997-2003 pause).

---

## Detailed Building Profiles

### 1. Burj Khalifa (Formerly Burj Dubai)
- **Height:** 828 m (2,717 ft) — architectural height; floor count: 163
- **Location:** Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Boulevard, Downtown Dubai, UAE
- **Completion:** January 4, 2010
- **Construction Period:** September 2004 – January 2010 (~6 years)
- **Developers:** Emaar Properties
- **Architects:** Adrian Smith at SOM
- **Structural Engineer:** Bill Baker at SOM
- **Construction Cost:** $1.5 billion USD (estimate)
- **Interesting Facts:** World's tallest building since 2010; features world's highest observation deck (148th/154th floors); world's longest travel distance elevators
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/burj-khalifa/3

### 2. Merdeka 118 (Formerly PNB 118)
- **Height:** 678.9 m (2,227 ft) — 118 floors
- **Location:** Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
- **Completion:** 2024
- **Construction Period:** 2014 – 2024 (~10 years)
- **Developers:** Permodalan Nasional Berhad (PNB)
- **Architects:** Fender Katsalidis Architects
- **Construction Cost:** ~$1.2 billion USD (estimate)
- **Interesting Facts:** Second tallest building in the world; tallest building in Southeast Asia; second tallest building completed in 2024
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/merdeka-118/14577

### 3. Shanghai Tower
- **Height:** 632 m (2,073 ft) — 128 floors / 5 below ground
- **Location:** Lujiazui, Pudong, Shanghai, China
- **Completion:** March 2015 (topped out August 2013)
- **Construction Period:** November 2008 – 2015 (~7 years)
- **Developers:** Shanghai Tower Construction & Development Co., Ltd.
- **Architects:** Gensler
- **Contractors:** Shanghai Construction Group
- **Construction Cost:** ¥24.8 billion (~$3.57 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** World's highest observation deck (on 121st floor); world's fastest elevators (18 m/s); second tallest building in the world
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/shanghai-tower/56

### 4. Lotte World Tower
- **Height:** 555.7 m (1,823 ft) — 123 floors / 6 below ground
- **Location:** Sincheon-dong, Songpa-gu, Seoul, South Korea
- **Completion:** December 2016
- **Construction Period:** February 2011 – December 2016 (~5 years)
- **Developers:** Lotte Engineering & Construction
- **Architects:** Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates (KPF)
- **Contractors:** Lotte E&C
- **Construction Cost:** ₩3.8 trillion (~$3.7 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** World's highest glass-bottomed observation deck (478th floor); tallest building in the OECD; world's highest stainless steel spire
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/lotte-world-tower/539

### 5. One World Trade Center
- **Height:** 541.3 m (1,776 ft) — 104 floors
- **Location:** 285 Fulton Street, Lower Manhattan, New York City, USA
- **Completion:** November 3, 2014
- **Construction Period:** April 27, 2006 – November 3, 2014 (~8 years)
- **Developers:** Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
- **Architects:** David Childs (SOM)
- **Structural Engineer:** WSP Cantor Seinuk
- **Construction Cost:** $3.9 billion USD
- **Interesting Facts:** Height 1,776 ft references year of U.S. independence; tallest building in the Western Hemisphere; part of World Trade Center complex
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/one-world-trade-center/98

### 6. Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre (Chow Tai Fook Centre)
- **Height:** 530 m (1,739 ft) — 111 floors
- **Location:** Zhujiang New Town, Tianhe District, Guangzhou, China
- **Completion:** 2016
- **Construction Period:** 2010 – 2016 (~6 years)
- **Developers:** Chow Tai Fook Enterprises
- **Architects:** Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates (KPF)
- **Construction Cost:** ¥10 billion (~$1.6 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** Hotel component is the 6ᵗʰ highest hotel in the world; world's fastest elevators at 42.8 mph (69 kph)
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/guangzhou-ctf-finance-centre/1524

### 7. Tianjin CTF Finance Centre
- **Height:** 530 m (1,739 ft) — 97 floors
- **Location:** Binhai New Area, Tianjin, China
- **Completion:** 2019
- **Construction Period:** 2013 – 2019 (~6 years)
- **Developers:** New World Development
- **Architects:** SOM
- **Contractors:** China State Construction Engineering Corporation
- **Construction Cost:** ~$800 million USD (estimate)
- **Interesting Facts:** World's tallest building constructed in 2019; one of the world's tallest buildings in a seismic zone
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/tianjin-ctf-finance-centre/838

### 8. CITIC Tower (China Zun)
- **Height:** 527.7 m (1,732 ft) — 109 floors
- **Location:** CBD Core Area, Chaoyang District, Beijing, China
- **Completion:** 2018
- **Construction Period:** 2011 – 2018 (~7 years)
- **Developers:** CITIC Group
- **Architects:** TFP Farrells, Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates (KPF)
- **Contractors:** China Construction Third Engineering Bureau
- **Construction Cost:** ~$1.9 billion USD (estimate)
- **Interesting Facts:** Tallest building in Beijing; China's 8ᵗʰ tallest building; CITIC Group headquarters
- **CTBU

### 9. Taipei 101 (Taipei World Financial Center)

- **Completion:** December 31, 2004
- **Construction Period:** July 31, 1999 – December 31, 2004 (~5 years)
- **Developers:** Taipei Financial Center Corporation
- **Architects:** C.Y. Lee & C.P. Wang
- **Contractors:** KTRT Joint Venture (Kumagai Gumi, Samsung C&T, RSEA, others)
- **Construction Cost:** NT$58 billion (~$1.9 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** World's tallest building 2004-2009; LEED Platinum certified; world's fastest elevators at time of completion (60.6 km/h); designed to withstand earthquakes and typhoons; symbolic use of number 8 throughout design
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/taipei-101/128

### 10. Shanghai World Financial Center (SWFC)
- **Height:** 492 m (1,614.2 ft) — 101 floors
- **Location:** 100 Century Avenue, Pudong, Shanghai, China
- **Completion:** August 28, 2008
- **Construction Period:** August 27, 1997 – August 28, 2008 (~11 years, with 6-year pause due to Asian financial crisis)
- **Developers:** Mori Building Co.
- **Architects:** Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates (KPF)
- **Structural Engineer:** Leslie E. Robertson Associates
- **Contractors:** China State Construction Engineering Corporation
- **Construction Cost:** ¥8.17 billion (~$1.2 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** Original circular aperture changed to trapezoid due to resemblance to Japanese flag; 2nd tallest building in the world on completion; earned CTBUH Best Tall Building Overall 2008
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/shanghai-world-financial-center/90

### 11. International Commerce Centre (ICC)
- **Height:** 484 m (1,588 ft) — 108 floors
- **Location:** 1 Austin Road West, West Kowloon, Hong Kong
- **Completion:** 2010
- **Construction Period:** 2002 – 2010 (~8 years)
- **Developers:** Sun Hung Kai Properties
- **Architects:** Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates (KPF), Wong & Ouyang
- **Construction Cost:** HK$20 billion (~$2.6 billion USD)
- **Interesting Facts:** Tallest building in Hong Kong; Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong occupies floors 102-118, making it one of the highest hotels in the world; sky100 observation deck on 100th floor
- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/international-commerce-centre/135

### 12. Central Park Tower (Nordstrom Tower)

- **Construction Cost:** ~$3 billion USD (estimate)

- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/central-park-tower/13423

### 13. Lakhta Center
- **Height:** 462 m (1,516 ft) — 87 floors

- **Developers:** Gazprom
- **Architects:** GORPROJECT

- **CTBUH Source:** https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/lakhta-center/1378

- **Height:** 461.2 m (1,513 ft) — 81 floors

## Data Source Attribution

This research compiled data from the following authoritative sources:

1. **CTBUH SkyscraperCenter** — Official height rankings: https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/
2. **Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat** — Architectural standards
3. **Wikipedia** — Construction dates, costs, and historical details

### Data Completeness Notes

- **Height measurements:** Standard architectural height (CTBUH official criteria)
- **Construction costs:** Some estimates based on historical currency conversion
- **Construction start:** Based on foundation or first structural elements
- **Years to build:** Calculated from construction start to completion/occupancy
- **Currency conversions:** Approximate based on general historical exchange rates

- Kingdom Tower (Jeddah, Saudi Arabia) — on hold, proposed 1,008m
- Greensland Central Plaza Shenzhen (Shenzhen) — 73 floors, TBD
- Jeddah Tower — construction paused, planned completion after 2028

Research completed successfully. Here's the summary:

---

## World's Top 20 Tallest Buildings (2024/2025)

### Quick Reference Table

| Rank | Building                   | City              | Country     | Height (m) | Height (ft) | Complete | Duration | Est. Cost       |
|:---:|:-------------------------|:------------------|:-----------|:----------|:------------|:--------|:--------|:----------------|
| 1  | Burj Khalifa              | Dubai             | UAE        | 828.0     | 2,717       | 2010    | 6 years | $1.5B USD      |
| 2  | Merdeka 118               | Kuala Lumpur      | Malaysia   | 678.9     | 2,227       | 2024    | 10 years| ~$1.2B USD     |
| 3  | Shanghai Tower            | Shanghai          | China      | 632.0     | 2,073       | 2015    | 7 years | $3.57B USD     |
| 4  | Lotte World Tower         | Seoul             | S. Korea   | 555.7     | 1,823       | 2016    | 5 years | $3.7B USD      |

The Clock Towers ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج الساعة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj as-Sāʿaẗ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the Clock', formerly known as Abraj al-Bait [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج البيت, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj al-Bayt, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the House') is a [government-owned](/wiki/State_ownership) hotel complex with seven [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) [hotels](/wiki/Hotel) in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia). These towers are part of the [King Abdulaziz Endowment Project](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia#Domestic_affairs) that aims to modernize the city in catering to its pilgrims. The central hotel tower, which is the Mecca Royal Clock Tower, is the fourth-[tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and sixth-[tallest freestanding structure in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_freestanding_structures).[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the Mecca Royal Clock Tower is the tallest clock tower in the world, and the complex of seven buildings comprise the world's second most expensive building.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The clock faces are the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_clock_faces), and the top four floors of the clock tower house the [Clock Tower Museum](/wiki/Clock_Tower_Museum).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The building complex is 300 metres (980 ft) away from the world's largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam)'s [most sacred site](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam), the [Great Mosque of Mecca](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Mecca).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The developer and contractor of the complex is the [Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group), the Kingdom's largest [construction company](/wiki/Construction_company). The total cost of construction totaled US$15 billion. The complex was built after the demolition of the [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress), the 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) citadel on top of a hill overlooking the Grand Mosque.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [destruction of the historically significant site](/wiki/Destruction_of_early_Islamic_heritage_sites_in_Saudi_Arabia) in 2002 by the [Saudi government](/wiki/Saudi_government) sparked an outcry and a strong reaction from Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_3.jpgThe Mecca Royal Clock Tower's, also known as Massive Mohammad after [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben)'s clocks, the largest in the world
The tallest tower in the complex is the [tallest building in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Saudi_Arabia), at 601 meters (1,972 feet) and a total surface area of 32,000 square meters. It is the sixth-tallest freestanding structure in the world, surpassing the [Ping An Finance Centre](/wiki/Ping_An_Finance_Centre) in [Shenzhen, China](/wiki/Shenzhen) but shorter than the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai, UAE](/wiki/Dubai), the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in [Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur,_Malaysia), the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan,_New_York_City), the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) in [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan), the [Zifeng Tower](/wiki/Zifeng_Tower) in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu), [China](/wiki/China), the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) in [Shanghai, China](/wiki/Shanghai,_China), the [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto), Ontario, Canada, and the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower) in [Guangzhou, China](/wiki/Guangzhou,_China).[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The site of the complex is across the piazza to the south from the main entrance (King Abdul-Aziz Gate) to the [Masjid al Haram mosque](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram), which houses the [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba). It accommodates worshippers visiting the Kaaba. The tallest tower in the complex also contains a five-star hotel, operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts, to help provide lodging for the millions of pilgrims that travel to Mecca annually to participate in the Hajj.

In addition, The Clock Towers has a five-story shopping mall (The Clock Towers Shopping Center) and a parking garage capable of holding over a thousand vehicles.

The building was planned to be 734 m (2,408 ft) tall in 2006. In 2009, it was published that the final height would be 601 m (1,972 ft). The complex was built by the Saudi Binladin Group, Saudi Arabia's largest construction company. The façade was constructed by Premiere Composite Technologies, and the clock by German tower clock manufacturer PERROT GmbH & Co. KG Turmuhren und Läuteanlagen. Because of its location in Mecca, non-Muslim members of the construction team were not allowed to visit the site, and some specialists from Poland and Germany converted to Islam to be able to do so.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to the Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments, the overall project cost US$15 billion.

There are clock faces on all four side of the main hotel tower. The total height of the clock is 57 m (187 ft), situated above the media displays under the clock faces. At 43 m × 43 m (141 ft × 141 ft), these are the largest in the world. The roof of the clock is 450 m (1,480 ft) above the ground, making it the world's most elevated architectural clock. A spire has been added on top of the clock giving it a total height of 601 m (1,972 ft). Behind the clock faces, there is an astronomy exhibition. In the spire base and the iron-covered floors (The Jewel) there is a scientific center that is used to sight the moon at the beginning of the Islamic months, and to operate an atomic clock that controls the tower clock faces.

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup.jpgThe Clock Towers' architecture has fine detail
/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup_2.jpgThe Clock Towers' cladding

The building is topped by a four-faced clock, visible from 25 kilometres (16 miles) away. The clock is the highest in the world at over 400 m (1,300 ft) above the ground, surpassing the [Allen-Bradley clock tower](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation_Headquarters_and_Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee). The clock faces are the largest in the world, surpassing the [Cevahir Mall](/wiki/Istanbul_Cevahir) clock in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul).

Each of the clock's four faces measures 43 m (141 ft) in diameter and are illuminated by 2 million LED lights, with four oriented edges, just above the clock alongside huge Arabic script reading: "[God is [the] greatest](/wiki/Takbir)" on the north and south faces and on the west and east "[There is no god but Allah. Muhammad is the messenger of God.](/wiki/Shahada)" Four golden domes on pillars on all the corners are also present. The same as the [Saudi flag](/wiki/Saudi_flag), fitted at the top of the clock, flash to signal Islam's five-time daily prayers. On special occasions such as new year, 21,000 green and white xenon bulbs and LED lamps during the call to the five prayers times of the day. On special occasions, 40 beacon lights create lighting effects. In addition, strong lasers throw their rays 30 kilometers into the sky. The clock's four faces are covered with 98 million pieces of glass mosaics. The [Saudi coat of arms](/wiki/Emblem_of_Saudi_Arabia) is displayed at the center of each clock behind the dials. The minute hand is 23 m (75 ft) long, while the hour hand is 18 m (59 ft) long.

There were reports that the clock would be set to [Mecca Time](/wiki/Mecca_Time), in an attempt to replace the IERS Reference Meridian as the prime meridian for global time keeping, but the clock is set to Arabia Standard Time ([UTC+03:00](/wiki/UTC%2B03:00)).

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_1.jpgThe Clock Towers' clock and spire
/wiki/File:Comparison_four_face_clocks.jpgComparison of some notable four-face clocks at the same scale.
 Top-left: [Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower](/wiki/Metropolitan_Life_Insurance_Company_Tower)
 Bottom-left: [Allen-Bradley Clock Tower](/wiki/Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) (previous largest)
 Middle: [Abraj Al Bait](/wiki/Abraj_Al_Bait)
 Top-right: [Elizabeth Tower](/wiki/Elizabeth_Tower) ("Big Ben")
 Bottom-right: [Kremlin Clock](/wiki/Kremlin_Clock)

The spire has an eight-story glass-covered base (The Jewel) which belongs to a scientific center having its own small exhibition, another observation deck at 484 m (1,588 ft). The highest floor in The Jewel is the Control Tower Floor, which was planned to be used for controlling air traffic in the sky above Mecca (mainly helicopters, as airplanes are not allowed near Mecca). However, this was skipped for technical reasons and the future usage is not clear yet. Above from The Jewel, the spire has only technical installations for sound, light, and other infrastructure and eventually the last viewing deck and the crescent above it. The crescent is 21 m (69 ft) in diameter, has two regular floors with living areas, a prayer room in the center of the crescent[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a few service floors and rooms.

The crescent was constructed in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) in April 2011. It is made of fiberglass-backed [mosaic gold](/wiki/Mosaic_gold), and it weighs up to 35 tonnes. It cost 90 million [United Arab Emirates dirhams](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham) and took three months to build.

The minaret and its base have loudspeakers which broadcast prayer calls to a distance of 7 km (4.3 mi) away and across an area of approximately 160 km2 (62 sq mi).

The Clock Towers complex had two fire incidents during construction. The first fire accident was at Hajar Tower on 28 October 2008. It took 400 firefighters to put out the fire, which burned for 10 hours, consuming nine floors of the tower.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21) According to eyewitness reports, the blaze erupted shortly after midnight, and spread rapidly because of wood used for construction stored in the premises. Soon, the entire building was engulfed in smoke. Hospitals were put on high alert, but no injuries were reported. A civil defence spokesman claimed the fire started on the 32nd floor of the Hajar Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The second fire struck the Safa Tower on 1 May 2009. No deaths or injuries were reported in the blaze which was quickly contained by Civil Defence. Eyewitnesses said the fire broke out soon after [Asr prayer](/wiki/Asr_prayer) while some workers in the building were welding iron rods on wooden scaffoldings. The fire damaged a large part of the under-construction tower. According to Major General Adel Zamzami, director general of Civil Defence in the Mecca province, the fire broke out at the 14th floor and reached up to the 20th floor.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21)

The location chosen for the towers was the historic 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress). The fortress was demolished to make way for the complex.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The development has been criticized by [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian) for having "transformed a type of architecture that evolved from a dense urban grain of low-rise courtyards and narrow streets into ... an endlessly repeatable pattern for the decoration of standardized [concrete] slab(s)".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Clock Towers
أبراج الساعة

/wiki/File:Abraj_Al_Bait_Tower_2017.jpg
Interactive map of the The Clock Towers area

Record height
Tallest in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia) since 2012[[I]](#endnote_talleststatus)
Preceded by[Kingdom Centre](/wiki/Kingdom_Centre)
General information
TypeMixed use:
Hotel, Residential
Architectural style[Postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture)
[New Classical](/wiki/New_Classical_architecture)
Location[Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia)
[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system)[21°25′08″N 39°49′35″E / 21.418888888889°N 39.826388888889°E](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=The_Clock_Towers&params=21.418888888889_N_39.826388888889_E_type:landmark_region:SA)
Construction started2002; 24 years ago
Completed2011; 15 years ago
Opening2012; 14 years ago
CostUS$15 billion[[4]](#cite_note-4)
Height
Architectural607 m (1,991 ft)[[5]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-5)
Tip607 m (1,991 ft)[[5]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-5)
Antenna spire93 metres (305 feet)[[1]](#cite_note-spire93m-1)[[2]](#cite_note-diagram93m-2)
Roof508 m (1,667 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-spire93m-1)[[2]](#cite_note-diagram93m-2)
Top floor494 m (1,621 ft)[[6]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-6)
Observatory484.4 m (1,589 ft)[[6]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-6)
Technical details
MaterialMain structural system: [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete) (lower part), steel/concrete composite construction, steel construction (upper part);
 cladding: [glass](/wiki/Glass), [marble](/wiki/Marble), natural stone, carbon-/glass-[fibre-reinforced plastic](/wiki/Fibre-reinforced_plastic)
Floor count120[[3]](#cite_note-skyscrapercenter.com-3) (Mecca Royal Clock Tower)
Floor areaTower: 310,638 m2 (3,343,680 sq ft)
Development: 1,575,815 m2 (16,961,930 sq ft)[[6]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-6) (389.4 acres)
Lifts/elevators96 (Mecca Royal Clock Tower with mall included)
Design and construction
Architects[SL Rasch GmbH](/wiki/SL_Rasch_GmbH_Special_and_Lightweight_Structures) and [Dar Al-Handasah](/wiki/Dar_Al-Handasah) Architects
Structural engineer[SL Rasch GmbH](/wiki/SL_Rasch_GmbH_Special_and_Lightweight_Structures) and [Dar Al-Handasah](/wiki/Dar_Al-Handasah)
Main contractor[Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group)
Website
[theclocktowers.com](https://theclocktowers.com)

The Clock Towers ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج الساعة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj as-Sāʿaẗ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the Clock', formerly known as Abraj al-Bait [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج البيت, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj al-Bayt, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the House') is a [government-owned](/wiki/State_ownership) hotel complex with seven [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) [hotels](/wiki/Hotel) in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia). These towers are part of the [King Abdulaziz Endowment Project](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia#Domestic_affairs) that aims to modernize the city in catering to its pilgrims. The central hotel tower, which is the Mecca Royal Clock Tower, is the fourth-[tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and sixth-[tallest freestanding structure in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_freestanding_structures).[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the Mecca Royal Clock Tower is the tallest clock tower in the world, and the complex of seven buildings comprise the world's second most expensive building.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The clock faces are the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_clock_faces), and the top four floors of the clock tower house the [Clock Tower Museum](/wiki/Clock_Tower_Museum).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The building complex is 300 metres (980 ft) away from the world's largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam)'s [most sacred site](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam), the [Great Mosque of Mecca](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Mecca).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The developer and contractor of the complex is the [Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group), the Kingdom's largest [construction company](/wiki/Construction_company). The total cost of construction totaled US$15 billion. The complex was built after the demolition of the [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress), the 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) citadel on top of a hill overlooking the Grand Mosque.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [destruction of the historically significant site](/wiki/Destruction_of_early_Islamic_heritage_sites_in_Saudi_Arabia) in 2002 by the [Saudi government](/wiki/Saudi_government) sparked an outcry and a strong reaction from Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_3.jpgThe Mecca Royal Clock Tower's, also known as Massive Mohammad after [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben)'s clocks, the largest in the world
The tallest tower in the complex is the [tallest building in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Saudi_Arabia), at 601 meters (1,972 feet) and a total surface area of 32,000 square meters. It is the sixth-tallest freestanding structure in the world, surpassing the [Ping An Finance Centre](/wiki/Ping_An_Finance_Centre) in [Shenzhen, China](/wiki/Shenzhen) but shorter than the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai, UAE](/wiki/Dubai), the [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in [Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur,_Malaysia), the [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [Lower Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan,_New_York_City), the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) in [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan), the [Zifeng Tower](/wiki/Zifeng_Tower) in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu), [China](/wiki/China), the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) in [Shanghai, China](/wiki/Shanghai,_China), the [CN Tower](/wiki/CN_Tower) in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto), Ontario, Canada, and the [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower) in [Guangzhou, China](/wiki/Guangzhou,_China).[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The site of the complex is across the piazza to the south from the main entrance (King Abdul-Aziz Gate) to the [Masjid al Haram mosque](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram), which houses the [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba). It accommodates worshippers visiting the Kaaba. The tallest tower in the complex also contains a five-star hotel, operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts, to help provide lodging for the millions of pilgrims that travel to Mecca annually to participate in the Hajj.

In addition, The Clock Towers has a five-story shopping mall (The Clock Towers Shopping Center) and a parking garage capable of holding over a thousand vehicles.

The building was planned to be 734 m (2,408 ft) tall in 2006. In 2009, it was published that the final height would be 601 m (1,972 ft). The complex was built by the Saudi Binladin Group, Saudi Arabia's largest construction company. The façade was constructed by Premiere Composite Technologies, and the clock by German tower clock manufacturer PERROT GmbH & Co. KG Turmuhren und Läuteanlagen. Because of its location in Mecca, non-Muslim members of the construction team were not allowed to visit the site, and some specialists from Poland and Germany converted to Islam to be able to do so.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to the Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments, the overall project cost US$15 billion.

There are clock faces on all four side of the main hotel tower. The total height of the clock is 57 m (187 ft), situated above the media displays under the clock faces. At 43 m × 43 m (141 ft × 141 ft), these are the largest in the world. The roof of the clock is 450 m (1,480 ft) above the ground, making it the world's most elevated architectural clock. A spire has been added on top of the clock giving it a total height of 601 m (1,972 ft). Behind the clock faces, there is an astronomy exhibition. In the spire base and the iron-covered floors (The Jewel) there is a scientific center that is used to sight the moon at the beginning of the Islamic months, and to operate an atomic clock that controls the tower clock faces.

### List of component towers

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup.jpgThe Clock Towers' architecture has fine detail
/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_closeup_2.jpgThe Clock Towers' cladding

The building is topped by a four-faced clock, visible from 25 kilometres (16 miles) away. The clock is the highest in the world at over 400 m (1,300 ft) above the ground, surpassing the [Allen-Bradley clock tower](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation_Headquarters_and_Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee). The clock faces are the largest in the world, surpassing the [Cevahir Mall](/wiki/Istanbul_Cevahir) clock in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul).

Each of the clock's four faces measures 43 m (141 ft) in diameter and are illuminated by 2 million LED lights, with four oriented edges, just above the clock alongside huge Arabic script reading: "[God is [the] greatest](/wiki/Takbir)" on the north and south faces and on the west and east "[There is no god but Allah. Muhammad is the messenger of God.](/wiki/Shahada)" Four golden domes on pillars on all the corners are also present. The same as the [Saudi flag](/wiki/Saudi_flag), fitted at the top of the clock, flash to signal Islam's five-time daily prayers. On special occasions such as new year, 21,000 green and white xenon bulbs and LED lamps during the call to the five prayers times of the day. On special occasions, 40 beacon lights create lighting effects. In addition, strong lasers throw their rays 30 kilometers into the sky. The clock's four faces are covered with 98 million pieces of glass mosaics. The [Saudi coat of arms](/wiki/Emblem_of_Saudi_Arabia) is displayed at the center of each clock behind the dials. The minute hand is 23 m (75 ft) long, while the hour hand is 18 m (59 ft) long.

There were reports that the clock would be set to [Mecca Time](/wiki/Mecca_Time), in an attempt to replace the IERS Reference Meridian as the prime meridian for global time keeping, but the clock is set to Arabia Standard Time ([UTC+03:00](/wiki/UTC%2B03:00)).

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_1.jpgThe Clock Towers' clock and spire
/wiki/File:Comparison_four_face_clocks.jpgComparison of some notable four-face clocks at the same scale.
 Top-left: [Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower](/wiki/Metropolitan_Life_Insurance_Company_Tower)
 Bottom-left: [Allen-Bradley Clock Tower](/wiki/Allen-Bradley_Clock_Tower) (previous largest)
 Middle: [Abraj Al Bait](/wiki/Abraj_Al_Bait)
 Top-right: [Elizabeth Tower](/wiki/Elizabeth_Tower) ("Big Ben")
 Bottom-right: [Kremlin Clock](/wiki/Kremlin_Clock)

The spire has an eight-story glass-covered base (The Jewel) which belongs to a scientific center having its own small exhibition, another observation deck at 484 m (1,588 ft). The highest floor in The Jewel is the Control Tower Floor, which was planned to be used for controlling air traffic in the sky above Mecca (mainly helicopters, as airplanes are not allowed near Mecca). However, this was skipped for technical reasons and the future usage is not clear yet. Above from The Jewel, the spire has only technical installations for sound, light, and other infrastructure and eventually the last viewing deck and the crescent above it. The crescent is 21 m (69 ft) in diameter, has two regular floors with living areas, a prayer room in the center of the crescent[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a few service floors and rooms.

The crescent was constructed in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) in April 2011. It is made of fiberglass-backed [mosaic gold](/wiki/Mosaic_gold), and it weighs up to 35 tonnes. It cost 90 million [United Arab Emirates dirhams](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham) and took three months to build.

The minaret and its base have loudspeakers which broadcast prayer calls to a distance of 7 km (4.3 mi) away and across an area of approximately 160 km2 (62 sq mi).

The Clock Towers complex had two fire incidents during construction. The first fire accident was at Hajar Tower on 28 October 2008. It took 400 firefighters to put out the fire, which burned for 10 hours, consuming nine floors of the tower.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21) According to eyewitness reports, the blaze erupted shortly after midnight, and spread rapidly because of wood used for construction stored in the premises. Soon, the entire building was engulfed in smoke. Hospitals were put on high alert, but no injuries were reported. A civil defence spokesman claimed the fire started on the 32nd floor of the Hajar Tower.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The second fire struck the Safa Tower on 1 May 2009. No deaths or injuries were reported in the blaze which was quickly contained by Civil Defence. Eyewitnesses said the fire broke out soon after [Asr prayer](/wiki/Asr_prayer) while some workers in the building were welding iron rods on wooden scaffoldings. The fire damaged a large part of the under-construction tower. According to Major General Adel Zamzami, director general of Civil Defence in the Mecca province, the fire broke out at the 14th floor and reached up to the 20th floor.[[21]](#cite_note-Sheqdar-21)

The location chosen for the towers was the historic 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress). The fortress was demolished to make way for the complex.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The development has been criticized by [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian) for having "transformed a type of architecture that evolved from a dense urban grain of low-rise courtyards and narrow streets into ... an endlessly repeatable pattern for the decoration of standardized [concrete] slab(s)".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

The inclusion or exclusion of items from this list or length of this list is disputed. Please discuss this issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:List_of_most_expensive_buildings#The_scope_of_this_list_is_unclear).

In the following, a building is defined as a structure with a roof and walls that stands permanently in one place. Airports are excluded as their construction cost includes runways and systems; however, terminal buildings are included in the list.

Marina Bay Sands

/wiki/File:Marina_Bay_Sands_(I).jpg
/wiki/Special:Map/13/1.2825/103.86/enInteractive map of Marina Bay Sands
