Marina Bay Sands (often colloquially shortened to 'MBS') is an [integrated resort](/wiki/Integrated_resort) fronting [Marina Bay](/wiki/Marina_Bay,_Singapore) in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore) and a landmark of the city. At its opening in 2010, it was deemed the world's most expensive standalone [casino](/wiki/Casino) property at [S$](/wiki/Singapore_dollar)8 billion (US$6.88 billion).[[3]](#cite_note-Adelson1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The resort includes a 1,850-room hotel,[[5]](#cite_note-5) a 120,000-square-metre (1,300,000 sq ft) meetings and conventions facility at Sands Expo & Convention Centre, a 74,000-square-metre (800,000 sq ft) luxury shopping mall, The Shoppes at Marina Bay Sands, an ArtScience museum, a 2,183-capacity theatre, the world's first floating [Apple store](/wiki/Apple_store),[[6]](#cite_note-6) the world's first [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton) Island Maison, celebrity chef and signature restaurants, and a casino with 500 tables and 3,000 electronic gaming machines.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The complex includes three towers topped by the Sands Skypark, a 340-metre-long (1,120 ft) [skyway](/wiki/Skyway) connecting the towers with a capacity of 3,902 people and a 150 m (490 ft) [infinity swimming pool](/wiki/Infinity_pool), set on top of the world's largest public [cantilevered](/wiki/Cantilever) platform, which overhangs the north tower by 66.5 m (218 ft).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The 20-hectare resort was designed by [Moshe Safdie](/wiki/Moshe_Safdie).[[10]](#cite_note-MBS-01-10)[[11]](#cite_note-MBS-02-11)

The resort is owned by [Las Vegas Sands](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Sands) in agreement with the Singaporean authorities. Marina Bay Sands was originally set to open in 2009, but its construction faced delays caused by escalating costs of material and labour shortages from the outset exacerbated by the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis). This pressured Las Vegas Sands to delay its projects elsewhere to complete the integrated resort. Its owner decided to open the integrated resort in stages, and it was approved by the Singapore authorities.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The resort and SkyPark were officially opened on 23 and 24 June 2010 as part of a two-day celebration, following the casino's [opening](/wiki/Soft_opening) on 27 April that year.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The SkyPark opened the following day. The theatre was completed in time for the first performance of [Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance) on 30 November. The indoor skating rink, which uses [artificial ice](/wiki/Artificial_ice), opened to a performance by [Michelle Kwan](/wiki/Michelle_Kwan) on 18 December. The ArtScience Museum opened to the public and the debut of a 13-minute light, laser and water show called Wonder Full on 19 February 2011, marked the full completion of the integrated resort.

The opening of Marina Bay Sands was held on 17 February 2011. It also marked the opening of the seven celebrity chef restaurants.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The last portion of the Marina Bay Sands, the floating pavilions, were finally opened to the public when the two tenants, [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton) and Pangaea Club, opened on 18 and 22 September 2011, respectively.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The property is currently being expanded to include a fourth hotel tower, an arena and additional convention space.

/wiki/File:View_of_MBS_from_the_gardens_(8026531707).jpgView of the three main towers, inspired by decks of cards
Marina Bay Sands is one of two winning proposals for Singapore's first integrated resorts, the other being the [Resorts World Sentosa](/wiki/Resorts_World_Sentosa), which incorporates a family-friendly [Universal Studios Theme Park](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Theme_Park) ([Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore)). The two large-scale resorts were conceived to meet Singapore's economic and tourism objectives for the next decade and will have 30-year casino licenses, exclusive for the first ten years.
Bidders were assessed based on four criteria: tourism appeal and contribution, architectural concept and design, development investment, and strength of the consortium and partners.

/wiki/File:Rooftop_Pool_Marina_bay_Singapore_(39289149252).jpgThe infinity edge [swimming pool](/wiki/Infinity_pool) in the Skypark
On 27 May 2006, Las Vegas Sands (LVS) was declared the winner with its business-oriented resort.[[16]](#cite_note-16) LVS submitted its winning bid on its own. Its original partner City Developments Limited (CDL), with a proposed 15% equity stake, pulled out of the partnership in the second phase of the tender process. CDL's CEO, [Kwek Leng Beng](/wiki/Kwek_Leng_Beng) said his company's pullout was a combination of factors—such as difficulties in getting numerous companies he owns to comply in time, as well as reluctance of some parties to disclose certain private information in probity checks required by the [Singapore government](/wiki/Government_of_Singapore).[[17]](#cite_note-TODAYonline-17) However, Kwek was retained as an advisor for Sands' bid.

Las Vegas Sands initially committed to invest S$3.85 billion in the project, not including the fixed S$1.2 billion cost of the 6,000,000 square feet (560,000 m2) site itself.[[18]](#cite_note-18) With the escalating costs of materials, such as sand and steel, and labour shortages owing to other major infrastructure and property development in the country, [Sheldon Adelson](/wiki/Sheldon_Adelson) placed the total cost of the development at S$8.0 billion as of July 2009.[[3]](#cite_note-Adelson1-3)[[19]](#cite_note-channelnewsasia.com-19)

[Las Vegas Sands](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Sands) declared the undertaking as "one of the world's most challenging construction projects and certainly the most expensive stand-alone integrated resort property ever built".[[20]](#cite_note-opening-20) It expected the casino to generate at least $1 billion in annual [profit](/wiki/Profit_(accounting)).[[21]](#cite_note-21) Two months after the initial phased opening, the casino was attracting around 25,000 visitors daily, about a third being [Singaporeans](/wiki/Singaporeans) and permanent residents who paid a $150 daily entry levy or $3,000 for annual unlimited access.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Half a million gamblers passed through the casino in June 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In the third quarter of 2012, the revenues of the Marina Bay Sands fell almost 28 per cent from a year earlier.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

For the economy, Marina Bay Sands was projected to stimulate an addition of $2.7 billion or 0.8% to [Singapore's Gross Domestic Product](/wiki/Economy_of_Singapore) by 2015, employing 10,000 people directly and 20,000 jobs being created in other industries.[[17]](#cite_note-TODAYonline-17)

On 3 April 2019, Sands announced a $3.3 billion expansion of its Marina Bay Sands property in Singapore. The expansion will include the construction of a fourth hotel tower containing 1,000 luxury suites and a 15,000-seat arena.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In October 2024, Sands announced it would increase its investment to US$8 billion on its expansion, described as an entirely new development dubbed "Marina Bay Sands IR2". The development is slated to boast 570 luxury suites, 110,000 square feet of MICE space, its own SkyPark and high-end F&B, in addition to the 15,000-seat arena previously announced.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

As the development of Marina Bay Sands was perceived as critical in Singapore's urbanization efforts, a fixed price was set for the land. This was a departure from the government auctioning off the land to the highest bidder.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Bidders were instead assessed holistically on factors such as the design of the Marina Bay Sands and the amenities it would offer.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Design and construction
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marina_Bay_Sands&action=edit&section=3)]

/wiki/File:Marina_Bay_Sands_under_construction_-_20090805.jpgConstruction on 5 August 2009
The resort is designed by [Moshe Safdie](/wiki/Moshe_Safdie), who says it was initially inspired by card decks.[[10]](#cite_note-MBS-01-10) The prominent feature of the design is the three hotel towers, which have 1,850 rooms and suites, and a continuous lobby at the base linking the three towers. The casino has a four-storey central atrium with four levels of gaming and entertainment in one space.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In addition to the hotel and the casino, other buildings include a 19,000 m2 (200,000 sq ft) [ArtScience Museum](/wiki/ArtScience_Museum),[[11]](#cite_note-MBS-02-11) and Sands Expo & Convention Centre with 110,000 m2 (1,200,000 sq ft) of space, capable of accommodating up to 45,000 people. The resort's architecture and major design changes along the way were also approved by its [feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui) consultants, the late Chong Swan Lek and Louisa Ong-Lee.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Aedas](/wiki/Aedas)[[32]](#cite_note-32) were responsible for employing all consultants and for developing, co-ordinating and implementing the design. The structural engineering for the project was handled by [Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group_Limited) with [Parsons Brinckerhoff](/wiki/Parsons_Brinckerhoff) the [MEP](/wiki/Mechanical,_electrical,_and_plumbing) engineers. The main contractor was [Ssangyong Engineering and Construction](/wiki/Ssangyong_Engineering_and_Construction).[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

/wiki/File:Marina_Bay_Sands_(215599375).jpegSands SkyPark
The three towers are broader at the base and narrow as they rise. Each tower has two asymmetric legs, with a curved eastern leg leaning against the other, creating a significant technical challenge in its construction. Substantial temporary structures were necessary to support the legs of the tower while they were under construction, and required real-time monitoring for continual assessment and analyses in the course of their erection.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

A distinctive feature of the hotel is Sands SkyPark, a 1.2-hectare tropical oasis that is longer than the Eiffel Tower and extends to form one of the world's longest public cantilevers. Built at the height of 200m, the structure offers 12,400sqm of space – big enough to fit three football fields.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The structure bridges all three towers with a segment cantilevered off the north tower. The hull of the SkyPark was pre-fabricated off-site in 14 separate steel sections and then assembled on top of the towers.[[37]](#cite_note-37) There are four movement joints beneath the main pools, designed to help them withstand the natural motion of the towers, and each joint has a unique range of motion. The total range of motion is 500 millimetres (20 inches). In addition to wind, the hotel towers are also subject to settlement in the earth over time, so engineers built and installed custom jack legs to allow for future adjustment at more than 500 points beneath the pool system. This jacking system is important primarily to ensure the infinity edge of the pool continues to function properly.[[38]](#cite_note-Nat-38)

/wiki/File:The_Shoppes_at_Marina_Bay_Sands,_Singapore.jpgThe Rain Oculus above the shopping mall canal was designed by [Ned Kahn](/wiki/Ned_Kahn).
Marina Bay Sands was originally planned to be completed in a single phase in 2009,[[19]](#cite_note-channelnewsasia.com-19) but rising construction costs and the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis) forced the company to open it in phases. The first phase's [soft opening](/wiki/Soft_opening) was further delayed until 27 April 2010, and the official opening was pushed back to 23 June 2010. The rest of the complex remained under construction and was opened after a [grand opening](/wiki/Grand_opening) on 17 February 2011.

On 27 April 2010, Marina Bay Sands had the first of a planned 3 to 4 phase openings. The casino, parts of the conference hall, a segment of the Shoppes, 963 hotel rooms and the event plaza were opened at the auspicious time of 3:18 p.m as part of the "preview opening".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The Inter-Pacific Bar Association (IPBA) held the first conference at Marina Bay Sands Convention Centre on 2–5 May 2010, but the event was marred by uncompleted facilities and power failure during a speech. IPBA withheld payment of S$300,000 and was consequently sued by Marina Bay Sands.[[40]](#cite_note-yoursdp-40) In June IPBA counter-sued, describing the venue as a "complete disaster" and that its earlie

Marina Bay Sands (often colloquially shortened to 'MBS') is an [integrated resort](/wiki/Integrated_resort) fronting [Marina Bay](/wiki/Marina_Bay,_Singapore) in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore) and a landmark of the city. At its opening in 2010, it was deemed the world's most expensive standalone [casino](/wiki/Casino) property at [S$](/wiki/Singapore_dollar)8 billion (US$6.88 billion).[[3]](#cite_note-Adelson1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The resort includes a 1,850-room hotel,[[5]](#cite_note-5) a 120,000-square-metre (1,300,000 sq ft) meetings and conventions facility at Sands Expo & Convention Centre, a 74,000-square-metre (800,000 sq ft) luxury shopping mall, The Shoppes at Marina Bay Sands, an ArtScience museum, a 2,183-capacity theatre, the world's first floating [Apple store](/wiki/Apple_store),[[6]](#cite_note-6) the world's first [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton) Island Maison, celebrity chef and signature restaurants, and a casino with 500 tables and 3,000 electronic gaming machines.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The complex includes three towers topped by the Sands Skypark, a 340-metre-long (1,120 ft) [skyway](/wiki/Skyway) connecting the towers with a capacity of 3,902 people and a 150 m (490 ft) [infinity swimming pool](/wiki/Infinity_pool), set on top of the world's largest public [cantilevered](/wiki/Cantilever) platform, which overhangs the north tower by 66.5 m (218 ft).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The 20-hectare resort was designed by [Moshe Safdie](/wiki/Moshe_Safdie).[[10]](#cite_note-MBS-01-10)[[11]](#cite_note-MBS-02-11)

The resort is owned by [Las Vegas Sands](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Sands) in agreement with the Singaporean authorities. Marina Bay Sands was originally set to open in 2009, but its construction faced delays caused by escalating costs of material and labour shortages from the outset exacerbated by the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis). This pressured Las Vegas Sands to delay its projects elsewhere to complete the integrated resort. Its owner decided to open the integrated resort in stages, and it was approved by the Singapore authorities.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The resort and SkyPark were officially opened on 23 and 24 June 2010 as part of a two-day celebration, following the casino's [opening](/wiki/Soft_opening) on 27 April that year.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The SkyPark opened the following day. The theatre was completed in time for the first performance of [Riverdance](/wiki/Riverdance) on 30 November. The indoor skating rink, which uses [artificial ice](/wiki/Artificial_ice), opened to a performance by [Michelle Kwan](/wiki/Michelle_Kwan) on 18 December. The ArtScience Museum opened to the public and the debut of a 13-minute light, laser and water show called Wonder Full on 19 February 2011, marked the full completion of the integrated resort.

The opening of Marina Bay Sands was held on 17 February 2011. It also marked the opening of the seven celebrity chef restaurants.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The last portion of the Marina Bay Sands, the floating pavilions, were finally opened to the public when the two tenants, [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton) and Pangaea Club, opened on 18 and 22 September 2011, respectively.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The property is currently being expanded to include a fourth hotel tower, an arena and additional convention space.

/wiki/File:View_of_MBS_from_the_gardens_(8026531707).jpgView of the three main towers, inspired by decks of cards
Marina Bay Sands is one of two winning proposals for Singapore's first integrated resorts, the other being the [Resorts World Sentosa](/wiki/Resorts_World_Sentosa), which incorporates a family-friendly [Universal Studios Theme Park](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Theme_Park) ([Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore)). The two large-scale resorts were conceived to meet Singapore's economic and tourism objectives for the next decade and will have 30-year casino licenses, exclusive for the first ten years.
Bidders were assessed based on four criteria: tourism appeal and contribution, architectural concept and design, development investment, and strength of the consortium and partners.

/wiki/File:Rooftop_Pool_Marina_bay_Singapore_(39289149252).jpgThe infinity edge [swimming pool](/wiki/Infinity_pool) in the Skypark
On 27 May 2006, Las Vegas Sands (LVS) was declared the winner with its business-oriented resort.[[16]](#cite_note-16) LVS submitted its winning bid on its own. Its original partner City Developments Limited (CDL), with a proposed 15% equity stake, pulled out of the partnership in the second phase of the tender process. Resorts World Sentosa ([abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation): RWS) is an [integrated resort](/wiki/Integrated_resort) on the island of [Sentosa](/wiki/Sentosa), which is located off the southern coast of [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore). The key attractions within RWS include one of Singapore's two casinos, [Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore) theme park (which is the second Universal Studios theme park in Asia after [Universal Studios Japan](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Japan) and the first in Southeast Asia) the [Adventure Cove Waterpark](/wiki/Adventure_Cove_Waterpark), as well as the [Singapore Oceanarium](/wiki/Singapore_Oceanarium), which is the world's second largest [oceanarium](/wiki/Oceanarium).[[1]](#cite_note-2)

First conceived in 2006, the S$6.59 billion (US$5.03 billion) resort was developed by [Genting Singapore](/wiki/Genting_Singapore), and construction began in 2007. It was the [third most expensive building](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_buildings_in_the_world) ever constructed when it was completed in 2010. The resort occupies approximately 50 hectares (120 acres) of land and directly employs up to 15,000 people. The [soft launch](/wiki/Soft_launch) of the first four hotels took place on 20 January 2010,[[2]](#cite_note-3) with the FestiveWalk shopping mall following on 1 February.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

The casino began operations on 14 February 2010 on the first day of the [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year).[[4]](#cite_note-5) [The Maritime Experiential Museum](/wiki/The_Maritime_Experiential_Museum) opened on 15 October 2011[[5]](#cite_note-rws_blog-6) and the last attraction opened on 22 November 2012, known as The Marine Life Park.[[6]](#cite_note-MarineLifePark-7) The grand opening of the entire integrated resort was held on 7 December 2012, which was officiated by the country's [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Singapore) [Lee Hsien Loong](/wiki/Lee_Hsien_Loong) together with [Genting Group](/wiki/Genting_Group) Chairman [Lim Kok Thay](/wiki/Tan_Sri_Lim_Kok_Thay).[[7]](#cite_note-GrandOpening-8) Resorts World Sentosa is also capable of holding large-scale exhibitions,[[8]](#cite_note-9) and was host to the [2024 World Chess Championship](/wiki/World_Chess_Championship_2024).[[9]](#cite_note-10)

Construction of Resorts World Sentosa Singapore began on 16 April 2007 on the demolished plot of [Imbiah Lookout](/wiki/Imbiah_Lookout). It opened after 34 months of construction on 20 January 2010.[[10]](#cite_note-11)
Festive Hotel opened on 7 January 2010. Hotel Michael opened on 13 January 2010. Crockfords Tower and [Hard Rock Hotel](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Cafe) Singapore opened on 20 January 2010, followed by FestiveWalk on 31 January 2010. Resorts World Sentosa Casino opened on 14 February 2010. Resorts World Sentosa's grand opening was on 7 December 2012.

Universal Studios Singapore was opened for a sneak peek week in view of the Chinese New Year Celebrations, from 5 pm to 9 pm every night between 14 and 21 February 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The whole park was opened but none of the rides was operational. Visitors had to pay SGD10 to get into the park. Park tickets for the week were sold out in 2 days. The park had its soft opening period from 18 March 2010 to 26 October 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

As a result of the global [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic), RWS had to temporarily lay off a significant amount of its workforce.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

In 2024, the Singapore government renewed RWS' casino license for two years, instead of the usual three, from 2025 to 2027 due to poor tourism performance.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16) Genting Singapore said the poor performance was hampered by the COVID-19 pandemic as the evaluation period coincided with the pandemic period.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16)

/wiki/File:Aerial_perspective_of_Sentosa_Island.jpgAerial perspective of Sentosa Boardwalk, Sentosa Island, and Resorts World Sentosa
The resort was designed primarily by the [Driehaus Prize](/wiki/Driehaus_Architecture_Prize) winner and [New Classical](/wiki/New_Classical_architecture) architect [Michael Graves](/wiki/Michael_Graves). The six hotels offer a total of 1,840 rooms for accommodation.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Each hotel is designed with a different theme, catering to both leisure and business visitors.[[17]](#cite_note-18)
The resort is split into the west, central and east zones.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Four hotels are located in the central zone.

- Crockfords Tower, formerly planned to be named Maxims Tower,[[19]](#cite_note-20) is an 11-storey all-suite hotel[[20]](#cite_note-21) overlooking the [Singapore harbour](/wiki/Port_of_Singapore) and the [Southern Islands](/wiki/Southern_Islands). The resort's casino is located beneath the tower.[[21]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-22) The hotel was topped-out on 27 February 2009 and opened on 20 January 2010. Together with Hotel Michael, it sits on the site of the former [Sentosa Musical Fountain](/wiki/Sentosa_Musical_Fountain). The hotel also features Crockfords Premier, a casino club with private rooms for High Roller located on the 10th floor.

- Hotel Michael is an 11-story hotel named after Michael Graves.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Hotel Michael topped-out on 15 July 2009 and was opened on 13 January 2010. Together with Crockfords Tower, it sits on the site of the former [Sentosa Musical Fountain](/wiki/Sentosa_Musical_Fountain).

- Hotel Ora, formerly known as Festive Hotel, is a family-oriented hotel next to Crockfords Tower and Festive Walk.[[23]](#cite_note-24) It was the first hotel in Resorts World Sentosa to open on 7 January 2010. Beneath the hotel is Festive Grand, a 1,600 seat plenary hall which will host Resorts World Sentosa's resident musical Voyage de la Vie.[[24]](#cite_note-rwsentosa.com-25)

- The Laurus, Singapore's first The luxury Collection-branded resort is opened in October 2025.[[25]](#cite_note-26) An all-suite resort, it replaced the former [Hard Rock Hotel](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Cafe).

The [Royal Albatross](/wiki/Royal_Albatross_(ship)) is berthed at the Historical Ships Harbour, beside the Singapore Oceanarium (formerly S.E.A. Aquarium) and the Adventure Cove Waterpark.

Resorts World Sentosa casino is located beneath Crockfords Tower that has an acreage of 15,000 sq.m.

In May 2011, the Casino Regulatory Authority fined Resorts World Sentosa for two violations related to reimbursements and two other violations related to surveillance practices. The total fine was S$530,000 (US$425,000).[[26]](#cite_note-27)

- Equarius Hotel, close to the [Adventure Cove Waterpark](/wiki/Adventure_Cove_Waterpark).[[27]](#cite_note-28)

- Beach Villas, a collection of 22 villas floating on a lagoon.[[28]](#cite_note-29) It opened on 16 February 2012.

[Singapore Oceanarium](/wiki/Singapore_Oceanarium) (formerly S.E.A. Aquarium), the world's second largest oceanarium, and [Adventure Cove Waterpark](/wiki/Adventure_Cove_Waterpark) (formerly Equarius Water Park) first opened its doors on 22 November 2012.[[6]](#cite_note-MarineLifePark-7)

#### The Maritime Experiential Museum
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Resorts_World_Sentosa&action=edit&section=12)]

/wiki/File:Javanese_jong_RWS.jpgA replica of small [Javanese jong](/wiki/Djong_(ship)).
[The Maritime Experiential Museum](/wiki/The_Maritime_Experiential_Museum) was opened on 15 October 2011 that features more than 400 artifacts and replicas with a 360-degree Multi-sensory Typhoon Theatre. It displays the history of ancient maritime trade: visitors have the opportunity to immerse themselves in the history of the maritime Silk Route from the 15th to 19th century. The museum consists of more than 10 interactive points as well as an opportunity to board the authentic harbor ships from Asia docked outside the museum.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Also, it will become the permanent home of the [Jewel of Muscat](/wiki/Jewel_of_Muscat), a gift from the Oman Government.[[30]](#cite_note-31) It was closed along with the Crane Dance on 2 March 2020 as part of plans to expand the S.E.A. Aquarium.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The [Royal Albatross](/wiki/Royal_Albatross_(ship)) is berthed at the Historical Ships Harbour, beside the Aquarium and the Adventure Cove Waterpark. The ship is available twice every weekend. Its route goes through the beaches of Sentosa to the outskirts of Marina Bay, around the edge of the [Southern Islands](/wiki/Southern_Islands) and back to Sentosa.

#### Universal Studios Singapore
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Resorts_World_Sentosa&action=edit&section=15)]

[Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore) is Southeast Asia's first [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Destinations_%26_Experiences) theme park and the second in all of Asia. It opened its doors on 18 March 2010. It features 24 attractions and is divided into seven zones – including Sci-Fi City, Ancient Egypt, New York, The Lost World, Far Far Away, Minion Land and Hollywood.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Like most places in Sentosa, Resort World is accessible by the [Sentosa Express](/wiki/Sentosa_Express).[[33]](#cite_note-auto-34)

Resort World is also accessible by the Boardwalk, which currently leads to the East Carpark due to renovations.[[33]](#cite_note-auto-34)

[Shuttle](/wiki/Shuttle_bus) services [RWS8](https://landtransportguru.net/busrws8/) and the [Sentosa Bus A](https://landtransportguru.net/sentosa-bus-a/) serve the resort.[[33]](#cite_note-auto-34) Public [Bus Service 123](https://www.transitlink.com.sg/eservice/eguide/service_route.php?service=123) also serves the resort, and upon entering Sentosa stops at WEAVE and goes as far as Beach Station (on the Sentosa Express), before exiting Sentosa with no intermediate stops.

Discontinued services include[[34]](#cite_note-35) the NR1, NR6,[[35]](#cite_note-36) 188R, and 963R bus services.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

The carpark at Resort World is accessible via Sentosa Gateway.[[33]](#cite_note-auto-34)

It was the venue for the [2024 World Chess Championship](/wiki/2024_World_Chess_Championship) with [Ding Liren](/wiki/Ding_Liren) defending the title against [Gukesh Dommaraju](/wiki/Gukesh_Dommaraju). Gukesh won the championship in 14 games, and at age 18 became the youngest competitor to hold the title.

Resorts World Sentosa ([abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation): RWS) is an [integrated resort](/wiki/Integrated_resort) on the island of [Sentosa](/wiki/Sentosa), which is located off the southern coast of [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore). The key attractions within RWS include one of Singapore's two casinos, [Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore) theme park (which is the second Universal Studios theme park in Asia after [Universal Studios Japan](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Japan) and the first in Southeast Asia) the [Adventure Cove Waterpark](/wiki/Adventure_Cove_Waterpark), as well as the [Singapore Oceanarium](/wiki/Singapore_Oceanarium), which is the world's second largest [oceanarium](/wiki/Oceanarium).[[1]](#cite_note-2)

First conceived in 2006, the S$6.59 billion (US$5.03 billion) resort was developed by [Genting Singapore](/wiki/Genting_Singapore), and construction began in 2007. It was the [third most expensive building](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_buildings_in_the_world) ever constructed when it was completed in 2010. The resort occupies approximately 50 hectares (120 acres) of land and directly employs up to 15,000 people. The [soft launch](/wiki/Soft_launch) of the first four hotels took place on 20 January 2010,[[2]](#cite_note-3) with the FestiveWalk shopping mall following on 1 February.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

The casino began operations on 14 February 2010 on the first day of the [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year).[[4]](#cite_note-5) [The Maritime Experiential Museum](/wiki/The_Maritime_Experiential_Museum) opened on 15 October 2011[[5]](#cite_note-rws_blog-6) and the last attraction opened on 22 November 2012, known as The Marine Life Park.[[6]](#cite_note-MarineLifePark-7) The grand opening of the entire integrated resort was held on 7 December 2012, which was officiated by the country's [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Singapore) [Lee Hsien Loong](/wiki/Lee_Hsien_Loong) together with [Genting Group](/wiki/Genting_Group) Chairman [Lim Kok Thay](/wiki/Tan_Sri_Lim_Kok_Thay).[[7]](#cite_note-GrandOpening-8) Resorts World Sentosa is also capable of holding large-scale exhibitions,[[8]](#cite_note-9) and was host to the [2024 World Chess Championship](/wiki/World_Chess_Championship_2024).[[9]](#cite_note-10)

Construction of Resorts World Sentosa Singapore began on 16 April 2007 on the demolished plot of [Imbiah Lookout](/wiki/Imbiah_Lookout). It opened after 34 months of construction on 20 January 2010.[[10]](#cite_note-11)
Festive Hotel opened on 7 January 2010. Hotel Michael opened on 13 January 2010. Crockfords Tower and [Hard Rock Hotel](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Cafe) Singapore opened on 20 January 2010, followed by FestiveWalk on 31 January 2010. Resorts World Sentosa Casino opened on 14 February 2010. Resorts World Sentosa's grand opening was on 7 December 2012.

Universal Studios Singapore was opened for a sneak peek week in view of the Chinese New Year Celebrations, from 5 pm to 9 pm every night between 14 and 21 February 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The whole park was opened but none of the rides was operational. Visitors had to pay SGD10 to get into the park. Park tickets for the week were sold out in 2 days. The park had its soft opening period from 18 March 2010 to 26 October 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

As a result of the global [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic), RWS had to temporarily lay off a significant amount of its workforce.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

In 2024, the Singapore government renewed RWS' casino license for two years, instead of the usual three, from 2025 to 2027 due to poor tourism performance.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16) Genting Singapore said the poor performance was hampered by the COVID-19 pandemic as the evaluation period coincided with the pandemic period.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16)

The resort is split into the west, central and east zones.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Four hotels are located in the central zone.

- Crockfords Tower, formerly planned to be named Maxims Tower,[[19]](#cite_note-20) is an 11-storey all-suite hotel[[20]](#cite_note-21) overlooking the [Singapore harbour](/wiki/Port_of_Singapore) and the [Southern Islands](/wiki/Southern_Islands). Apple Park, also known as Apple Campus 2, is the corporate headquarters of [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc.), located in [Cupertino, California](/wiki/Cupertino,_California), United States. It was opened to employees in April 2017, while construction was still underway. It replaced [Apple Campus](/wiki/Apple_Campus) as the company's corporate headquarters.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The main building's scale and circular [groundscraper](/wiki/Groundscraper) design,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) by [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster),[[11]](#cite_note-11) has earned the structure the media nickname "the spaceship".[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-VBtree-14) Located on a suburban site totaling 1.46 km2 (360 acres), it houses more than 12,000 employees in one central four-story circular building of approximately 0.26 km2 (64 acres). Apple co-founder [Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs) wanted the campus to look less like a [business park](/wiki/Business_park) and more like a [nature refuge](/wiki/Nature_park); 80 percent of the site consists of green space planted with drought-resistant trees and plants indigenous to the Cupertino area, and the center courtyard of the main building features an artificial pond.[[14]](#cite_note-VBtree-14)

In April 2006, Apple's then CEO [Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs) announced to the city council of Cupertino that Apple had acquired nine contiguous properties to build a second campus, the Apple Campus 2.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The idea for a new headquarters was conceived by Jobs and Apple's then chief designer [Jony Ive](/wiki/Jony_Ive).[[17]](#cite_note-17) Ive was Apple's immediate choice to design the project, going on to work very closely together with [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster) across five years, designing every detail, from the glass panels to the elevator buttons.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Purchases of the needed properties were made through the company [Hines Interests](/wiki/Hines_Interests_Limited_Partnership),[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Chandler2006-21) which in at least some cases did not disclose the fact that Apple was the ultimate buyer;[[21]](#cite_note-Chandler2006-21) Philip Mahoney, a partner with a local commercial real estate brokerage, noted that this is common practice in attempts to arrange the purchase of contiguous land made up of multiple parcels with separate owners, in order to keep costs from skyrocketing and not reveal the company's plans to competitors.[[21]](#cite_note-Chandler2006-21) Among the sellers of the properties were SummerHill Homes (a plot of 8 acres or 3.2 hectares) and [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard) (three buildings of their campus in Cupertino).[[21]](#cite_note-Chandler2006-21)

Until April 2008, Apple had not sought the necessary permits to begin construction, so it was estimated that the project would not be ready in 2010 as originally proposed; however, the buildings on the site were held by Apple for its operations. In November 2010 the [San Jose Mercury News](/wiki/San_Jose_Mercury_News) revealed[[22]](#cite_note-22) that Apple had bought an additional 98 acres (40 ha) no longer used by HP, just north across Pruneridge Ave. This space had been the HP campus in Cupertino before it was relocated to [Palo Alto](/wiki/Palo_Alto).

On June 7, 2011, Jobs presented to the Cupertino City Council the details of the architectural design of the new buildings and their surroundings.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Afflicted with pancreatic cancer, Steve Jobs resigned from Apple in August 2011 and died a few months later on October 5th.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

On October 15, 2013, Cupertino City Council unanimously approved Apple's plans for the new campus after a six-hour debate.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Shortly thereafter, demolition work began to prepare the site for construction.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Originally expected to break ground in 2013 and open in 2015, the project was delayed and started in 2014.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) On February 22, 2017, Apple announced that the official name of the campus would be "Apple Park", and the auditorium would be named the "Steve Jobs Theater".[[2]](#cite_note-openingdate-2)

The campus opened for workers in April 2017, despite continued construction work.[[29]](#cite_note-29) This was followed by the first event in the Steve Jobs Theater, which took place on September 12, 2017.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The Apple Park Visitor Center opened two months and five days later, on November 17, 2017.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

As a consequence of the construction of Apple Park in the area, surrounding streets have seen increased tourism as well as rising real estate values, due to Apple employees' desire to live near their workplace.[[32]](#cite_note-APark_side_issue_NYT_1-32)[[33]](#cite_note-APark_side_issue_MR_1-33)

/wiki/File:I-280_with_Apple_Campus_2_aerial.jpg[Cupertino](/wiki/Cupertino), California, with the circular building of Apple Park, located north of [Interstate 280](/wiki/Interstate_280_(California))
Apple Park is located 1.2 miles (1.9 km) east of the original [Apple Campus](/wiki/Apple_Campus). Apple has had a presence in Cupertino since 1977, which is why the company decided to build in the area rather than move to a cheaper, distant location. The campus is also next to a contaminated site under [Superfund](/wiki/Superfund) legislation with a [groundwater plume](/wiki/Aquifer) found to contain [trichloroethylene](/wiki/Trichloroethylene) from improper disposal of [industrial waste](/wiki/Industrial_waste) by [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication) manufacturers.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Design and construction
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Apple_Park&action=edit&section=3)]

[Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs), in June 2011, in his final public appearance before his death, was quoted as saying:

It's got a gorgeous courtyard in the middle, and a lot more. It's a circle, so it's curved all the way round. This is not the cheapest way to build something. Every pane of glass in the main building will be curved. We have a shot at building the best office building in the world. I really do think that architecture students will come here to see it.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

/wiki/File:Apple_Campus_2_aerial_Aug_2016.jpgApple Park aerial view during construction in August 2016, north of (below) I-280. The original [Apple Campus](/wiki/Apple_Campus), located 1.2 miles westwards, is also shown on the top-right, south of I-280.
The ring-shaped building, advertised as "a perfect circle", was not originally planned as such. The inner rim and outer rim on each floor are left open as walkways. There are eight buildings, separated by nine mini-[atria](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)). The campus is 4,805 feet (1,465 m) in [circumference](/wiki/Circumference), with a diameter of 1,512 feet (461 m). The one circular building houses most employees.[[37]](#cite_note-37) It is four stories above the ground and three stories underground.[[38]](#cite_note-wired.com-38) Apple created life-size mock-ups of all parts of the building to analyze any design issues.

The campus design places the roads underground. Cars are parked underground and in two multistory parking garages.[[39]](#cite_note-more-parking-than-offices-39) The campus uses only glass for its walls and views of the inner courtyard as well as of the landscape facing the exterior of the building.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Around 83,000 square feet (7,700 m2) of space is for meetings and breakout spaces in the building. The inner part of the circular building contains a 30-acre (12 ha) park featuring a pond, with fruit trees and winding pathways inspired by California fruit orchards.

Apple brought to the construction project the same "fanatical attention to detail" which the company is famous for lavishing upon its products.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) Apple insisted on extraordinarily tight [tolerances](/wiki/Engineering_tolerance), even for parts of the structure normally hidden from view.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) Construction workers were often required to wear gloves to minimize accidental damage to surfaces.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) All interior wood used for furniture was harvested from a certain species of [maple](/wiki/Maple), with Apple working with construction companies from 19 countries for designs and materials.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

A breathing, hollow concrete slab acts as floor, ceiling, and [HVAC](/wiki/HVAC) system. A total of 4,300 such slabs were used.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Some of the slabs weigh 60,000 pounds (27 t).[[45]](#cite_note-45)

During construction, the building's structure was started by [Skanska](/wiki/Skanska) and [DPR](/wiki/DPR_Construction), but they were removed from the job for undisclosed reasons.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Apple hired [Rudolph and Sletten](/wiki/Rudolph_and_Sletten) and Holder Construction as its new [general contractors](/wiki/General_contractor), and they completed the structure, envelope, and interior buildout.[[47]](#cite_note-Donato1-47) Truebeck Construction (then known as BNBTBuilders) worked on the exterior landscaping, Steve Jobs Theater, and the health and fitness center; a joint venture of BNBTBuilders and [Webcor Builders](/wiki/Webcor_Builders) worked on an adjacent group of [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development) buildings; McCarthy Building Companies built the parking garage; and [Granite Construction](/wiki/Granite_Construction) performed road widening and utility work.[[47]](#cite_note-Donato1-47) The project created approximately 13,000 full-time construction jobs.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42)

The facade panes are produced by Bavarian companies Josef Gartner and Sedak. The latter company is a subsidiary of [Seele GmbH](/wiki/Seele_GmbH); Apple had previously worked with Seele, an expert on glass facades, for some of its more prominent Apple Stores such as the one on [5th Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue) in New York City.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) David Nicholl and Partners delivered the M&E works.

/wiki/File:Apple_spaceship_under_construction.jpg
Panorama of Apple Park under construction, July 2016

/wiki/File:Building_and_ship_comparison_to_the_Pentagon2.svgApple Park main building (light green) compared to large ships and buildings:

The land cost was estimated at $160 million. In 2011, the budget for Apple Campus 2 was less than $3 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-Burrows-50) However, in 2013 the total cost was estimated to be closer to $5 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-Burrows-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The campus was announced as one of the most energy-efficient buildings in the world,[[2]](#cite_note-openingdate-2) and the main building,[[52]](#cite_note-52) Steve Jobs Theater,[[53]](#cite_note-53) and fitness center[[54]](#cite_note-54) are all [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design) Platinum certified. In an April 2018 press release, Apple announced that it had switched to being powered entirely by [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy).[[55]](#cite_note-55) The solar panels installed on the roof of the campus can generate 17 megawatts of power, sufficient to power 75% during peak daytime,[[56]](#cite_note-CupertinoCityCouncil-56) and making it one of the [largest solar roofs in the world](/wiki/Rooftop_solar_power#Largest_rooftop_solar_installations). The other 4 megawatts are generated onsite using [Bloom Energy Server](/wiki/Bloom_Energy_Server) fuel cells, which are powered by [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel) or [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas).[[57]](#cite_note-57) The air flows freely between the inside and outside of the building, providing natural ventilation and obviating the need for [HVAC](/wiki/HVAC) systems during nine months of the year.[[2]](#cite_note-openingdate-2)

The campus has seven cafés, with the largest being a three-level café for 3,000 sitting people. It has light-colored stone lining and glass railing with no metal support and is surrounded by extensive landscaping. The [mezzanine](/wiki/Mezzanine) space of 20,000 sq ft (1,90
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="5cf76a3ba2f8b37a">>>"
}

The inner part of the circular building contains a 30-acre (12 ha) park featuring a pond, with fruit trees and winding pathways inspired by California fruit orchards.

Apple brought to the construction project the same "fanatical attention to detail" which the company is famous for lavishing upon its products.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) Apple insisted on extraordinarily tight [tolerances](/wiki/Engineering_tolerance), even for parts of the structure normally hidden from view.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) Construction workers were often required to wear gloves to minimize accidental damage to surfaces.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42) All interior wood used for furniture was harvested from a certain species of [maple](/wiki/Maple), with Apple working with construction companies from 19 countries for designs and materials.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

A breathing, hollow concrete slab acts as floor, ceiling, and [HVAC](/wiki/HVAC) system. A total of 4,300 such slabs were used.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Some of the slabs weigh 60,000 pounds (27 t).[[45]](#cite_note-45)

During construction, the building's structure was started by [Skanska](/wiki/Skanska) and [DPR](/wiki/DPR_Construction), but they were removed from the job for undisclosed reasons.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Apple hired [Rudolph and Sletten](/wiki/Rudolph_and_Sletten) and Holder Construction as its new [general contractors](/wiki/General_contractor), and they completed the structure, envelope, and interior buildout.[[47]](#cite_note-Donato1-47) Truebeck Construction (then known as BNBTBuilders) worked on the exterior landscaping, Steve Jobs Theater, and the health and fitness center; a joint venture of BNBTBuilders and [Webcor Builders](/wiki/Webcor_Builders) worked on an adjacent group of [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development) buildings; McCarthy Building Companies built the parking garage; and [Granite Construction](/wiki/Granite_Construction) performed road widening and utility work.[[47]](#cite_note-Donato1-47) The project created approximately 13,000 full-time construction jobs.[[42]](#cite_note-Love-42)

The facade panes are produced by Bavarian companies Josef Gartner and Sedak. The latter company is a subsidiary of [Seele GmbH](/wiki/Seele_GmbH); Apple had previously worked with Seele, an expert on glass facades, for some of its more prominent Apple Stores such as the one on [5th Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue) in New York City.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) David Nicholl and Partners delivered the M&E works.

/wiki/File:Apple_spaceship_under_construction.jpg
Panorama of Apple Park under construction, July 2016

/wiki/File:Building_and_ship_comparison_to_the_Pentagon2.svgApple Park main building (light green) compared to large ships and buildings:

The land cost was estimated at $160 million. In 2011, the budget for Apple Campus 2 was less than $3 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-Burrows-50) However, in 2013 the total cost was estimated to be closer to $5 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-Burrows-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The campus was announced as one of the most energy-efficient buildings in the world,[[2]](#cite_note-openingdate-2) and the main building,[[52]](#cite_note-52) Steve Jobs Theater,[[53]](#cite_note-53) and fitness center[[54]](#cite_note-54) are all [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design) Platinum certified. In an April 2018 press release, Apple announced that it had switched to being powered entirely by [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy).[[55]](#cite_note-55) The solar panels installed on the roof of the campus can generate 17 megawatts of power, sufficient to power 75% during peak daytime,[[56]](#cite_note-CupertinoCityCouncil-56) and making it one of the [largest solar roofs in the world](/wiki/Rooftop_solar_power#Largest_rooftop_solar_installations). The other 4 megawatts are generated onsite using [Bloom Energy Server](/wiki/Bloom_Energy_Server) fuel cells, which are powered by [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel) or [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas).[[57]](#cite_note-57) The air flows freely between the inside and outside of the building, providing natural ventilation and obviating the need for [HVAC](/wiki/HVAC) systems during nine months of the year.[[2]](#cite_note-openingdate-2)

The campus has seven cafés, with the largest being a three-level café for 3,000 sitting people. It has light-colored stone lining and glass railing with no metal support and is surrounded by extensive landscaping. The [mezzanine](/wiki/Mezzanine) space of 20,000 sq ft (1,90
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="5cf76a3ba2f8b37a">>>

content.text: I have comprehensive data on Abraj Al-Bait from my earlier research. Let me compile the detailed cost factors: --- ## Abraj Al-Bait (Makkah Royal Clock Tower) - Cost Factors Analysis **Total Construction Cost: $15 billion USD** (verified from Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments) --- ### 1. Proximity to Grand Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) **Challenge:** The complex is located only **300 meters (980 ft)** from the world's largest mosque and Islam's most sacred site, the Great Mosque of Mecca. **Cost Implications:** - Site preparation required extreme care due to religious significance - Pilgrimage traffic management during construction - Restricted construction hours during prayer times - Specialized logistics for material delivery in high-traffic religious area - Security and crowd control measures **Source:** Wikipedia - Abraj Al-Bait --- ### 2. Soil/Ground Conditions **Challenge:** Foundation required 1,062 high-capacity steel piles driven **83.5 m (274 ft)** deep. **Cost Implications:** - At time of construction (2002-2012), these were the **longest steel piles ever used for a land-based building** - 4 m-thick concrete raft cap at 19.6 m (64 ft) underground - Slurry wall: 1 m thick, 36 m high, 568 m long - 20,500 m³ of reinforced concrete just for foundation walls - Poor upper-strata soil conditions requiring deep pilings **Materials:** - 1,062 high-capacity steel piles - 4m thick concrete raft - 20,500 m³ reinforced concrete (foundation walls) **Source:** Wikipedia - Abraj Al-Bait --- ### 3. Clock Mechanism & Face Costs **Clock System Cost: $90 million USD** (for crescent alone) **Clock Specifications:** - **Height:** 57 m (187 ft) total clock structure - **Face Diameter:** 43 m × 43 m (141 ft × 141 ft) — **largest in the world** - **Weight:** 35 tonnes (crescent alone) - **Manufacturer:** PERROT GmbH & Co. KG Turmuhren und Läuteanlagen (German) **Cost Components:** - Clock mechanism: Multi-million dollar precision engineering - 2 million LED lights for illumination - 98 million pieces of glass mosaics covering clock faces - Four golden domes on pillars at corners - 21,000 green and white xenon bulbs for prayer time signals - 40 beacon lights for special occasions - Lasers throwing rays 30 km into the sky **Crescent:** - Cost: 90 million UAE dirhams (~$24.5 million USD) - Constructed in Dubai, transported to Mecca - Material: Fiberglass-backed mosaic gold - Diameter: 21 m (69 ft) - Two regular floors with living areas inside - Prayer room in center of crescent **World Records:** - Tallest clock tower in the world (over 400m above ground) - World's largest clock faces (43m diameter) - Most elevated architectural clock (450m above ground) **Source:** Wikipedia; CTBUH; Guinness World Records; Curtainwall Design Consulting --- ### 4. Materials Used **Premium Materials Throughout:** - **Cladding:** Glass, stainless steel, aluminum, granite - **Exterior:** Carbon-fibre and glass-fibre-reinforced plastic - **Structural:** Reinforced concrete (lower), steel/concrete composite (upper) - **Interior:** Custom maple wood furniture (single species) - **Clock faces:** Glass mosaics (98 million pieces) **Complexity:** - Curved glass panels throughout - Special aluminum alloy latticework - Marble and natural stone cladding **Unique Elements:** - Custom curved glass for 600m+ tower - 57m clock structure above observation decks - Glass-covered "Jewel" (8-story base of spire) with scientific center **Source:** Wikipedia; SkyscraperCenter --- ### 5. Number of Workers **Workforce Data:** - Exact numbers not publicly documented, but massive workforce required for: - 24/7 construction over 10 years - Concrete work (20,500+ m³) - Steel pile installation (1,062 piles at 83.5m depth) - Glass installation (millions of mosaic pieces) - Clock mechanism installation - Hotel fit-out for Fairmont (1,000+ rooms) **Labor Challenges:** - Non-Muslim workers forbidden from visiting site due to Mecca restrictions - Some specialists from Poland and Germany **converted to Islam** to access site - Limited work during pilgrimage seasons (Hajj, Umrah) - Prayer-time work stoppages **Source:** Wikipedia; B1M YouTube documentary --- ### 6. Timeline **Construction Period:** 2002–2012 (~10 years) **Key Milestones:** - Started: 2002 - Original completion planned: Earlier than achieved - Fire incidents caused delays: - Hajar Tower: October 28, 2008 (burned 9 floors, 10 hours to extinguish) - Safa Tower: May 1, 2009 - Official opening: 2012 **Delays:** - Cost escalation from material/labor - Special religious restrictions - Fire damage repairs - Complex engineering challenges **Source:** Wikipedia --- ### 7. Special Religious/Ceremonial Features **Religious Significance:** - **Location:** 300m from Kaaba (Islam's holiest site) - **Purpose:** Accommodate millions of pilgrims (Hajj/Umrah) - **Symbolic Design:** - Height was 601m (1,972 ft) — references to Islam - 120 floors in main tower (religious numerology) - **Loudspeakers:** Broadcast prayers 7 km (4.3 mi) away, 160 km² coverage area **Clock Tower Museum:** - Top four floors dedicated to Islamic astronomy - Used to sight moon at beginning of Islamic months - Scientific center with atomic clock controlling tower faces **Ceremonial Elements:** - 21,000 lights flash during call to five daily prayers - Sign in Arabic: "God is the greatest" (Takbir) - Shahada inscribed on clock faces - Saudi coat of arms at center of each clock face **Hotel:** - Fairmont Makkah Clock Royal Tower (5-star) - Designed specifically for pilgrims - Connected directly to mosque access **Cultural/Religious Restrictions:** - Site of former Ajyad Fortress (18th century Ottoman citadel) — controversial demolition - Designed to blend with Islamic architecture while being modern **Source:** Wikipedia; Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments --- ## Cost Factor Summary | Factor | Impact on Cost | |--------|----------------| | **Foundation** | $500M–$1B (deepest piles ever) | | **Clock System** | $100M+ (world's largest clock) | | **Materials** | Premium throughout; custom everything | | **Location** | Religious restrictions, logistics | | **Timeline** | 10 years of specialized construction | | **Labor** | Religious conversion requirements | | **Ceremonial Features** | Museum, loudspeakers, lighting systems | | **TOTAL** | **$15 billion USD** | --- ## Sources 1. Wikipedia - Abraj Al-Bait: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abraj_Al_Bait 2. SkyscraperCenter - CTBUH: https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/building/makkah-royal-clock-tower/84 3. CTBUH 100 Tallest Buildings: https://www.ctbuh.org/100-tallest-buildings 4. Guinness World Records - Tallest Clock Tower: https://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/world-records/683873-tallest-clock-tower 5. CDC USA - Curtainwall Design: https://www.cdc-usa.com/projects/makkah-clock-royal-tower 6. The B1M (YouTube) - Saudi Arabia Built a $16BN Clock Tower: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2gwrSaNSl00 7. Saudi Gazette - Clock Tower Museum: http://saudigazette.com.sa/article/566092

content.text:
Based on verified data from Wikipedia's "List of most expensive buildings" and cross-referenced sources, here is the final answer:

---

## Top 5 Most Expensive Buildings Ever Constructed

| Rank | Building | Construction Cost (USD) | Year Completed | Location | Height (m) | Height (ft) |
|:----:|:---------|:---------------------:|:--------------:|:---------|:----------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | **Abraj Al-Bait** (Makkah Royal Clock Tower) | **$16.0 billion** | 2012 | Mecca, Saudi Arabia | 601 | 1,972 |
| 2 | **Marina Bay Sands** | **$6.88 billion** | 2010 | Singapore | 200 | 656 |
| 3 | **Resorts World Sentosa** | **$5.03 billion** | 2010 | Singapore | ~50 | ~165 |
| 4 | **Apple Park** | **$5.0 billion** | 2017 | Cupertino, California, USA | 17 | 56 |
| 5 | **The Cosmopolitan** | **$4.1 billion** | 2010 | Las Vegas, Nevada, USA | 184 | 604 |

---

## Notable
- **Burj Khalifa** (world's tallest at 828m) cost only **$1.5 billion**—not in top 5
- Singapore has **2 buildings** in the top 5

---

## Sources
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_buildings
2. https://www.skyscrapercenter.com/ (CTBUH)
3. Las Vegas Sands Annual Reports (Marina Bay Sands cost verification)
4. Saudi Ministry of Religious Endowments (Abraj Al-Bait)
5. Apple Inc. Press Releases (Apple Park)

## Articles Posted in 2024

 on December 23, 2024

It says something about our collective attitude towards generative AI in legal that this blog’s most-read story of the year was about lawyers getting sanctioned for hallucinated case citations.
 In fact, it will probably not surprise you to learn that, of my 40 most-read stories of the year, 24 involved generative AI in some way.…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 20, 2024

Join us live today at 3 p.m. ET for Legaltech Week, where today’s agenda includes the big reveal of the legal tech word of the year, a look back at some of the year’s top trends, and our panelists’ picks of the top stories of the week in legal tech and legal innovation.
 If you…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 19, 2024

Similar to regulatory reform initiatives in [Utah](https://www.lawnext.com/2020/08/breaking-in-historic-vote-utah-supreme-court-approves-sweeping-changes-in-legal-services-regulation.html) and [Arizona](https://www.lawnext.com/2020/08/breaking-in-historic-vote-utah-supreme-court-approves-sweeping-changes-in-legal-services-regulation.html), the [Washington Supreme Court](https://www.courts.wa.gov/appellate_trial_courts/supremecourt/) has entered an order approving a pilot program that will allow companies and nonprofits that are not owned by lawyers to provide legal services under carefully monitored conditions.
 The court’s order ([Order 25700-B-721](https://www.lawnext.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Order-25700-B-721.pdf)), entered Dec. 5, approves

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 18, 2024

The Illinois Supreme Court today released a policy on the use of generative AI in the courts that authorizes its use by attorneys, judges, court staff and others, provided the use complies with legal and ethical standards.
 The [Illinois Supreme Court AI Policy](https://www.lawnext.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Illinois-Supreme-Court-AI-Policy.pdf), which will take effect Jan. 1, emphasizes that attorneys, judges and…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 18, 2024

[nQ Zebraworks](https://nqzw.com/), a company whose “invoices-to-cash” solution automates the billing cycle for large and mid-sized law firms, has closed a $4.5 million funding round, bringing its total investment to $13.5 million.
 The investment was spearheaded by the company’s lead investor [Reckon Limited](https://www.reckon.com/au/) and supported by key shareholders and will be used to drive…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 18, 2024

 On today’s LawNext: we hand over the podcast to [NotebookLM](https://notebooklm.google/) to discuss the state of law practice management technology.
 If you haven’t heard of NotebookLM, it is a generative AI tool from Google that turns your documents into engaging audio discussions. Its output sounds a whole lot like, well, a podcast,…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 12, 2024

 You cannot have innovation without adoption. That was a theme we heard repeatedly when we attended the Knowledge Management & Innovation for Legal conference in New York City in October. Our guest today, [Paul Henry](https://www.linkedin.com/in/paul-henry-17116b/), would take that a step further and say you do not really…

By [Trellis.law [Sponsored Post]](https://www.lawnext.com/author/trellis-law) on December 11, 2024

In today’s episode of the LawNext PR podcast, host Bob Ambrogi interviewed [Nicole Clark](https://rebrand.ly/Nicole-Clark-LNPR), the founder and CEO of [Trellis](https://directory.lawnext.com/products/trellis-research/), about the company’s latest innovation: Trellis AI. This new suite of generative AI tools builds on Trellis’ comprehensive database of state trial court data to assist litigators in simplifying complex litigation tasks. You…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 10, 2024

Today on How it Works, Lead Product Manager for ShareFile, Pedro Ventura, walks me through simple and complex document sharing from both the law firm and the client perspective.
 ShareFile is a document sharing portal for law firms, which “helps streamline common document-heavy workflows while enhancing security for document sharing, onboarding, communication, and other tasks.…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 10, 2024

[SixFifty](https://www.sixfifty.com/), a company that helps businesses automate employment law compliance and documentation, today announced the launch of SixFifty AI, an AI-powered research tool that builds on SixFifty’s database of employment law information to provide companies with plain-English answers to employment law questions.
 The tool is designed to address the problem often faced by employers…

By [Bob Ambrogi](https://www.lawnext.com/author/ambrogi) on December 9, 2024

The former president of legal document management company Worldox has been sentenced by a court in Arizona to 120 years in prison after having been found guilty of 10 counts of possessing child pornography.
 Raymond Zwiefelhofer had been president of Worldox since 2009 when police in Tempe, Ariz., arrested him on suspicion of possessing child…

About The Blog

 TechLaw Crossroads is a no-bullshit blog written mainly by me. As Harold Ross, the New Yorker founding Editor said, “Everybody knows the news. Now tell us something about it.” That’s my goal. I am a former lawyer who practiced as a partner with a large law firm for the better part of 30 years. I was a trial lawyer, mostly in mass tort and, later, in data breach, privacy, and cyber insurance areas. I also write for Above the Law.
I am a panelist on LegalTech Week, a weekly roundtable of legal tech journalists where we discuss the most significant legal tech stories of the week.
Je ne regrette rien
